reason: Chronologic age within an already very elderly cohort (≥80). Likely adds limited incremental signal beyond baseline creatinine normalization and periop physiology, so low sparse-selection priority.
Sex is a proxy for muscle mass/creatinine generation and AKI susceptibility. Can add modest unique signal even with creatinine ratio, but is still indirect.

Race category is a coarse demographic descriptor. In a sparse model it is usually weak/unstable and largely redundant with comorbidity and physiologic variables; also risks encoding non-causal structure.

Race category may correlate with baseline CKD risk and perioperative outcomes. However, for creatinine ratio it is indirect and often dominated by direct renal/hemodynamic features, so only a weak candidate.
Race category with typically smaller subgroup sizes. Likely low incremental predictive value and unstable estimates in sparse linear selection.
Race category that is often rare in single-center cardiac surgery cohorts. Low expected unique signal and high risk of sparsity/instability for selection.
Race category that is typically very low prevalence. Unlikely to survive sparse screening due to limited unique predictive value and instability.
Catch-all race category with heterogeneous meaning. Generally weak and non-specific for AKI prediction compared with direct physiologic/renal markers.
Ethnicity is a broad demographic variable. Usually provides little unique predictive information for postoperative creatinine trajectory once comorbidities and ICU course are available.
Height reflects body size but is only indirectly related to renal injury risk. Likely redundant with weight/BSA-related effects and low priority for sparse inclusion.

Weight captures body size and may proxy volume of distribution, hemodilution, and dosing/obesity-related AKI risk. Still indirect and often dominated by direct hemodynamics/fluid balance features, so modest priority.
Family history relates to long-term cardiovascular risk rather than acute postoperative renal hypoperfusion/AKI. Very unlikely to add unique near-term predictive value for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Diabetes is a strong preoperative risk factor for CKD/AKI and impaired renal reserve. It provides meaningful baseline susceptibility information that can complement postoperative physiologic signals in a sparse model.

Indicator of diabetes control (likely treatment/controlled status) is a noisier proxy than diabetes itself. Adds limited incremental value beyond the diabetes flag and periop glucose/insulin-related features (if present), so lower priority.

Dyslipidemia reflects chronic atherosclerotic risk but is not a direct driver of acute postoperative creatinine rise. Typically redundant with broader comorbidity burden and low value for sparse selection.

Hypertension is associated with chronic kidney disease and reduced renal autoregulation, increasing AKI susceptibility. Useful baseline risk information, though less specific than diabetes and often correlated with other comorbidities.

History of infective endocarditis implies prior severe infection and possible embolic/immune renal injury. For Hour-60 creatinine ratio it is an indirect, low-prevalence signal and likely dominated by perioperative hemodynamics/labs in a sparse model.

Chronic pulmonary disease reflects baseline frailty and risk of hypoxemia/ventilator dependence. It is a nonspecific severity marker and usually adds limited unique renal predictive value once acute ICU physiology is included.

Home oxygen use is a proxy for advanced lung disease and limited physiologic reserve. It may correlate with postoperative complications but is indirect for AKI and likely redundant with other comorbidity/acuity features.

Bronchodilator therapy indicates obstructive airway disease treatment and is heavily influenced by prescribing patterns. It is a weak, clinician-behavior proxy with minimal direct linkage to postoperative creatinine trajectory.

Sleep apnea is a chronic condition associated with cardiometabolic risk but not a near-term driver of postoperative AKI. In sparse selection it is typically dominated by direct renal/perfusion signals and other comorbidities.

Pneumonia indicates active infection/inflammation and potential hypoxemia/sepsis physiology that can precipitate AKI. It can add some unique risk information, but remains indirect compared with hemodynamics, lactate, and renal labs.

IV drug use is a social/behavioral history that may relate to endocarditis or chronic infections. It is low-prevalence and indirect for immediate postoperative renal dysfunction, so unlikely to survive sparse screening.
Depression is a baseline psychosocial comorbidity. It has little mechanistic connection to acute postoperative kidney injury and is unlikely to add unique predictive value in a sparse linear model.

Alcohol use can reflect liver disease, malnutrition, and perioperative instability risk. As coded here it is nonspecific and likely redundant with explicit liver disease and acute physiologic variables.

Liver disease captures impaired perfusion reserve, inflammation, and hepatorenal physiology that can increase AKI susceptibility. It is still a baseline comorbidity (not a direct renal marker) but may contribute unique risk beyond other items in this batch.

Immunosuppression increases infection risk and may reflect complex chronic disease burden. For predicting Hour-60 creatinine ratio it is indirect and often mediated through postoperative complications captured by ICU features.

Prior mediastinal radiation mainly signals surgical complexity and cardiopulmonary sequelae. It is a weak, indirect predictor of AKI and likely dominated by intra/postoperative hemodynamics and renal labs.

Cancer reflects chronic illness burden and potential nephrotoxic exposures. As a broad label it is heterogeneous and typically provides limited unique information for acute postoperative creatinine changes.

Peripheral artery disease is a marker of systemic atherosclerosis and renal vascular disease, increasing susceptibility to renal hypoperfusion/contrast-related injury. Within this comorbidity-only batch it is one of the stronger, more kidney-proximal baseline predictors likely to survive sparse selection.

Thoracic aortic disease implies complex surgery and higher risk of embolic/low-flow states that can drive AKI. It can add predictive value, though much of its effect may be captured by postoperative hemodynamics and procedure-related variables elsewhere.
Syncope is a nonspecific symptom history with many etiologies. It has little direct relationship to postoperative renal injury and is unlikely to contribute unique signal in a sparse model.

History of unresponsive state reflects neurologic comorbidity/frailty rather than renal physiology. Likely adds little unique signal for Hour-60 creatinine ratio beyond overall illness severity proxies.

Cerebrovascular disease is a marker of systemic atherosclerosis and frailty that can correlate with perioperative risk. It is indirect for AKI and often redundant with other comorbidity/vascular-burden variables in sparse selection.

Baseline hemoglobin captures anemia/hemodilution reserve and likelihood of transfusion/oxygen delivery issues that can contribute to renal hypoxia. Moderately useful but indirect and often mediated by intra/post-op hemodynamics and bleeding/transfusion features (if present elsewhere).

Pre-op albumin reflects nutrition/inflammation and oncotic pressure, associated with AKI risk and fluid handling. Useful as a baseline vulnerability marker but still indirect and may be redundant with other frailty/liver-disease severity measures.

Baseline bilirubin is a liver dysfunction/cholestasis marker and can indicate systemic illness. It is an indirect predictor of AKI and is typically dominated by more kidney-proximal risk markers (e.g., baseline renal function, periop perfusion).

HbA1c captures chronic glycemic exposure/diabetes severity, which increases susceptibility to postoperative AKI. It can add baseline risk information, but it is distal to the 60-hour creatinine trajectory and may be redundant with explicit diabetes/CKD variables.

HIT antibodies indicate a specific anticoagulation complication risk rather than a common driver of postoperative creatinine rise. Low prevalence and weak direct linkage to AKI make it unlikely to survive sparse feature selection.

MELD score (including creatinine component) summarizes baseline hepatorenal dysfunction and overall physiologic reserve. Strong baseline vulnerability signal for postoperative creatinine rise, though partially overlapping with baseline creatinine if that is separately available.

BNP reflects cardiac filling pressures/heart failure severity and predicts postoperative low-output states and venous congestion, both key AKI mechanisms after cardiac surgery. Provides relatively unique cardiorenal risk information beyond generic comorbidity flags.

5-meter walk time is a frailty/functional status measure associated with postoperative complications. It is a nonspecific risk proxy and usually weaker than physiologic/hemodynamic predictors for creatinine ratio in a sparse linear model.

History of prior cardiovascular intervention indicates advanced coronary/vascular disease burden. It is indirect for AKI and likely redundant with more specific prior CABG/PCI variables in this same batch.

Prior CABG suggests complex coronary disease and possible redo-sternotomy risk. It may correlate with operative complexity but is an indirect AKI predictor and often dominated by intra/post-op perfusion and shock markers.

Prior cardiac surgery is a proxy for redo status and surgical complexity. It can relate to AKI risk via longer bypass/bleeding but is indirect and typically redundant with direct perioperative course variables.

Prior PCI reflects coronary disease burden and prior contrast exposure history. For predicting a 60-hour postoperative creatinine ratio, it is a weak/indirect baseline marker and overlaps with other CAD history features.

PCI during the same episode implies recent contrast load and acute coronary instability, which can predispose to perioperative AKI. More temporally relevant than remote PCI, so it may add unique baseline risk signal if contrast timing/dose are not otherwise captured.
Presence of a stent is mainly a marker of CAD history and antiplatelet management complexity. It is indirect for AKI and largely redundant with the broader PCI history variables.

Recency of prior PCI/CABG reflects baseline coronary disease burden and procedural complexity. It is an indirect AKI risk marker and likely dominated by more proximal hemodynamic/renal signals, so low sparse-selection priority.

History/number of prior cardiac interventions is a proxy for comorbidity and surgical complexity. Useful context but indirect for Hour-60 creatinine ratio and likely redundant with other severity/operative-course features.

Recent ACE inhibitor exposure can predispose to perioperative hypotension and reduced renal autoregulation. It can add some unique baseline susceptibility signal, but remains indirect and often outweighed by post-op perfusion/vasopressor variables.

Recent P2Y12 inhibitor use mainly reflects antiplatelet management and bleeding risk. Any association with AKI is largely mediated through transfusion/bleeding pathways and is a weak, indirect predictor for sparse selection.

Amiodarone use is a marker of atrial/ventricular arrhythmia history and cardiac disease severity. It has limited direct renal relevance and is likely redundant with hemodynamic instability markers captured postoperatively.

Pre-op anticoagulant use reflects AF/VTE/mechanical valve and may correlate with bleeding/transfusion risk. It is an indirect clinician-management proxy and typically weaker than direct post-op shock/bleeding/renal signals.
Aspirin use is common and mostly reflects baseline CAD prevention rather than renal physiology. It is unlikely to add unique predictive value for creatinine ratio in a sparse model.

Beta-blocker use indicates CAD/HF/arrhythmia management and can influence perioperative heart rate and hypotension risk. Still an indirect baseline-treatment marker and usually dominated by actual post-op hemodynamics/vasopressor needs.

Longer-term beta-blocker therapy is largely redundant with the simpler pre_op_beta_blockers indicator. It adds little incremental information for AKI prediction beyond baseline comorbidity/treatment status.

CCB therapy duration is a baseline medication marker with weak mechanistic linkage to postoperative AKI. Likely redundant with broader comorbidity/severity features and low value for sparse selection.

GPIIb/IIIa inhibitor use is uncommon and mainly reflects acute coronary syndrome/PCI management. Any renal signal is indirect (bleeding/transfusion/acuity) and unlikely to be a stable sparse-model keeper.

Pre-op inotrope requirement is a strong marker of low cardiac output/cardiogenic shock physiology and poor renal perfusion reserve. This baseline severity signal can add unique predictive value for subsequent AKI beyond routine medication history.

Statin/lipid-lowering therapy is prevalent and primarily reflects chronic cardiovascular risk management. It has minimal direct predictive value for near-term postoperative creatinine changes and is low priority for sparsity.
Chronic nitrate therapy indicates angina burden but does not directly encode renal vulnerability. It is an indirect comorbidity proxy and unlikely to survive sparse feature selection.

IV nitrates suggest active ischemia/heart failure management and potential hemodynamic lability pre-op. This can capture acute severity not fully reflected by chronic meds, but remains indirect compared with post-op perfusion markers.

Other chronic antianginal therapy is a broad, heterogeneous medication bucket and largely redundant with CAD severity indicators. Low specificity and low incremental value for predicting Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Pre-op steroid exposure may reflect chronic inflammatory disease and can affect glucose/infection risk, indirectly influencing AKI risk. It is a weak, nonspecific proxy and likely dominated by direct hemodynamic/renal trajectory features in a sparse model.

Recent direct thrombin inhibitor use mainly signals anticoagulation management and bleeding risk. Any renal signal is indirect (e.g., periop bleeding/transfusion), so it is unlikely to add unique predictive value for creatinine_ratio.

Warfarin use reflects comorbidity burden (AF/VTE/mechanical valve) and periop anticoagulation complexity. It is an indirect clinician-behavior/comorbidity proxy and typically redundant with stronger periop physiology signals.

Factor Xa inhibitor use indicates anticoagulation status and potential bleeding/transfusion risk. It has limited direct mechanistic linkage to postoperative creatinine rise and is unlikely to survive sparse selection.
Thrombin inhibitor use within 5 days is another anticoagulation-management marker. It is largely redundant with other anticoagulant indicators and provides only indirect AKI risk information.
Statin use is a marker of baseline cardiovascular disease and chronic medication adherence. Any association with AKI is modest and confounded, so it is a low-priority sparse-model feature.

Pre-op cardiac cath can imply recent contrast exposure and higher coronary disease burden, both linked to AKI susceptibility. It may add some unique pre-op risk signal, though it remains indirect versus post-op hemodynamics/labs.

Known coronary anatomy disease is a broad comorbidity indicator. It is likely redundant with more granular CAD severity measures (e.g., number of diseased vessels) and with periop instability features.

Extent of CAD captures baseline cardiovascular reserve and likelihood of complex surgery/low-output states that predispose to AKI. It is more informative than a binary CAD flag but still indirect compared with direct renal/ICU trajectory variables.
Aortic valve disease (often AS) relates to baseline hemodynamics and risk of periop low cardiac output/hypoperfusion. It can contribute unique context for AKI risk but is not a direct renal marker.

Mitral valve disease is associated with heart failure/pulmonary hypertension and periop complexity. Its renal predictive value is indirect and often captured better by post-op hemodynamics and fluid balance.

Tricuspid disease can indicate right-sided failure/venous congestion, which is mechanistically linked to renal dysfunction. This may provide some unique baseline risk signal, though still less direct than ICU renal/hemodynamic features.
Pulmonic valve disease is relatively uncommon and usually not a primary driver of postoperative AKI in this setting. It is unlikely to add stable, unique predictive value in a sparse model.

Number of prior cardiac surgeries reflects surgical complexity, adhesions, and higher risk of prolonged bypass/bleeding, which can contribute to AKI. It is a reasonable baseline risk feature but remains an indirect proxy.

Status likely encodes operative urgency/elective vs emergent, a general severity and care-pathway marker. It can correlate with AKI risk but is nonspecific and often redundant with physiologic instability variables.

Case cancellation is primarily an administrative/workflow outcome and may reflect logistics rather than patient physiology. It is unlikely to provide meaningful, generalizable signal for creatinine_ratio prediction.

Conversion (e.g., minimally invasive/robotic to open) is a marker of intraop complexity/complications that can increase AKI risk via longer ischemic time, bleeding, and hemodynamic instability. Useful as a relatively unique procedural-risk signal, but still indirect compared with physiologic renal-perfusion markers.

Robotic approach mainly reflects case selection and generally less invasive surgery, which may correlate with lower AKI risk. In a sparse model it is a weak, indirect proxy and often redundant with procedure type and conversion status.

CABG indicates a specific surgical category associated with CPB exposure, atheroembolism risk, and periop hemodynamic swings that can affect renal function. Moderately useful for baseline procedural risk stratification but indirect and often shared with other procedure indicators.

Valve surgery is associated with different CPB times, hemodynamics, and transfusion profiles that influence AKI risk. Provides moderate unique procedural context, but still a coarse proxy compared with direct perfusion/renal markers.

Additional cardiac procedures suggest greater operative complexity and longer bypass/cross-clamp exposure, increasing AKI risk. Potentially additive as a complexity flag, but heterogeneous and less specific than direct intraop physiologic insults.

AF ablation/maze procedures add operative time and inflammation but usually contribute modest incremental renal risk relative to major hemodynamic/bleeding factors. Likely redundant with broader “other cardiac procedure/complexity” signals in sparse selection.

Aortic procedures (especially arch/ascending) strongly increase AKI risk via longer CPB, hypothermia/circulatory arrest, embolic burden, and major hemodynamic stress. This is a high-yield, relatively unique procedural-risk indicator likely to survive sparse screening.

Non-cardiac procedures performed around the operation mainly reflect complexity and comorbidity burden rather than a direct renal insult. Weak and heterogeneous proxy, typically dominated by more specific operative and physiologic variables.

Appropriate prophylactic antibiotic choice is a quality/process metric and only weakly related to short-term creatinine rise unless nephrotoxic agents are specifically captured. Mostly clinician-behavior/protocol adherence with low unique predictive value for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Timing of prophylactic antibiotics is a periop process measure with minimal direct linkage to early AKI trajectory. In sparse modeling it is largely noise/behavioral proxy rather than renal physiology.

Ordering discontinuation reflects stewardship/workflow and is not a direct determinant of early postoperative creatinine changes. Low priority for sparse selection due to weak mechanistic connection and high proxy content.

An extra intraop antibiotic dose can indicate prolonged surgery or high blood loss, indirectly associated with AKI risk. Some signal as a duration/complexity proxy, but redundant with more direct complexity markers (e.g., conversion, aortic procedure, low Hgb).

Lowest core temperature captures hypothermia strategy and may proxy for complex CPB (e.g., deep hypothermia/circulatory arrest) which is associated with AKI. Moderately useful, but partly redundant with aortic procedure and other complexity indicators.

Intraop nadir hemoglobin reflects bleeding/hemodilution and oxygen delivery, both tightly linked to renal hypoxia and subsequent AKI. A strong, relatively direct physiologic insult marker that often adds unique value beyond procedure labels.

Intraoperative hyperglycemia is a nonspecific stress response and may correlate with illness severity and diabetes rather than directly driving early AKI. Likely weaker and more redundant than hemodynamic/oxygen-delivery variables for sparse selection.

Use of coronary perfusion/CPB-related technique indicates operative strategy and complexity but is not itself a direct renal biomarker. Provides limited incremental value and is often captured better by procedure type, temperature, and anemia/bleeding signals.

Ascending aortic arterial cannulation is the standard CPB access and mainly reflects operative approach. It has at most weak incremental AKI signal beyond bypass time/complexity, so low sparse-selection priority.

Femoral arterial cannulation often indicates peripheral cannulation, redo/urgent cases, or aortic pathology with potentially higher embolic/malperfusion risk. It may add some unique risk-context beyond CPB time, but remains an indirect proxy.

Axillary cannulation is commonly used for aortic surgery/dissection and complex arch work, correlating with higher operative complexity and potential hypoperfusion. Provides some incremental context but is still largely a procedure-proxy rather than a direct renal signal.

Innominate cannulation similarly suggests arch/aortic procedures and altered perfusion strategies. Moderately informative as a complexity/perfusion-pattern marker, but likely redundant with circulatory arrest/DHCA time.
Other arterial cannulation sites are heterogeneous and mainly encode unusual operative circumstances. The heterogeneity and overlap with other complexity markers limit sparse-model value.

Femoral venous cannulation often reflects minimally invasive/peripheral CPB setup or access limitations. It is an indirect procedural marker with limited unique predictive value for creatinine trajectory once CPB time/instability are accounted for.

Jugular venous cannulation is uncommon and typically reflects special access/ECMO-like configurations or complex cases. It is a weak, indirect proxy and likely too sparse/heterogeneous to survive feature selection.
Right atrial venous cannulation is standard for many CPB cases and conveys little additional renal-risk information. Largely redundant with overall CPB exposure and operative type.

Left atrial cannulation is procedure-specific (e.g., certain valve/left-sided operations) and mainly reflects surgical technique. Any AKI association is indirect and likely dominated by CPB time and circulatory arrest variables.
Pulmonary venous/arterial cannulation is rare and highly procedure-specific. Low prevalence and heterogeneity make it unlikely to add stable, unique predictive signal in a sparse linear model.
Bicaval cannulation indicates certain intracardiac procedures and may correlate with complexity. Still an indirect marker and typically redundant with bypass time and arrest-related features.
Other venous cannulation sites are heterogeneous and primarily encode unusual operative setups. This is unlikely to provide consistent, interpretable incremental AKI prediction.

Total CPB duration captures ischemia-reperfusion, inflammation, hemolysis, and nonpulsatile flow exposure—strong drivers of post-op AKI. It is a high-yield, nonredundant operative summary likely to survive sparse selection.
Use of circulatory arrest marks major aortic/arch complexity and periods of global hypoperfusion risk. Adds distinct information beyond CPB time, though partially overlaps with DHCA time.

Duration of DHCA directly quantifies the magnitude of profound hypoperfusion/ischemic exposure, a strong mechanistic precursor to AKI. This is the most informative version of the arrest signal and should dominate over the binary circulatory_arrest flag.

Cerebral perfusion use reflects specific arch protection strategies and indirectly indicates arrest/arch complexity. It may add some nuance but is largely redundant with circulatory arrest and DHCA time for renal-risk prediction.

Duration of selective cerebral perfusion is mainly a marker of complex arch surgery and prolonged bypass exposure. It may correlate with AKI risk but is indirect and largely redundant with more general ischemic-time variables (e.g., cross-clamp/circulatory arrest).

Minutes of circulatory arrest reflect profound systemic hypoperfusion/ischemia during surgery, a strong upstream driver of postoperative AKI. Adds relatively unique signal about extreme low-flow exposure beyond routine clamp time.
Binary indicator of aortic occlusion is a coarse proxy for operative technique/complexity. Likely dominated by continuous duration measures (cross-clamp/arrest time) for sparse prediction.

Cross-clamp duration captures myocardial ischemic time and correlates with CPB duration, inflammation, and low-output states that predispose to AKI. Strong, commonly predictive intraop exposure variable with good signal-to-noise for sparse models.

Use of cerebral oximetry is primarily a monitoring/clinician-practice indicator rather than a physiologic biomarker. In sparse selection it is a weak proxy and likely confounded by case complexity without adding direct renal signal.

Diffuse aortic calcification reflects systemic atherosclerosis and surgical complexity, which can associate with renal vulnerability. However it is indirect and often redundant with comorbidity burden and operative time variables.
Whether/extent the ascending aorta/arch was assessed is largely a documentation/monitoring behavior feature. It provides little direct physiologic information for predicting Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Plaque assessment is mainly a procedural/assessment indicator and a proxy for surgeon concern. It is unlikely to add unique predictive value beyond calcification/complexity and ischemic-time measures.

Change in surgical plan due to aortic condition signals higher complexity and potentially longer bypass/embolization risk. Still an indirect proxy and likely captured better by arrest/clamp time and transfusion variables.
Refusal of blood products reflects patient preference/consent rather than physiology. It is not a reliable predictor of AKI trajectory and is unlikely to survive sparse screening.

Any intraoperative blood product exposure is a marker of bleeding, hemodilution, and inflammatory load associated with AKI risk. Useful but somewhat redundant with specific component volumes (RBC/FFP/platelets/cryo), which carry more granular signal.

RBC transfusion volume captures bleeding severity/hemodilution and is consistently associated with postoperative AKI risk. Likely to add strong, relatively unique predictive value compared with the generic 'blood products received' indicator.

FFP transfusion reflects coagulopathy/bleeding and large-volume resuscitation, which can contribute to renal congestion and AKI risk. Predictive but typically less directly informative than RBC volume and may be collinear with overall transfusion burden.
Platelet transfusion is a proxy for bleeding/coagulopathy and complex surgery. Often redundant with RBC/FFP and may be driven by practice patterns, lowering sparse-model priority.

Cryoprecipitate use indicates severe bleeding or hypofibrinogenemia and high-acuity cases. It is relatively infrequent and tends to be redundant with other transfusion measures, limiting unique predictive value.

Intraoperative clotting factor administration (e.g., PCC/factor concentrates) signals significant coagulopathy/bleeding and high-risk surgery. It is an indirect severity/treatment proxy and likely overlaps with transfusion volumes and operative times.

Intraoperative antifibrinolytic use (aminocaproic acid) mainly reflects bleeding-risk management and institutional practice. It has weak, indirect linkage to AKI and is likely dominated by hemodynamics/transfusion/renal markers not in this batch.

Intraoperative tranexamic acid use is largely a protocol/bleeding-risk indicator rather than a renal physiology signal. Any association with creatinine rise is indirect and likely redundant with stronger severity/bleeding variables.

TEE performed is a procedural/monitoring indicator and correlates with case complexity and clinician behavior. It provides little unique physiologic information for predicting Hour-60 creatinine ratio in a sparse model.

Residual aortic regurgitation post-repair can worsen forward flow and contribute to low-output physiology that predisposes to renal hypoperfusion. Predictive value is plausible but indirect and likely partly captured by EF and gradients.

Residual mitral regurgitation can drive pulmonary congestion/low effective cardiac output and renal venous congestion. Useful but indirect for AKI risk and somewhat redundant with EF and other valve-performance metrics.

Residual tricuspid regurgitation is a strong marker of right-sided failure and elevated venous pressures, which can directly worsen renal congestion and AKI risk. Compared with left-sided insufficiency, it often adds more unique renal-relevant signal in older post-cardiac surgery patients.

Indicator of valve surgery reflects procedure type/complexity and associated bypass time/physiologic stress. It is a coarse proxy and likely redundant with more specific post-repair functional measures (EF, gradients, leaks).

Atrial fibrillation procedure (e.g., MAZE/ablation) is mainly a marker of operative complexity and baseline atrial disease. Any renal impact is indirect and likely dominated by hemodynamics and postoperative cardiac function variables.
General anesthesia is near-universal in cardiac surgery and offers minimal discrimination. It is unlikely to contribute unique predictive value for creatinine_ratio in a sparse model.

PCC use signals significant coagulopathy/bleeding and high-acuity intraoperative course, which can correlate with hypotension/transfusion and subsequent AKI. It’s still an indirect proxy, but more informative than routine antifibrinolytics.

Post-repair aortic mean gradient reflects residual outflow obstruction and potential for reduced systemic perfusion. Moderately useful but indirect for renal outcomes and partially redundant with EF and overall valve performance.

Aortic paravalvular leak indicates suboptimal valve seating with potential hemodynamic compromise and hemolysis-related stress. Predictive signal for AKI is plausible but not as direct as right-sided congestion markers.

Mitral mean gradient reflects residual stenosis and left atrial pressure burden, which may affect congestion. Compared with MR/TR and EF, it is a weaker and more redundant predictor of renal dysfunction.

Mitral paravalvular leak is a marker of repair quality and potential hemodynamic inefficiency. Its renal relevance is indirect and likely dominated by broader cardiac function measures (EF, degree of regurgitation).

Combined mitral/tricuspid leak suggests more global valvular dysfunction and higher likelihood of congestion/low-output state. It can add signal beyond isolated left-sided metrics, but overlaps with explicit TR and MR variables.

Post-procedure EF summarizes cardiac pump function and is a key determinant of renal perfusion and need for vasoactive support. It is a strong, relatively non-redundant physiologic predictor within this batch for subsequent creatinine rise.

Indicates a planned PCI (care pathway/planning variable) rather than a physiologic renal insult. Likely adds little unique signal for Hour-60 creatinine ratio beyond overall cardiac disease severity and procedural complexity proxies.

Use of an intra-aortic balloon pump reflects perioperative low cardiac output/cardiogenic shock risk and need for mechanical circulatory support, which strongly relates to renal hypoperfusion and AKI. It is a relatively specific severity marker that can add unique predictive value in a sparse model.

Baseline cath-lab assist suggests preop hemodynamic support needs, a proxy for cardiac severity. Predictive for AKI risk but largely overlaps with stronger support indicators (e.g., IABP/ECMO) and downstream hemodynamic/vasopressor features not shown here.

ECMO indicates profound cardiopulmonary failure with high risk of renal hypoperfusion, hemolysis/inflammation, and frequent AKI. This is a top-tier, non-redundant severity/support signal likely to survive sparse selection.

Blood product transfusion volume reflects bleeding/hemodilution and inflammatory load and is associated with AKI after cardiac surgery. Useful but partly redundant with postoperative anemia measures (hemoglobin/hematocrit) and overall surgical complexity.

Extubation in the OR is a marker of uncomplicated course and better cardiopulmonary reserve. It is an indirect proxy for lower illness severity and may add some signal but is not kidney-specific and can be redundant with other acuity indicators.

Re-intubation suggests respiratory failure or hemodynamic instability, often accompanying shock, infection, or fluid overload that can worsen renal function. Moderately informative but indirect and partly a clinician-behavior/outcome proxy rather than a direct renal signal.

ICU visit is essentially universal post-cardiac surgery and mainly reflects workflow/triage rather than physiology. It is unlikely to provide discriminative or unique predictive value for creatinine ratio.

Postoperative hemoglobin captures anemia/hemodilution and bleeding/transfusion burden, which can contribute to renal hypoxia and AKI risk. Moderately useful but redundant with hematocrit and transfusion variables, so not top priority in a sparse model.
Postoperative hematocrit is another anemia/hemodilution measure closely collinear with hemoglobin. Likely redundant with postop_hemoglobin, so lower priority for sparse selection.

Postoperative intubation status/duration is a general severity marker and may correlate with shock, sedation, and fluid balance. It is indirect for renal injury and likely overlaps with re-intubation and mechanical support variables.

History of prior MI reflects baseline coronary disease and potential reduced cardiac reserve. It may modestly increase AKI risk via low-output propensity but is distal and often dominated by perioperative support/hemodynamic features.

Timing of prior MI is a nuanced cardiac history detail with weak direct linkage to postoperative renal trajectory. Any effect is likely small and mediated through current hemodynamics/support, making it low-yield for sparse selection.

Symptom timing at admission is a presentation/history variable and mainly a proxy for acuity of coronary syndrome. It is unlikely to add unique predictive value for Hour-60 creatinine ratio beyond stronger perioperative physiologic/support signals.

Symptom timing at surgery similarly reflects preop clinical course rather than renal physiology. Likely redundant with overall case urgency/complexity and weak for predicting postoperative creatinine ratio.

Angina class measures baseline ischemic symptom burden and functional limitation. It may weakly relate to perioperative risk but is indirect for AKI and typically overshadowed by immediate postoperative support and hemodynamic variables.

NYHA functional class reflects baseline heart failure symptom burden and overall reserve. It is an indirect, pre-op severity marker and is likely dominated by more proximal hemodynamic/renal signals for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Cardiogenic shock indicates profound low cardiac output and renal hypoperfusion risk. It is a strong upstream driver of AKI and can add unique predictive value beyond generic comorbidity flags in a sparse model.

Resuscitation suggests perioperative instability (hypotension/bleeding) and high catecholamine/volume exposure, which can precede AKI. However it is partly a clinician-action proxy and may be redundant with direct hemodynamic/vasopressor features if those exist.

A broad arrhythmia history/flag captures baseline rhythm issues and potential hemodynamic lability. It is nonspecific and likely redundant with the more specific arrhythmia subtype indicators in this batch.

Recent VT/VF implies severe electrical instability and potential low-flow episodes that can contribute to renal ischemia. Predictive value is plausible but indirect and often mediated through shock/pressors, so it is not top-tier for sparse selection.

Sick sinus syndrome mainly reflects conduction system disease and pacing need rather than renal perfusion. It is weakly linked to AKI and unlikely to survive sparse selection versus stronger hemodynamic predictors.

Recent atrial flutter can affect cardiac output and anticoagulation/bleeding risk but is usually less hemodynamically catastrophic than shock states. It is a modest, indirect predictor and likely redundant with AF/overall arrhythmia burden.

Second-degree AV block is primarily a conduction abnormality and often managed with pacing without major systemic hypoperfusion. It has limited unique signal for predicting creatinine rise compared with shock/heart failure variables.

Third-degree AV block can cause bradycardia and low output if not paced, which could affect renal perfusion. In practice it is frequently stabilized with pacing, making it an indirect and often redundant predictor.

A paced rhythm is largely a management state and proxy for conduction disease rather than a direct renal risk factor. It is unlikely to add unique predictive value for Hour-60 creatinine ratio in a sparse model.

Heart failure indicates chronic low forward flow/venous congestion and reduced renal reserve, both strong predispositions to postoperative AKI. It is a high-yield baseline risk factor likely to remain informative even with other covariates.

Recent atrial fibrillation is common and can worsen hemodynamics and increase perioperative complication risk. It is moderately predictive but partly overlaps with heart failure and general arrhythmia indicators, limiting uniqueness.

Preoperative pain score reflects symptom reporting and analgesic needs rather than renal physiology. It is largely noise/proxy and unlikely to contribute meaningfully to sparse prediction of creatinine ratio.

Cell saver volume is a proxy for surgical blood loss/complexity and transfusion exposure, which can correlate with AKI risk. It is indirect and often dominated by direct hypotension, lactate, transfusion, or vasopressor variables.

Heparin dose before bypass mainly reflects protocolized anticoagulation and patient size rather than renal injury mechanisms. It is unlikely to add unique predictive signal for postoperative creatinine changes.

Protamine dose reflects heparin reversal and can be associated with hemodynamic reactions, but it is mostly procedural/protocol-driven. Any renal signal is indirect and likely redundant with direct measures of instability (pressors/hypotension).

Intraoperative viscoelastic testing (e.g., TEG/ROTEM) reflects bleeding/coagulopathy and transfusion propensity. It is an indirect severity/procedure-complexity proxy with weak unique linkage to AKI compared with hemodynamics/renal markers, so low sparse-selection priority.
Indicator/amount that any volatile anesthetic was used. This is largely practice-pattern and redundant with the specific volatile-agent variables, with minimal direct renal signal.

Use/dose of isoflurane during anesthesia. Volatile choice is mainly clinician preference and not a strong independent driver of postoperative creatinine trajectory, so unlikely to survive sparse selection.
Use/dose of sevoflurane during anesthesia. Any renal effect is small and confounded by case mix; adds little unique predictive value versus more proximal perfusion/renal features.
Use/dose of desflurane during anesthesia. Primarily reflects anesthetic strategy rather than kidney injury physiology, and is redundant with other volatile indicators.
Use of other volatile agents. Heterogeneous and mostly a documentation/practice-pattern variable, offering minimal interpretable or unique signal for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
Whether volatile anesthetic was used before cardiopulmonary bypass. Timing is intraop and largely reflects anesthetic workflow rather than renal hypoperfusion/AKI biology, so low priority.
Whether volatile anesthetic was used during bypass. This is a weak, indirect proxy for anesthetic management and is unlikely to add unique predictive value for postoperative creatinine ratio.
Whether volatile anesthetic was used after bypass. Still an intraoperative management marker with limited mechanistic connection to later AKI compared with perfusion/vasopressor/urine output signals.
Whether volatile anesthetic was used for maintenance. Highly redundant with other volatile-use flags and mainly reflects clinician choice, not a strong renal predictor.

Intraoperative dexmedetomidine infusion reflects sedation strategy and sometimes hemodynamic tolerance (bradycardia/hypotension). It is an indirect proxy and likely dominated by direct BP/pressor features, but could add small unique signal about instability/sedation depth.

Intraoperative propofol infusion reflects anesthetic technique and can contribute to hypotension. Predictive value for AKI is mostly mediated through hemodynamics and thus is indirect/redundant, but may weakly capture case complexity and instability.
Intraoperative midazolam infusion is primarily a sedation/practice-pattern variable. It has little direct relationship to renal injury physiology and is unlikely to be retained in a sparse model.

Total insulin given intraoperatively/at exit is a proxy for hyperglycemia/diabetes severity and stress response, which are associated with AKI risk. It can add some unique metabolic-risk information beyond anesthetic-choice variables, though still indirect.

Pre-anesthesia systolic BP captures baseline hemodynamic status and chronic hypertension/low perfusion reserve. It may modestly improve risk stratification for postoperative AKI but is distal and often dominated by intra/post-op hypotension measures.

Pre-anesthesia diastolic BP reflects vascular tone and coronary/renal perfusion pressure but is noisier and less directly tied to AKI than systolic/MAP-based measures. Likely redundant with stronger hemodynamic summaries, so lower sparse-selection priority.

Pre-induction heart rate is a nonspecific marker of baseline stress/fitness and periop physiology. It is an indirect, early signal and likely dominated by more proximal hemodynamic/vasopressor and renal markers for Hour-60 creatinine.

Use of a PA catheter is largely a clinician-behavior/illness-complexity proxy (sicker patients, complex hemodynamics). It may correlate with AKI risk but is not a direct renal signal and is likely redundant with actual hemodynamic/vasopressor features.

Maximum core temperature intraop/early periop reflects warming/fever but is not a strong mechanistic driver of AKI in this context. Likely weak and noisy relative to perfusion, fluids, and cardiac function variables.

Inhaled nitric oxide use indicates pulmonary hypertension/RV strain and severe cardiopulmonary physiology. It is an indirect proxy for low-output states that can contribute to AKI, but selection priority is limited versus direct cardiac function and perfusion markers.

Total intraop crystalloid reflects volume loading/hemodilution and can relate to renal congestion and postoperative creatinine kinetics. Moderately useful but partly confounded by practice patterns and overlaps with other fluid/albumin/colloid totals.

Synthetic colloid volume captures a specific resuscitation choice and may associate with AKI risk depending on agent, but is often low-frequency and practice-driven. Adds limited unique value beyond overall fluid strategy and illness severity.

Albumin administration reflects intravascular volume support and capillary leak/vasoplegia, which can track with renal hypoperfusion or congestion. Potentially informative and somewhat less redundant than generic crystalloid, but still partly clinician-choice driven.

Lowest intraop glucose may reflect metabolic stress, insulin use, or hypoglycemia episodes. It is an indirect and early signal with weaker mechanistic linkage to AKI than hemodynamics/renal perfusion variables.

Intraop vasodilator use suggests hypertension management or afterload reduction and is more a treatment marker than physiology. It is a weak, confounded proxy and likely redundant with direct blood pressure/vasopressor requirement features (not in this batch).
Intraoperative EEG monitoring mainly reflects anesthetic management and neurologic monitoring intensity. It is a clinician-behavior proxy with minimal direct connection to renal injury prediction.
Pre-incision TEE use is largely a procedural/monitoring choice indicating case complexity. It provides little unique physiologic information beyond the actual echo-derived function variables.

Pre-incision LVEF summarizes baseline LV systolic function and risk of low cardiac output and renal hypoperfusion postoperatively. Strong, relatively unique physiologic predictor within this batch for downstream creatinine rise.

Baseline RV function captures propensity for venous congestion, low forward flow, and need for high vasoactive support—key AKI pathways after cardiac surgery. Adds complementary information to LVEF and is a strong sparse-model candidate.

Mitral regurgitation severity reflects chronic volume overload and can influence periop hemodynamics. However it is an indirect contributor and often mediated through LV/RV function measures, limiting unique sparse-model value.

Mitral stenosis indicates fixed inflow obstruction and potential pulmonary hypertension/RV strain. Predictive value for AKI is indirect and likely captured better by RV function and need for advanced support proxies.

Aortic regurgitation severity reflects chronic LV volume load and periop hemodynamic considerations. It is less directly tied to postoperative renal dysfunction than global ventricular function measures and is likely redundant with LVEF-related risk.

Pre-incision aortic valve disease severity can correlate with perioperative hemodynamics and CPB complexity. It is an indirect, largely baseline structural marker and is usually dominated by direct post-op perfusion/renal signals for sparse selection.

Indicator that aortic valve area was assessed pre-incision (measurement/availability rather than physiology). Mostly a documentation/monitoring proxy with little unique value for predicting Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Preoperative tricuspid regurgitation reflects right-sided dysfunction/venous congestion that can predispose to AKI. Still indirect and likely redundant with downstream hemodynamic/volume balance features captured post-op.
Presence of a patent foramen ovale is generally unrelated to renal perfusion or AKI biology. Unlikely to add unique predictive value for creatinine trajectory in a sparse model.
Flag that the ascending aorta was assessed (process/measurement indicator). Primarily a clinician-behavior/documentation proxy and not a direct renal risk signal.
Ascending aortic size may reflect aortopathy and influence surgical complexity and CPB/cross-clamp strategy. It is a weak, indirect predictor of AKI compared with direct perfusion/renal markers.

Aortic atheroma burden can increase embolic risk and operative complexity, which may secondarily affect renal outcomes. However it is distal to kidney injury mechanisms and likely dominated by CPB use and post-op physiology.

Mobile atheroma suggests higher embolic potential and more complex aortic manipulation. This is an indirect risk factor for renal dysfunction and typically not a top sparse-model keeper versus direct hemodynamic/renal features.
Whether the aortic arch was visualized is mainly a procedural/assessment indicator. It provides little direct physiologic information and is unlikely to survive sparse feature selection.
Maximum arch atheroma severity reflects systemic atherosclerosis and embolic risk during surgery. It is still an indirect proxy and likely redundant with other operative complexity indicators.

Arch atheroma mobility indicates embolic potential and may correlate with complicated intraoperative management. Predictive value for creatinine ratio is indirect and usually weaker than direct perfusion/renal trajectory features.

Use of cardiopulmonary bypass is a strong upstream driver of AKI risk via inflammation, hemolysis, and nonpulsatile renal perfusion. It is a high-yield, nonredundant operative exposure likely to remain in a sparse model.

Autologous priming/retrograde autologous priming reflects perfusion strategy to reduce hemodilution/transfusion. It may weakly relate to renal outcomes but is a technique proxy and often dominated by CPB use and fluid/transfusion variables.

Perfusion-team crystalloid volume captures intraoperative hemodilution and fluid loading that can affect renal perfusion and postoperative congestion. Moderately useful but partially redundant with broader fluid balance and hemodynamic features.

Synthetic colloid exposure (if used) can be associated with renal injury risk and reflects intraoperative volume strategy. Potentially informative and less common (thus more distinctive), but still an indirect exposure and may be confounded by case complexity.

Albumin volume reflects intraoperative volume/oncotic strategy and may correlate with hemodynamics or capillary leak. Generally a weaker, treatment-choice proxy and often redundant with total fluid balance and vasopressor/hemodynamic signals.

Use of hemofiltration/perfusion ultrafiltration reflects significant fluid overload, metabolic derangement, or early renal support needs. It is a near-direct marker of evolving AKI risk and adds high unique predictive value for later creatinine rise.

Need for/ability to wean inotropes off CPB reflects myocardial dysfunction and low cardiac output risk. It is an indirect renal hypoperfusion signal and may be partly redundant with other hemodynamic severity markers, but can still help in a sparse model.

Difficulty weaning vasopressors off CPB indicates vasoplegia/shock physiology and risk of renal hypoperfusion. This is a strong perioperative severity signal with relatively direct linkage to AKI risk and can survive sparse selection.

Having a post-op intraoperative TEE is largely a clinician-behavior/complexity proxy (performed when concern exists). It is indirect and likely redundant with more physiologic/hemodynamic variables, so lower sparse-model priority.

SAM suggests dynamic LVOT obstruction that can cause episodic hypotension/low output. It is relatively rare and its renal impact is mediated through hemodynamics that are better captured by broader shock/vasopressor features.

Return to CPB for echocardiographic/hemodynamic issues signals intraoperative instability. It is a coarse event/proxy variable and overlaps with vasopressor/inotrope difficulty and lactate, limiting unique value.

Post-incision LVEF estimates baseline cardiac reserve and propensity for low cardiac output after surgery. It is moderately predictive but indirect for AKI and may be superseded by immediate post-CPB support requirements.

Postoperative RV dysfunction can drive low forward flow, venous congestion, and renal dysfunction. It is a meaningful hemodynamic contributor but somewhat subjective and overlaps with vasopressor/inotrope needs.

Intraoperative cardiac arrhythmias can cause transient hypoperfusion and instability. Often episodic and variably consequential, and its renal signal is typically captured better by lactate/pressor requirements.

Post-op core temperature reflects rewarming strategy and perioperative management rather than kidney-specific physiology. It is weakly and indirectly related to AKI and unlikely to survive sparse selection.
INR reflects coagulopathy/hepatic dysfunction and bleeding/transfusion milieu. It is an indirect severity marker and usually redundant with more proximal perfusion/renal stress indicators.
WBC is a nonspecific inflammation/stress marker in the immediate post-op period. It is noisy and weakly linked to later creatinine changes compared with perfusion and renal-support variables.

Platelet count can reflect CPB-related consumption, bleeding, or systemic inflammation. It is a nonspecific severity proxy and typically dominated by more direct hypoperfusion/metabolic markers for AKI prediction.

Hematocrit captures anemia/hemodilution and potential oxygen delivery limitations after CPB. It can contribute unique signal about renal oxygenation risk, though it is still indirect and influenced by transfusion/fluid practices.
Fibrinogen reflects acute-phase response and coagulation status post-CPB. It is not a direct renal injury marker and is unlikely to add unique predictive value in a sparse model.

Postoperative lactate is a strong marker of global hypoperfusion/low cardiac output and correlates with subsequent AKI risk. It provides high-yield, relatively proximal physiologic information likely to remain predictive for Hour 60 creatinine ratio.

Postoperative propofol exposure mainly reflects sedation/ventilation management rather than renal physiology. Likely a clinician-behavior proxy and weakly/indirectly related to AKI risk, so low sparse-selection priority.

Pain score on POD3 is temporally misaligned with features available only through 36h and is more a recovery/analgesia marker than a renal signal. Unlikely to add unique predictive value for Hour-60 creatinine ratio in a sparse linear model.

Current smoking captures baseline vascular risk and pulmonary comorbidity that can correlate with postoperative complications. However it is indirect for AKI and often dominated by more proximal hemodynamic/lab signals, so only a weak keeper.

Former smoking is a coarse historical risk factor with heterogeneous exposure and weaker association than current smoking. It is indirect and likely redundant with other comorbidity/severity features, so low priority.

Paroxysmal AF indicates underlying cardiac disease and potential perioperative hemodynamic instability. Still an indirect predictor of AKI and likely dominated by post-op hemodynamics/vasopressors, so modest value.

Persistent AF is a stronger marker of chronic cardiac dysfunction and may predict low cardiac output states that predispose to renal hypoperfusion. It can add baseline risk information not fully captured by immediate vitals, so moderate sparse-model priority.

Antegrade cardioplegia delivery is primarily a surgical technique choice and not a direct renal insult marker. Any association with AKI is likely confounded by procedure complexity and redundant with stronger perioperative physiology variables.
Retrograde cardioplegia is also a technique/complexity proxy rather than a direct kidney function signal. Likely redundant with other operative severity indicators and weak for sparse selection.

Blood cardioplegia type reflects institutional/surgeon preference and case characteristics more than renal physiology. Expected to be a weak, indirect predictor and not competitive for a sparse feature budget.

Crystalloid cardioplegia is similarly a procedural choice with limited direct mechanistic link to postoperative creatinine trajectory. Likely redundant with other operative/context variables and low priority.

Acute heart failure history indicates recent decompensation and higher risk of perioperative low-output states affecting renal perfusion. It provides baseline risk information but remains indirect compared with post-op hemodynamics, so moderate priority.

Chronic heart failure is a strong baseline determinant of renal vulnerability (cardiorenal physiology) and postoperative low cardiac output risk. This comorbidity can add unique pre-op risk signal that complements early post-op measurements, making it a strong sparse-model candidate.

Systolic HF (reduced EF phenotype) is closely tied to low forward flow and renal hypoperfusion risk after cardiac surgery. Likely more predictive than generic HF labels and can survive sparse selection as a high-value baseline feature.

Diastolic HF reflects impaired filling and congestion risk, which can contribute to renal venous congestion and AKI. Predictive but often less directly tied to low-output physiology than systolic HF, so slightly lower priority.

Mean SpO2 in the immediate 0–12h window is often influenced by transient post-op factors (ventilation, sedation) and is a nonspecific severity marker. As a mean aggregation it may miss hypoxemic extremes and is unlikely to add unique AKI prediction value.

Mean SpO2 at 13–24h is somewhat more reflective of early trajectory than 0–12h and may correlate with cardiopulmonary dysfunction affecting renal perfusion. Still nonspecific and mean-based (not capturing hypoxemia extremes), so only modest sparse-model value.

Represents average oxygen saturation in the closest (25–36h) window. Mean SpO2 is a nonspecific illness/respiratory support marker and is usually dominated by hypoxemia extremes for renal-risk signal in a sparse model.
Represents peak oxygen saturation immediately post-op (0–12h). Max SpO2 is typically uninformative (often near 100% with supplemental O2) and early timing adds noise, so low sparse-selection value.
Represents peak oxygen saturation in 13–24h. Max SpO2 is generally a ceiling effect and provides little unique information about evolving AKI risk compared with hypoxemia measures.
Represents peak oxygen saturation in 25–36h. Even in the closest window, maximum SpO2 is largely a therapy/ceiling artifact and unlikely to survive sparse feature selection.

Represents worst hypoxemia in the immediate 0–12h post-op period. Hypoxemia can contribute to renal injury, but early post-bypass/anesthesia effects make it less reliable for Hour-60 creatinine than later minima.
Represents worst hypoxemia in 13–24h, reflecting early trajectory after initial post-op noise. Minimum SpO2 can capture clinically meaningful hypoxic stress and may add some unique predictive value.

Represents worst hypoxemia in the closest available window (25–36h). This extreme is the most informative SpO2 aggregation for downstream AKI risk and is the likeliest SpO2 feature to keep in a sparse model.
Represents frequency of SpO2 measurements in 0–12h, mainly a monitoring/acuity proxy. It is not a physiologic renal signal and is especially confounded by routine immediate post-op monitoring.
Represents frequency of SpO2 measurements in 13–24h, reflecting clinician behavior and care intensity. Typically redundant with severity markers and weak for sparse selection.

Represents frequency of SpO2 measurements in 25–36h. May weakly proxy ongoing instability/ventilation needs in the closest window, but remains indirect and generally dominated by actual hypoxemia minima.

Represents average right pulmonary artery pressure early (0–12h), a marker of RV afterload/volume status. It is indirect for renal hypoperfusion and early post-op hemodynamics are noisy, limiting sparse-model priority.

Represents average right pulmonary artery pressure in 13–24h, reflecting evolving RV/pulmonary vascular status. Can relate to venous congestion and renal dysfunction, but mean is less sensitive than extremes and may be redundant with other hemodynamic features.

Represents average right pulmonary artery pressure in 25–36h, closest to the outcome window. Potentially informative for ongoing congestion/RV dysfunction affecting kidneys, but still an indirect mean-based signal.
Represents peak right pulmonary artery pressure in 0–12h. Extremes can capture acute RV strain/congestion, but immediate post-op variability reduces reliability for Hour-60 creatinine prediction.

Represents peak right pulmonary artery pressure in 13–24h. The max better captures episodic RV failure/venous congestion than the mean, but remains an indirect renal-risk marker and may be redundant with other congestion/hemodynamic variables.

Represents peak right pulmonary artery pressure in 25–36h, the closest window and most informative aggregation in this PA-pressure family. Peaks may reflect clinically important congestion/RV dysfunction that can drive AKI, making it a stronger sparse-model candidate than mean values.

Minimum mean PAP in the first 12h reflects early RV/pulmonary vascular load and congestion that can affect renal perfusion. However it is an indirect AKI signal and early post-op values are noisy, so it is a lower-priority sparse feature.

Minimum mean PAP during 13–24h may capture evolving pulmonary hypertension/RV dysfunction contributing to venous congestion. Still indirect for kidney injury and likely dominated by more direct congestion markers (e.g., CVP), so modest sparse value.

Minimum mean PAP in 25–36h is closer to the 60h creatinine outcome and may reflect persistent RV/pulmonary pressure burden affecting renal perfusion. Among PAP-min epochs this is the most temporally relevant, but remains a secondary/indirect renal predictor.

Count/frequency of mean PAP measurements in 0–12h is primarily a monitoring-intensity/clinician-behavior proxy. It adds little unique physiologic information for a sparse model and is likely confounded by care patterns.

Measurement frequency of mean PAP in 13–24h reflects how closely the team is monitoring hemodynamics rather than renal physiology. Typically redundant with severity-of-illness and unlikely to survive sparse selection.
Measurement frequency of mean PAP in 25–36h is a proxy for ongoing invasive monitoring/instability. It is not a direct biomarker and is generally weak and confounded for predicting creatinine ratio.

Frequency of continuous cardiac index device readings in 0–12h mainly indicates device presence and monitoring intensity. It is a clinician-behavior proxy and early post-op monitoring is common, so minimal unique predictive value.

Measurement frequency of continuous cardiac index in 13–24h reflects ongoing hemodynamic monitoring rather than actual perfusion status. As a proxy variable it is unlikely to add stable, unique signal in a sparse linear model.

Ongoing continuous cardiac index monitoring at 25–36h may indicate persistent shock/low-output concern, which can relate to AKI risk. Still largely a monitoring proxy (not the CI value), so only a weak-to-modest sparse candidate.

Mean CVP in 0–12h reflects venous congestion and right-sided filling pressures, which are mechanistically linked to renal dysfunction. Early values can be confounded by fluid shifts, but CVP is a relatively direct congestion signal worth moderate consideration.

Mean CVP in 13–24h captures sustained venous congestion after initial post-op turbulence. Useful for AKI prediction but somewhat redundant with CVP extremes (max) that better capture harmful congestion.

Mean CVP in 25–36h is a near-term indicator of persistent venous congestion, a strong driver of postoperative AKI and creatinine rise. This timing makes it a strong sparse-model candidate, though still partially redundant with CVP max.

Peak CVP in 0–12h captures episodes of severe venous congestion that can impair renal filtration. However early post-op peaks may be transient and influenced by ventilation/fluid boluses, lowering sparse priority versus later peaks.

Peak CVP in 13–24h captures clinically important congestion extremes more directly than the mean and is less dominated by immediate post-op noise. It is a strong candidate because congestion is a key mechanistic AKI pathway.

Peak CVP in 25–36h is the most temporally proximate and pathophysiologically specific congestion extreme available, strongly linked to renal venous hypertension and AKI progression. Among this batch it is the highest-yield unique predictor likely to survive sparse selection.

Minimum CVP in 0–12h may reflect relative hypovolemia/low preload, which can contribute to renal hypoperfusion. But it is less specific than hypotension/low output measures and early minima are noisy, so low sparse priority.

Minimum CVP in 13–24h reflects venous congestion/right-sided filling pressures that can impair renal perfusion. Moderately useful but partly redundant with the closer 25–36h CVP signal and CVP is an indirect renal marker.

Minimum CVP in 25–36h captures near-term venous congestion/low preload states closest to the 60h creatinine outcome. Stronger sparse-model candidate than earlier CVP because timing is closer and extremes can reflect hemodynamic insults relevant to AKI.

Count/frequency of CVP measurements in 0–12h is mainly a monitoring-intensity/line-availability proxy rather than physiology. Early post-op measurement frequency is especially confounded and unlikely to add unique predictive value.

Frequency of CVP measurements in 13–24h reflects clinician behavior/acuity rather than a direct renal mechanism. Typically dominated by actual CVP values and other severity markers in sparse selection.

Frequency of CVP measurements in 25–36h may weakly proxy ongoing hemodynamic instability closer to the outcome. Still largely a clinician-behavior signal and usually inferior to using CVP extremes themselves.

Mean FiO2 in 0–12h reflects early ventilator support/oxygenation strategy, heavily influenced by routine post-op management. It is an indirect severity proxy and early values are noisy for predicting 60h creatinine ratio.
Mean FiO2 in 13–24h indicates sustained oxygen requirement and overall cardiopulmonary severity. Some prognostic value but indirect for AKI and likely redundant with FiO2 max in the same epoch.

Mean FiO2 in 25–36h captures ongoing respiratory/circulatory illness closer to the 60h outcome. Still a nonspecific severity marker and typically less informative than an extreme (max) for sparse selection.

Peak FiO2 in 0–12h captures early severe oxygenation/instability but is confounded by immediate post-bypass/anesthesia practices. Useful as a severity proxy yet early timing limits unique predictive value for 60h creatinine.

Peak FiO2 in 13–24h reflects episodes of significant oxygenation failure or hemodynamic compromise, which can correlate with renal hypoperfusion risk. More informative than the mean and earlier epoch, but still indirect for kidney injury.

Peak FiO2 in 25–36h captures late episodes of high oxygen requirement closest to the outcome window. A reasonable sparse-model severity feature, though still indirect and potentially redundant with other hemodynamic/respiratory markers not shown here.
Minimum FiO2 in 0–12h mostly reflects weaning practices and routine ventilation targets rather than organ dysfunction. Early minima are unlikely to add unique signal beyond max/mean FiO2.
Minimum FiO2 in 13–24h is largely a weaning/management indicator and not a direct physiologic insult marker. Typically dominated by FiO2 max or mean for capturing severity.
Minimum FiO2 in 25–36h mainly reflects ability to wean oxygen and is less sensitive to acute decompensation than FiO2 max. Low priority for sparse selection due to indirectness and redundancy.
Frequency of FiO2 recordings in 0–12h is a documentation/monitoring proxy rather than a physiologic biomarker. Unlikely to contribute unique predictive value for creatinine ratio.

Frequency of FiO2 measurements in 13–24h reflects charting intensity and ventilator management rather than kidney-relevant physiology. Generally redundant with actual FiO2 levels and other acuity indicators.

Count/frequency of FiO2 documentation in 25–36h, mainly reflecting monitoring intensity and ventilator management. It is a clinician-behavior proxy with weak direct linkage to AKI and unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Average set ventilator respiratory rate in the immediate 0–12h post-op period. This is an indirect severity/ventilation-management signal and early post-op noise makes it low-yield for predicting 60h creatinine ratio.

Average set respiratory rate during 13–24h, reflecting ongoing ventilatory support needs. Some association with illness severity/acid-base issues, but still indirect and likely dominated by more proximal or extreme physiologic/renal features not in this batch.

Maximum set respiratory rate in 0–12h, which may capture brief episodes of acidosis/hypercapnia management. Extremes are more informative than means, but this is early and remains a nonspecific proxy for AKI risk.

Maximum set respiratory rate in 13–24h, potentially capturing sustained/worsening respiratory failure or metabolic acidosis compensation. Within the RR family this extreme is the most informative and later than 0–12h, so it has moderate sparse-model value.
Minimum set respiratory rate in 0–12h, often reflecting sedation/controlled ventilation rather than organ dysfunction. It is weakly related to renal outcomes and redundant with other RR summaries.

Minimum set respiratory rate in 13–24h, typically driven by ventilator strategy and sedation rather than kidney physiology. Adds little unique predictive value compared with RR maxima or other severity markers.
Frequency of recording set respiratory rate in 0–12h, a monitoring/EMR density proxy. Not a physiologic biomarker and unlikely to contribute uniquely in a sparse model.

Frequency of recording set respiratory rate in 13–24h, reflecting documentation intensity and care complexity. This is indirect and generally dominated by actual physiologic values rather than measurement counts.
Frequency of recording set respiratory rate in 25–36h, again a proxy for monitoring intensity. Despite being closer to the outcome window, it remains weak and nonspecific for AKI prediction.

Average set tidal volume in 0–12h, largely determined by ventilator protocol (e.g., PBW-based) rather than evolving renal dysfunction. Early timing and high practice-pattern dependence make it low priority.

Average set tidal volume in 13–24h, mostly reflecting ventilator strategy and patient size rather than kidney injury. Provides little unique signal for creatinine ratio compared with hemodynamics/renal labs.

Average set tidal volume in 25–36h may reflect ongoing ventilatory dependence and lung mechanics closer to the 60h target. Still largely protocol-driven and indirect for AKI, so only a weak-to-plausible sparse candidate.
Maximum set tidal volume in 0–12h, often reflecting initial ventilator settings or transient adjustments. It is not kidney-specific and is early/noisy, so unlikely to be selected.
Maximum set tidal volume in 13–24h, mainly a ventilation-management artifact with limited renal pathophysiologic specificity. Redundant with other tidal volume summaries and low sparse-model value.

Maximum set tidal volume in 25–36h could capture less lung-protective ventilation or changing mechanics closer to outcome. Still indirect and practice-pattern driven, but among TV features this later extreme is the most defensible.

Minimum set tidal volume in the immediate post-op window mainly reflects ventilator strategy/size rather than renal physiology. Weak, indirect signal for AKI and likely dominated by hemodynamics/renal labs in sparse selection.

Minimum set tidal volume over 13–24h is still primarily a ventilation management parameter with limited direct linkage to creatinine rise. Adds little unique predictive value for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Minimum set tidal volume closest to the outcome may proxy ongoing respiratory failure/ARDS severity and associated multi-organ dysfunction. Still indirect and likely redundant with stronger severity/hemodynamic features, so low priority.

Count/frequency of tidal volume set measurements is a monitoring-intensity proxy rather than a physiologic biomarker. Early post-op documentation frequency is noisy and unlikely to survive sparse feature selection.
Measurement frequency of tidal volume settings reflects clinician behavior and charting patterns more than kidney risk. Provides minimal unique information for predicting creatinine_ratio.

Later measurement frequency may weakly indicate ongoing ventilator dependence/ICU complexity, which can correlate with AKI risk. Still a proxy variable and generally dominated by direct physiologic/renal signals.

Mean PEEP in 0–12h reflects early ventilatory support and can affect venous return/renal perfusion, but this window is confounded by immediate post-bypass physiology. Likely redundant with later PEEP and hemodynamic variables.

Mean PEEP over 13–24h may capture sustained intrathoracic pressure burden and respiratory failure severity. However, mean smooths extremes and is less informative than max PEEP for acute insult, so lower sparse priority.

Mean PEEP in 25–36h is temporally closer and may reflect sustained high PEEP affecting renal perfusion and ongoing lung injury severity. Moderately useful but still indirect and partly redundant with max PEEP.

Maximum PEEP in the immediate post-op period captures acute respiratory support intensity but is often transient and influenced by OR/early ICU practices. Some severity signal, but timing noise limits sparse-model value.

Maximum PEEP in 13–24h better reflects evolving respiratory failure and higher intrathoracic pressures that can impair renal perfusion. More informative than mean/min, but still an indirect AKI predictor.

Maximum PEEP in 25–36h is the strongest aggregation here for capturing severe ongoing respiratory failure and high intrathoracic pressure burden near the prediction horizon. Likely the best ventilator-derived candidate to retain in a sparse model from this batch.
Minimum PEEP early post-op often reflects baseline ventilator settings and is less tied to acute severity or renal hypoperfusion. Dominated by max/mean PEEP and weak for sparse selection.
Minimum PEEP over 13–24h is typically a floor setting and provides little incremental information beyond max/mean PEEP. Low unique predictive value for creatinine_ratio.

Minimum PEEP in 25–36h may indicate whether the patient ever tolerates low support, weakly reflecting recovery vs persistent lung pathology. Still less informative than max PEEP and thus low-to-moderate priority.
PEEP measurement frequency is primarily a documentation/monitoring proxy and not a direct physiologic marker. Early post-op charting intensity is noisy and unlikely to add unique predictive value.

Count of PEEP recordings in hours 13–24, mainly reflecting monitoring intensity/vent management rather than renal physiology. Low unique value for creatinine_ratio and largely a clinician-behavior proxy.

Count of PEEP recordings in hours 25–36; closer to the outcome window so may weakly proxy ongoing respiratory instability/ICU complexity. Still primarily a measurement-frequency proxy and unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Average plateau pressure in the immediate 0–12h post-op period, reflecting early ventilator mechanics. Early window is noisy and mean smooths extremes; weak and redundant with later/extreme summaries.

Average plateau pressure at 13–24h, a marker of lung compliance/ARDS severity that can correlate with overall illness and hemodynamics. Still indirect for AKI and dominated by extreme (max) measures if kept.
Peak plateau pressure in 0–12h may capture acute high intrathoracic pressure that can reduce venous return/renal perfusion. However it is early post-op and often transient; modest priority.

Peak plateau pressure in 13–24h captures clinically important extremes (stiff lungs/high pressures) that may impact cardiac output and renal perfusion. Among plateau-pressure variants, this extreme is the most informative, but remains an indirect AKI predictor.
Minimum plateau pressure in 0–12h is usually not a renal-risk signal and may reflect ventilator settings or measurement artifacts. Low relevance and dominated by max/extreme injury signals.
Minimum plateau pressure in 13–24h is not a clear marker of renal hypoperfusion or evolving AKI. Adds little unique predictive value versus max/mean plateau pressure.
Number of plateau pressure measurements in 0–12h reflects monitoring/ventilator management intensity. It is a weak proxy for severity and unlikely to be selected in a sparse model.
Number of plateau pressure measurements in 13–24h is primarily a clinician-behavior/monitoring proxy. Minimal direct renal information and redundant with other severity indicators.

Number of plateau pressure measurements in 25–36h may weakly indicate persistent respiratory failure closer to the outcome time. Still a measurement-frequency proxy with limited unique value for creatinine_ratio.
Average minute ventilation in 0–12h reflects early ventilatory support and metabolic/respiratory status. Early post-op variability and indirectness to kidney injury make it low priority.

Average minute ventilation in 13–24h can reflect sustained metabolic acidosis/CO2 clearance needs and overall severity. Indirect for AKI and likely redundant with stronger hemodynamic/lab predictors outside this batch.
Peak minute ventilation in 0–12h may capture episodes of high ventilatory demand (acidosis, agitation) that correlate with severity. However it is early and nonspecific for renal dysfunction.

Peak minute ventilation in 13–24h captures extremes of ventilatory demand that may reflect evolving shock/acidosis and higher illness burden. Within minute-ventilation features, the max is the most informative, but remains an indirect AKI signal.

Minimum minute ventilation in 0–12h is often driven by ventilator weaning/sedation practices rather than pathophysiology linked to AKI. Low unique predictive value and high susceptibility to practice variation.

Minimum minute ventilation reflects the lowest ventilatory support/drive in 13–24h, which is only indirectly related to AKI risk via overall respiratory failure/sedation. Likely redundant with more direct hemodynamic/renal features and not a strong unique predictor of creatinine ratio.
Count/frequency of minute ventilation measurements in 0–12h is mainly a monitoring-intensity proxy. Early post-op measurement frequency is noisy and unlikely to add unique value in a sparse model.

Measurement frequency for minute ventilation in 13–24h reflects clinician attention/ventilation complexity rather than renal physiology. Typically dominated by actual physiologic extremes and direct renal/hemodynamic variables.

Later (25–36h) measurement frequency may weakly proxy ongoing critical illness/vent dependence closer to the outcome window. Still primarily a behavior/monitoring signal and usually not worth a sparse feature slot.

Average oxygen device level in 0–12h reflects early respiratory support needs, which are indirect for AKI prediction. Early post-op respiratory support is often routine/transient and low-yield for sparse selection.
