Mean oxygen device level in 13–24h captures sustained respiratory support needs, a general severity marker that can correlate with AKI risk. However it is indirect and likely redundant with stronger physiologic predictors.

Mean oxygen device level in 25–36h is closer to Hour 60 and may reflect persistent cardiopulmonary dysfunction associated with renal hypoperfusion. Still a coarse, clinician-driven severity proxy and not a top sparse-model feature.

Maximum oxygen device level in 0–12h captures the highest early respiratory support, which can reflect immediate post-op instability. Early extremes are less reliable for Hour 60 creatinine and remain indirect for kidney injury.

Maximum oxygen device level in 13–24h captures episodes of significant hypoxemia/respiratory failure, which can contribute to renal ischemia and correlate with severity. Among device summaries, this extreme is more informative than means/mins, but still indirect.

Maximum oxygen device level in 25–36h reflects late, severe respiratory support needs closest to the prediction horizon and may track ongoing shock/low output states linked to AKI. It’s the strongest in this device family but remains a proxy compared with direct renal/hemodynamic measures.

Minimum oxygen device level in 0–12h largely reflects periods of stability or routine weaning immediately post-op. This is unlikely to add unique predictive value for creatinine ratio and is dominated by max/extreme support measures.
Minimum oxygen device level in 13–24h reflects best respiratory status rather than insult severity. Typically not informative for AKI prediction and redundant with max/mean support indicators.

Minimum oxygen device level in 25–36h captures the least support needed, which is not a strong signal for impending renal dysfunction. In sparse selection it is dominated by max support and direct renal/hemodynamic variables.
Frequency of oxygen device documentation in 0–12h is a monitoring/workflow proxy. Early post-op documentation intensity is noisy and not a direct physiologic predictor of creatinine ratio.
Measurement frequency for oxygen device in 13–24h reflects care intensity rather than renal pathophysiology. Unlikely to survive sparse-model selection when physiologic extremes are available.

Later measurement frequency (25–36h) may weakly indicate ongoing respiratory instability and ICU complexity closer to Hour 60. Still primarily a clinician-behavior proxy and low priority for a sparse linear model.

Average early postoperative chest tube drainage reflects bleeding/inflammation and can drive transfusion/volume shifts that indirectly affect renal perfusion. In a sparse model it is an indirect, early-window signal and likely dominated by later or extreme drainage measures.

Mean chest tube output in 13–24h captures ongoing bleeding/serous drainage with potential hemodynamic consequences. Still indirect for AKI and typically redundant with max output or more proximal (25–36h) physiology.

Mean chest tube output closest to the target window may reflect persistent bleeding/effusions contributing to anemia, transfusion, and renal hypoperfusion risk. Among means, this is the most temporally relevant, but remains an indirect proxy compared with direct renal/hemodynamic markers.

Peak early chest tube output captures acute bleeding episodes that can precipitate hypotension/transfusion and downstream AKI risk. Extremes are more informative than means, but the 0–12h window is noisy and less predictive of Hour-60 creatinine trajectory.

Maximum chest tube output in 13–24h reflects significant ongoing bleeding with potential renal hypoperfusion and transfusion exposure. Useful as an extreme-value summary, but still an indirect pathway and may be redundant with other severity/hemodynamic variables.

Maximum chest tube output in 25–36h captures late/persistent bleeding closest to the prediction horizon, which can strongly affect perfusion, anemia, and need for re-intervention/transfusion. This extreme, proximal measure is the strongest chest-tube-derived candidate for sparse selection in this batch.
Minimum chest tube output is usually near zero and reflects periods of low drainage rather than a renal-relevant insult. It is low-information and dominated by max/mean output measures.
Minimum drainage in 13–24h is typically uninformative (often zero) and does not capture bleeding severity. Redundant and unlikely to survive sparse feature selection.
Minimum chest tube output in 25–36h mainly indicates low/absent drainage and adds little about hemodynamic stress or AKI risk. Strongly dominated by max output in the same epoch.

Measurement count/frequency is primarily a monitoring-intensity/ICU workflow proxy rather than a physiologic renal signal. Early postoperative documentation patterns add noise and are unlikely to add unique predictive value.
How often chest tube output is recorded reflects clinician attention and unit practice more than bleeding severity. As a proxy variable it is low priority versus actual output extremes.

Recording frequency in 25–36h may correlate with complexity but is not a direct biomarker and is easily confounded by care patterns. Low likelihood of surviving sparse selection compared with max output.

Mean systolic pulmonary artery pressure early post-op reflects RV afterload/volume status and can relate indirectly to renal congestion/perfusion. However, the early window is noisy and mean values may miss transient extremes that matter more for AKI risk.

Mean PAP systolic in 13–24h may capture evolving RV dysfunction or pulmonary hypertension contributing to venous congestion and reduced renal perfusion. Moderately useful but still indirect and potentially redundant with other hemodynamic/vasopressor features.

Mean PAP systolic closest to Hour 60 can reflect sustained RV strain/venous congestion, a plausible driver of worsening creatinine in older cardiac surgery patients. This proximal, sustained hemodynamic signal is a strong candidate within this batch.

Peak early PAP systolic captures acute pulmonary hypertension/RV stress episodes that can impair forward flow and renal perfusion. Extremes are informative, but the 0–12h period is confounded by immediate post-bypass physiology and may be less stable for Hour-60 prediction.

Peak systolic pulmonary artery pressure in hours 13–24 reflects RV afterload/pulmonary hypertension and venous congestion risk. Indirect for AKI and likely redundant with later (25–36h) extremes, so low sparse-selection priority.

Peak systolic pulmonary artery pressure in hours 25–36 is a near-term marker of RV strain/pulmonary hypertension that can contribute to renal venous congestion and low forward flow. Among PAP systolic summaries, this later extreme is the most plausible keeper, but remains indirect for creatinine rise.

Minimum systolic pulmonary artery pressure in the immediate 0–12h post-op window is heavily influenced by transient peri-bypass/anesthesia effects. Weak and noisy for Hour-60 creatinine ratio and dominated by later-window hemodynamics.

Minimum systolic pulmonary artery pressure in hours 13–24 may reflect low pulmonary pressures from low preload/low output states. Still an indirect renal signal and less informative than the closest (25–36h) window, so unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Minimum systolic pulmonary artery pressure in hours 25–36 can proxy low preload/low cardiac output physiology near the prediction horizon. It may add some unique information versus PAP maxima, but overall remains a secondary, indirect AKI predictor.

Count/frequency of systolic PAP measurements in 0–12h mainly reflects monitoring intensity and catheter use rather than physiology. As a clinician-behavior proxy and early window, it has minimal sparse-model value.

Measurement frequency of systolic PAP in 13–24h is primarily a proxy for illness complexity/monitoring rather than a direct renal-risk signal. Typically redundant with other severity indicators and low priority for sparse inclusion.

Measurement frequency of systolic PAP in 25–36h may weakly indicate ongoing hemodynamic instability requiring close monitoring. Still a proxy variable and generally dominated by actual pressure extremes, so only modest value.

Mean diastolic PAP in 0–12h reflects average pulmonary vascular/venous pressures but is confounded by immediate post-op fluid shifts. Mean aggregation blunts extremes and is early/noisy, making it a poor sparse-model candidate.

Mean diastolic PAP in 13–24h can reflect sustained pulmonary pressures and potential venous congestion. However, mean values are often less predictive than extremes and are indirect for AKI, so low-to-moderate priority.

Mean diastolic PAP in 25–36h is the closest sustained congestion/afterload signal available and may relate to renal venous congestion. Still indirect and potentially redundant with diastolic maxima, but could be a reasonable secondary keeper within this batch.

Peak diastolic PAP in 0–12h may capture early pulmonary congestion/left-sided filling pressure surrogates. The early window is noisy, but an extreme is more informative than a mean, yielding limited sparse value.

Peak diastolic PAP in 13–24h captures episodes of elevated pulmonary pressures/possible congestion that can contribute to renal venous congestion. More informative than mean/min, but still less compelling than the closest 25–36h extreme.

Peak diastolic PAP in 25–36h is the strongest near-term congestion/afterload extreme in this batch and most plausibly linked to evolving cardiorenal physiology before Hour 60. Among these PAP features, this is the most likely to survive sparse selection.
Minimum diastolic PAP in 0–12h is susceptible to transient post-op effects and does not directly encode renal injury risk. It is a weak, indirect signal and dominated by later-window measures.

Minimum diastolic PAP in 13–24h may reflect low filling pressures/low output physiology but is an indirect and less specific renal predictor than maxima. Likely redundant with other hemodynamic indicators, so low priority.

Minimum diastolic pulmonary artery pressure in the 25–36h window, reflecting RV/pulmonary vascular loading and venous congestion. Can relate to renal congestion/perfusion but is indirect and likely weaker than more direct renal/hemodynamic markers, so moderate sparse-model priority.

Count/frequency of diastolic PAP measurements in the first 12h, mainly reflecting monitoring intensity. This is a clinician-behavior proxy and early post-op noise, so low priority for sparse selection.

Frequency of diastolic PAP measurements in 13–24h, a proxy for illness complexity/monitoring rather than physiology. Adds little unique predictive value versus actual PAP values, so very low priority.

Frequency of diastolic PAP measurements in 25–36h, reflecting ongoing need for invasive monitoring. Slightly more informative than earlier windows as a severity proxy, but still indirect and dominated by physiologic extremes.
Average heart rate in the immediate 0–12h post-op period. Mean HR is a nonspecific stress/illness marker and early values are noisy, so weak sparse-model candidate.

Average heart rate in 13–24h, reflecting sustained sympathetic drive/arrhythmia burden. Still nonspecific for AKI and likely redundant with more informative extremes and other hemodynamics, so low priority.

Average heart rate in 25–36h, closer to the outcome and may capture persistent tachycardia from low output/sepsis/AF affecting renal perfusion. Moderately useful but indirect and often redundant with BP/vasopressor signals.
Peak heart rate in 0–12h, capturing transient tachyarrhythmia/stress immediately after surgery. Early peaks are often perioperative noise and nonspecific, so limited sparse-model value.
Peak heart rate in 13–24h, which can reflect clinically meaningful tachyarrhythmia or evolving shock impacting renal perfusion. More informative than mean HR but still indirect, so moderate priority.

Peak heart rate in 25–36h, closer to Hour 60 and may capture ongoing instability/AF with RVR associated with renal hypoperfusion. Useful as an extreme but still a general severity marker, so moderate priority.

Lowest heart rate in 13–24h, reflecting bradycardia/pacing needs. Generally indirect for renal outcomes and often clinician-driven, so weak sparse-model candidate.

Lowest heart rate in 25–36h, which may indicate conduction disease or over-sedation/pacing. Still not a strong or specific precursor of creatinine rise compared with perfusion/renal markers, so low priority.
Frequency of pulse measurements in 0–12h, largely reflecting routine monitoring intensity. This is a proxy variable with minimal unique physiologic signal, so very low priority.
Frequency of pulse measurements in 13–24h, mainly indicating monitoring/acuity rather than renal physiology. Unlikely to survive sparse selection when true hemodynamic variables are available.

Frequency of pulse measurements in 25–36h, still primarily a monitoring-intensity proxy. Adds little unique predictive value for creatinine_ratio beyond actual HR/hemodynamic features, so lowest priority.

Mean inspired O2 from anesthetic/respiratory gas delivery in the immediate post-op window. Weak and indirect for AKI prediction and largely reflects ventilator/OR practice rather than renal physiology; likely dominated by hemodynamics/renal markers.

Count/frequency of recorded inspired O2 values in 0–12h. Primarily a monitoring/documentation intensity proxy rather than physiology; low value for sparse modeling.
Measurement frequency of inspired O2 in 13–24h. Still mainly reflects care intensity/ventilation workflow and is not a renal biomarker; unlikely to survive feature selection.
Measurement frequency of inspired O2 in 25–36h, closer to the Hour-60 target. Could weakly proxy ongoing respiratory failure/ICU complexity, but remains indirect and clinician-behavior driven.
Mean nitrous oxide delivery early post-op, largely reflecting intraoperative/anesthesia practice. Minimal mechanistic link to AKI trajectory and likely noise/redundant with other periop factors.
Maximum nitrous oxide exposure in 0–12h. Not a recognized driver of postoperative creatinine rise and mostly indicates anesthetic technique; very low sparse-model priority.
Minimum nitrous oxide exposure early post-op. Essentially a practice artifact (often zero) with little physiologic information relevant to renal dysfunction.
Frequency of nitrous oxide documentation in 0–12h. Monitoring/protocol proxy with negligible direct renal predictive content.
Frequency of nitrous oxide documentation in 13–24h. N2O is rarely used in ICU; this is likely sparse/noisy and not informative for AKI prediction.
Frequency of nitrous oxide documentation in 25–36h. Almost certainly reflects documentation artifacts rather than physiology; no unique value for creatinine_ratio.
Mean medical air delivery early post-op, essentially complementary to FiO2 settings. Indirect and redundant with oxygen/ventilator management signals; low priority for sparse selection.
Maximum air flow/delivery early post-op, reflecting ventilator gas blending rather than patient renal status. Not a plausible unique predictor of Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
Minimum air delivery early post-op, often driven by FiO2 changes and equipment settings. Highly redundant and not pathophysiologically tied to AKI risk.
Measurement frequency of air delivery in 0–12h. Primarily a documentation/monitoring proxy with little direct relevance to evolving kidney dysfunction.

Count/frequency of recorded “air” as an anesthetic agent is essentially a documentation/monitoring artifact. It has no direct renal physiology and is unlikely to add unique predictive value for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Average isoflurane exposure in the immediate post-op window mainly reflects intraop/early sedation practice and hemodynamic management. It is indirect for renal injury and likely dominated by more direct perfusion/renal markers, so low sparse-selection priority.

Peak isoflurane dose may correlate with episodes of vasodilation/hypotension and overall anesthetic intensity. Still an indirect, early (0–12h) clinician-driven signal and unlikely to survive sparse selection over direct perfusion/AKI predictors.

Measurement frequency of isoflurane in 13–24h reflects sedation/ventilation management and documentation patterns. It is an indirect proxy and typically dominated by actual hemodynamics, urine output, and labs for AKI prediction.

Later (25–36h) continued isoflurane measurement may indicate prolonged ventilation/sedation and higher illness complexity. Still a clinician-behavior proxy rather than renal physiology, so only weak-to-modest sparse-model value.

Mean mixed/central venous O2 saturation reflects global oxygen delivery/consumption balance and can relate to perfusion. In 0–12h it is often confounded by bypass/anesthesia and averages dilute insults, so limited sparse-selection value.

Sustained venous O2 saturation in 13–24h can reflect ongoing low cardiac output or impaired oxygen delivery relevant to renal hypoperfusion. Mean is still less sensitive than extremes, but this window is more informative than 0–12h.

High venous O2 saturation is often non-specific (e.g., high FiO2, reduced extraction, sampling issues) and not a strong AKI precursor. Early post-op maxima are particularly noisy and unlikely to be selected sparsely.

Maximum venous O2 saturation is generally less informative for renal risk than low values and can reflect non-pathologic factors. It is unlikely to add unique predictive value compared with minima or other perfusion markers.

Minimum venous O2 saturation captures episodes of impaired oxygen delivery/low cardiac output that can precipitate renal hypoperfusion. Despite early-window noise, the extreme (min) preserves the insult signal better than the mean.

Minimum venous O2 saturation in 13–24h is a strong marker of ongoing low cardiac output/oxygen delivery deficits, a plausible driver of evolving AKI. Among SvO2 summaries in this batch, this extreme in the more informative window is the best sparse-model candidate.

Number of SvO2 measurements is primarily a monitoring-intensity proxy (sicker patients get checked more). It is not a direct physiologic signal and is typically redundant with actual SvO2 values and other severity markers.
SvO2 measurement frequency in 13–24h may weakly indicate clinician concern for low output/shock. Still a proxy variable and generally dominated by the SvO2 minima themselves for sparse selection.

Indicates whether mixed/central venous O2 saturation was checked in 25–36h, mainly reflecting monitoring intensity or hemodynamic concern. As a clinician-behavior proxy (not the value), it is unlikely to add unique predictive signal for Hour-60 creatinine ratio in a sparse model.

Average maintenance IV fluid volume in the immediate 0–12h post-op window, reflecting early resuscitation/maintenance strategy. Early fluids are noisy and this mean is likely redundant with later fluid balance/hemodynamic signals, so low sparse-selection priority.

Average maintenance IV fluid volume during 13–24h, capturing ongoing fluid administration. Still an indirect exposure and a mean that can miss bolus-driven extremes; likely redundant with max or later-window dosing patterns.

Average maintenance IV fluid volume in 25–36h, closer to the Hour-60 outcome and may reflect sustained fluid needs/renal perfusion strategy. Among means, this is the most temporally relevant, but remains indirect and often dominated by extremes or net fluid balance.

Peak maintenance IV fluid volume in 0–12h, potentially capturing early aggressive fluid administration. However, the earliest window is confounded by post-bypass/anesthesia effects and is less reliable for Hour-60 AKI prediction than later peaks.

Peak maintenance IV fluid volume in 13–24h, which may better reflect evolving hemodynamic/renal support needs than a mean. This extreme can add some unique signal, but is still an indirect clinician-driven exposure and may be redundant with later-window peaks.

Peak maintenance IV fluid volume in 25–36h, the closest window to the target and an extreme that can capture ongoing instability or attempts to support perfusion. Within this batch, this is the strongest fluid-administration summary and most likely to survive sparse selection.

Minimum maintenance IV fluid volume in 0–12h, often reflecting brief holds or documentation artifacts rather than a renal insult. Highly redundant and low physiologic specificity for later creatinine rise.

Minimum maintenance IV fluid volume in 13–24h, which is typically less informative than peaks for capturing clinically meaningful exposure. Likely reflects transient pauses and adds little unique predictive value.

Minimum maintenance IV fluid volume in 25–36h may reflect fluid restriction in response to overload/oliguria, but is still ambiguous and clinician-driven. Some temporal relevance, yet generally dominated by max/mean or direct renal/hemodynamic markers.

Count/indicator of maintenance IV volume measurements in 0–12h, primarily a documentation/monitoring proxy. Measurement frequency is not a direct biomarker and is unlikely to be selected in a sparse model.
Measurement frequency for maintenance IV volume in 13–24h, reflecting charting intensity rather than physiology. Low unique value and should be deprioritized versus actual dose summaries.

Measurement frequency for maintenance IV volume in 25–36h, again a proxy for care complexity rather than renal function. Even with better timing, it is unlikely to add signal beyond the actual volume features.

Whether systolic pulmonary artery pressure was measured invasively in 0–12h, indicating PA catheter use/monitoring intensity. This is a clinician-behavior proxy and early post-op timing further limits its unique predictive value for creatinine ratio.

Indicator that systolic PAP was measured in 13–24h, mainly reflecting invasive monitoring and perceived instability. Without the PAP value, it is unlikely to contribute meaningfully in sparse selection.

Indicator that systolic PAP was measured in 25–36h, which may weakly proxy ongoing cardiopulmonary instability closer to the outcome. Still a monitoring proxy (not physiology), so only a modest score.

Indicates whether diastolic pulmonary artery pressure was recorded (monitoring intensity). This is a clinician-behavior proxy with weak direct renal signal and is dominated by actual hemodynamic values if available.
Binary marker that PAP diastolic was measured in the 13–24h window. Mostly reflects illness complexity/monitoring rather than physiology, so low priority for sparse selection.

Binary indicator of PAP diastolic monitoring closer to the outcome window. Slightly more informative as a proxy for ongoing shock/PH/right-heart concerns, but still indirect and typically redundant with actual hemodynamics/vasopressors.
Whether CVP was measured in the immediate post-op period. Primarily a monitoring proxy and early (0–12h) post-bypass noise limits usefulness for predicting 60h creatinine ratio.
Binary marker of CVP monitoring during 13–24h. Adds little unique predictive value beyond other severity indicators and is not a direct renal perfusion/AKI biomarker.

Indicates CVP monitoring in the 25–36h window, which may correlate with persistent hemodynamic instability/volume management affecting kidneys. Still largely a clinician-intensity proxy and inferior to actual CVP values or fluid balance.

Mean end-tidal CO2 early post-op reflects ventilation/perfusion and cardiac output indirectly. Early window and averaging dilute transient low-flow episodes, so it’s a weak-to-moderate sparse-model candidate.

Mean EtCO2 in 13–24h may reflect sustained low cardiac output or ventilation changes that relate to renal hypoperfusion. However, mean is less sensitive than minima and is often confounded by ventilator management.

Mean EtCO2 closest to the outcome can capture ongoing low-flow physiology that may precede AKI. Still indirect and potentially redundant with EtCO2 minima/maxima and hemodynamic/vasopressor features, so moderate priority.
Peak EtCO2 in 0–12h is usually driven by ventilator settings/hypoventilation rather than renal-relevant perfusion. Early timing and weak mechanistic link make it low value for sparse selection.
Maximum EtCO2 in 13–24h more often reflects hypoventilation or reduced minute ventilation than kidney risk. Provides little unique AKI signal compared with EtCO2 minima or direct perfusion markers.

Maximum EtCO2 in 25–36h could reflect hypoventilation/CO2 retention and severity, but remains an indirect and management-sensitive signal. Lower priority than EtCO2 minima for capturing low-perfusion risk.

Minimum EtCO2 early can indicate transient low pulmonary blood flow/low cardiac output or major V/Q issues, which can precede renal hypoperfusion. Despite early noise, the extreme (min) preserves the most relevant signal within EtCO2 features.

Minimum EtCO2 in 13–24h is a stronger marker of evolving low-flow physiology than means/maxima and is less dominated by immediate post-bypass artifacts. High sparse-model priority as an indirect but sensitive perfusion surrogate.

Minimum EtCO2 in 25–36h is the closest-window extreme and can sensitively capture ongoing low cardiac output/pulmonary blood flow reductions that threaten renal perfusion. Among this batch it is the most informative, least redundant EtCO2 aggregation for predicting 60h creatinine ratio.

Measurement frequency/availability of EtCO2 in 0–12h mainly reflects monitoring and ventilation status rather than physiology. As a proxy variable it is dominated by actual EtCO2 values and has low sparse-model value.

Count/frequency of end-tidal CO2 documentation in hours 13–24, mainly reflecting monitoring intensity and ventilation status. It is a clinician-behavior proxy with weak, indirect linkage to AKI and unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Frequency of ETCO2 measurements in hours 25–36, reflecting ongoing intubation/monitoring rather than renal physiology. Even though closer to Hour 60, it remains a low-specificity proxy and is dominated by direct hemodynamic/renal markers (not present in this batch).

Average FiO2 requirement in the immediate post-op window, a marker of respiratory dysfunction/oxygenation support. It is indirect for kidney injury and early post-bypass noise plus redundancy with FiO2 extremes makes it a weak sparse-model candidate.

Peak FiO2 in hours 0–12 captures transient severe oxygenation/instability. Extremes are preferable to means, but this is still an early, nonspecific severity signal with limited unique value for predicting creatinine ratio.

Frequency of FiO2 recordings in hours 0–12 is primarily a documentation/monitoring proxy. It adds little physiologic information and is unlikely to be selected in a sparse linear model.

Measurement frequency of FiO2 in hours 13–24 reflects care intensity and ventilator management rather than kidney perfusion or injury. Low specificity and high proxy nature make it a poor sparse-selection feature.

Mean tidal volume in hours 0–12 reflects ventilator strategy and patient size/ventilation needs. It is an indirect severity/management marker and early timing plus redundancy across tidal-volume aggregates limits sparse-model value.

Mean tidal volume in hours 25–36 is closer to the Hour 60 outcome and may capture sustained ventilatory support/illness trajectory. Among tidal-volume summaries, this timing makes it the most plausible keeper, though still indirect for renal injury.

Maximum tidal volume in hours 0–12 may indicate transient high ventilatory demand or non-protective settings. It is nonspecific for kidney outcomes and early post-op variability reduces its unique predictive value.

Maximum tidal volume in hours 25–36 may reflect episodes of worsening respiratory mechanics or aggressive ventilation closer to the creatinine outcome. It can add some unique trajectory information beyond the mean, but remains indirect for AKI.

Minimum tidal volume in hours 13–24 is typically driven by transient clinician adjustments rather than organ dysfunction. It is low-specificity and unlikely to contribute uniquely in a sparse model.

Minimum tidal volume in the 25–36h window reflects ventilator strategy/respiratory mechanics rather than a direct renal signal. It may weakly proxy severity or lung-protective ventilation but is unlikely to survive sparse selection for creatinine_ratio.

Heart rate being measured at 13–24h is a low-information monitoring indicator. It is unlikely to add unique predictive signal compared with actual HR extremes/means (not provided here).

MAP measurement presence at 13–24h is an indirect proxy for acuity and clinician attention. It is redundant with actual MAP summaries and unlikely to be selected in a sparse linear model.

MAP measurement at 25–36h may indicate ongoing hemodynamic monitoring closer to the outcome and could weakly correlate with instability. Still a proxy variable and inferior to actual MAP minima/vasopressor dose features.

Whether pulse oximetry was measured during 13–24h post-op. Still largely a clinician/monitoring proxy and not a direct precursor of AKI compared with hemodynamics/renal markers.

Amount/recording of tube (enteral) intake early post-op, reflecting nutrition/feeding tolerance and care patterns. It is an indirect severity/trajectory marker and likely redundant with stronger fluid balance/renal perfusion signals.

Tube intake during 13–24h post-op, reflecting recovery and ability to tolerate feeds. Some weak association with overall illness severity, but limited direct mechanistic link to creatinine rise and low sparse-model priority.

Tube intake during 25–36h post-op, a later marker of recovery vs ongoing critical illness. Closer timing helps slightly, but it remains an indirect proxy and unlikely to add unique predictive value for AKI.

Average maintenance/bolus IV volume in the first 12h, reflecting early resuscitation and fluid exposure. Mean blunts short high-volume resuscitation episodes and early post-bypass fluid shifts add noise, so it is a weak sparse candidate.

Pacemaker status in 13–24h may capture ongoing conduction/low-output physiology. Still indirect for kidney injury and generally redundant with the closer 25–36h pacemaker feature.

Pacemaker status in 25–36h reflects persistent need for pacing near the prediction horizon, which can correlate with renal hypoperfusion risk. Useful as a sparse-model candidate within this family, though still a clinician/physiology proxy rather than direct renal dysfunction.

Measurement frequency of pacemaker-related documentation in 0–12h is mainly a monitoring/complexity proxy. Low direct physiologic content and early post-op timing makes it unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Pacemaker measurement frequency in 25–36h may weakly proxy ongoing instability/ICU intensity closer to Hour 60. Still primarily a behavior/monitoring signal and inferior to direct pacemaker use (min/max) for sparse modeling.

Sevoflurane measurement frequency at 25–36h is primarily a clinician-behavior/ventilation-management proxy. Minimal direct linkage to renal injury and unlikely to survive sparse feature selection.

How often IV intake was recorded in 13–24h is mainly monitoring intensity and care complexity. It provides little unique physiologic information for predicting creatinine ratio compared with true fluid balance or renal-support indicators.

Count/indicator of IV intake documentation in 25–36h, mainly reflecting monitoring intensity and care complexity. It is a clinician-behavior proxy and offers little unique physiologic signal for AKI compared with actual fluid balance/urine output variables.

Measurement frequency/availability of right-sided drain output in the immediate 0–12h post-op window. This is largely a documentation/monitoring proxy and early post-op noise, with weak direct linkage to Hour-60 creatinine trajectory.

Measurement frequency/availability of right drain output during 13–24h. Still primarily a monitoring proxy and not a direct renal perfusion or kidney function marker, so unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Measurement frequency/availability of right drain output in 25–36h, closer to the outcome and may weakly proxy ongoing bleeding/instability. However it remains indirect and dominated by more direct hemodynamic/renal features in a sparse model.

Indicator that a right-sided arrival-time field was recorded in 0–12h. Pure workflow/documentation signal with no direct renal physiology content and high redundancy with general acuity/ICU process variables.
Indicator that a right-sided arrival-time field was recorded in 13–24h. This is a process/EMR artifact rather than a physiologic predictor, so very low sparse-model priority.

Indicator that a right-sided arrival-time field was recorded in 25–36h. Despite being temporally closer, it remains a documentation proxy and is unlikely to add unique predictive value for creatinine_ratio.

Proportion of time in normal sinus rhythm during 0–12h, reflecting early rhythm stability. Rhythm can relate to cardiac output/renal perfusion, but this early mean is indirect and likely redundant with stronger hemodynamic/vasopressor signals.

Proportion of time in normal sinus rhythm during 13–24h, a marker of ongoing rhythm stability. Still an indirect proxy for perfusion and typically dominated by direct BP/CO/vasopressor and lactate/urine output features in sparse selection.

Proportion of time in normal sinus rhythm during 25–36h, closest window and may capture sustained atrial arrhythmia burden affecting renal perfusion. Among rhythm summaries, this mean is the most temporally relevant and could add modest unique value if hemodynamic variables are limited.

Whether normal sinus rhythm occurred at any point in 0–12h (max), which is a very low-information binary in a noisy immediate post-op period. It is dominated by more informative rhythm burden measures (mean) and later epochs.

Whether normal sinus rhythm occurred at any point in 13–24h. This max/binary is coarse and largely redundant with the corresponding mean rhythm burden, offering little incremental value for sparse modeling.
Whether normal sinus rhythm occurred at any point in 25–36h. Even though timing is favorable, the max is a weak binary that is typically redundant with the 25–36h mean rhythm burden.

Whether the patient was continuously in normal sinus rhythm throughout 0–12h (min), effectively flagging any arrhythmia early. It is more informative than max but still early and indirect for AKI prediction.

Whether the patient remained in normal sinus rhythm for the entire 13–24h window, capturing any arrhythmia occurrence. Useful as a simple arrhythmia flag but still indirect and often redundant with hemodynamic/vasopressor variables.

Whether the patient stayed in normal sinus rhythm throughout 25–36h, capturing persistent arrhythmia burden close to the outcome. This can proxy sustained perfusion compromise and is the strongest of the min/max rhythm indicators, though still secondary to direct renal/hemodynamic markers.

Count/frequency of documentation of normal sinus rhythm in the first 12h. This is mainly a monitoring/EMR density proxy and early post-op rhythm documentation is noisy, so it adds little unique value for creatinine_ratio.
Frequency of normal sinus rhythm charting in hours 13–24. Still largely reflects documentation intensity rather than physiology and is unlikely to survive sparse selection for AKI prediction.
Frequency of normal sinus rhythm charting in hours 25–36. Despite being closer to Hour 60, it remains a clinician-behavior/monitoring proxy with minimal direct renal signal.

Average burden of sinus rhythm variants (e.g., sinus brady/tachy/arrhythmia variants) in the first 12h. Early post-op rhythm variability is common and indirect for kidney injury, and the mean smooths transient events.

Average burden of sinus rhythm variants in hours 13–24. Could weakly reflect hemodynamic instability but is indirect and likely dominated by more direct perfusion/vasopressor/urine output features in a sparse model.

Average burden of sinus rhythm variants in hours 25–36. Timing is favorable, but mean rhythm-variant burden is still a nonspecific severity marker and usually redundant with stronger hemodynamic predictors.
Whether any sinus rhythm variant occurred (max indicator) in the first 12h. Very common early after surgery and not specific for renal hypoperfusion, so low sparse-model priority.
Whether any sinus rhythm variant occurred in hours 13–24. Presence may correlate with instability but is a coarse, indirect signal and likely redundant with direct hemodynamic/vasopressor variables.

Whether any sinus rhythm variant occurred in hours 25–36. Closer to outcome and an extreme/occurrence flag, but still nonspecific and unlikely to add unique predictive value versus direct renal/perfusion features.

Minimum indicator for sinus rhythm variant in the first 12h (typically reflects absence/persistence depending on encoding). This is hard to interpret clinically and is dominated by clearer occurrence/burden measures, so low value.
Minimum indicator for sinus rhythm variant in hours 13–24. Ambiguous encoding and redundant with max/mean rhythm-variant features, with little direct renal relevance.
Minimum indicator for sinus rhythm variant in hours 25–36. Even with better timing, it remains an ambiguous and redundant rhythm proxy unlikely to survive sparse selection.
Frequency of documentation/measurement of sinus rhythm variants in the first 12h. Primarily reflects monitoring intensity and early post-op charting practices rather than kidney-risk physiology.
Frequency of documentation/measurement of sinus rhythm variants in hours 13–24. This is a clinician-behavior proxy and offers little unique information for predicting creatinine_ratio.
Frequency of documentation/measurement of sinus rhythm variants in hours 25–36. Despite proximity to Hour 60, it remains a monitoring proxy and is not a direct or strong precursor of AKI.

Average burden of atrial arrhythmia (e.g., AF/flutter) in the first 12h, which can reduce cardiac output and renal perfusion. It is still indirect and early/noisy, but more specific than sinus-variant features and may add some unique signal if hemodynamic variables are limited.

Represents proportion of time in atrial arrhythmia during 13–24h post-op. It is an indirect hemodynamic/illness-severity signal and is likely redundant with a simpler presence/absence indicator in the closer 25–36h window.

Represents burden of atrial arrhythmia in the 25–36h window, which can worsen renal perfusion via reduced cardiac output. Closer timing helps, but mean burden is still an indirect proxy and often dominated by hypotension/vasopressor/urine-output features (not in this batch).

Indicates any atrial arrhythmia occurred in the immediate 0–12h post-op period. Early post-bypass rhythm instability is common/transient, so this is a weaker and noisier predictor for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Indicates presence of atrial arrhythmia at any point in 13–24h post-op. This can reflect evolving hemodynamic compromise and may add some unique signal, but remains indirect for AKI and is partly redundant with the 25–36h version.

Indicates any atrial arrhythmia occurred in the closest available window (25–36h), which can plausibly precede worsening renal function by Hour 60. Among the atrial-arrhythmia family, this binary extreme is the most sparse-model-friendly and least diluted by averaging.

Binary minimum for atrial arrhythmia over 0–12h is typically uninformative (often just reflects whether arrhythmia was continuously present). It is redundant with max/mean and especially noisy in the immediate post-op window.

Binary minimum for atrial arrhythmia over 13–24h mainly captures continuous presence rather than clinically meaningful extremes. It is dominated by the corresponding max (any occurrence) and mean (burden) for sparse selection.

Binary minimum for atrial arrhythmia over 25–36h is a poor summary (continuous arrhythmia) and is rarely the key renal-relevant signal. It is redundant with the 25–36h max/mean and unlikely to survive sparse screening.
Counts/reflects frequency of rhythm documentation/measurement in 0–12h. This is primarily a monitoring-intensity/clinician-behavior proxy and not a direct physiologic predictor of AKI.
Measurement frequency of atrial arrhythmia in 13–24h is mostly a proxy for attention/acuity rather than renal pathophysiology. It is unlikely to add unique value in a sparse linear model.

Measurement frequency in 25–36h reflects monitoring intensity and documentation patterns. Even though closer in time, it remains a weak proxy and is dominated by actual arrhythmia presence/burden features.

Represents burden of paced rhythm in 0–12h, often reflecting post-op conduction issues and temporary pacing use. It is an indirect severity/procedure signal and early pacing is common, limiting unique predictive value for Hour-60 creatinine.

Represents paced rhythm burden in 13–24h, which may correlate with low-output states or conduction disease. Still largely a proxy and likely redundant with a simpler paced-rhythm presence indicator in the later window.

Represents sustained pacing burden in the 25–36h window, potentially signaling persistent conduction/low-output physiology relevant to renal perfusion. Closer timing improves usefulness, but it remains indirect and may be redundant with paced-rhythm max.

Indicates any paced rhythm occurred in 0–12h, which is common immediately post-cardiac surgery. As an early, non-specific management marker, it is a weaker sparse-model candidate for predicting Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Indicates any paced rhythm occurred in 13–24h, which may reflect ongoing conduction problems or hemodynamic support needs. More informative than 0–12h, but still an indirect proxy and would compete with a 25–36h paced indicator if available.

Indicates any paced rhythm during the 25–36h window, reflecting conduction disease/low-output support needs. It is an indirect proxy for hemodynamic instability and is likely dominated by more direct perfusion/renal markers, so low sparse-model priority.
Captures whether paced rhythm was present throughout the early 0–12h period. Early post-op pacing is common/transient and adds little unique signal for Hour-60 creatinine beyond later windows.

Represents sustained pacing across 13–24h, suggesting persistent conduction issues or ongoing support. Somewhat more informative than 0–12h but still an indirect severity proxy and redundant with the closer 25–36h pacing indicators.

Sustained pacing in the closest (25–36h) window may reflect persistent low-output state that can contribute to renal hypoperfusion. Among pacing features this is the most temporally relevant and least redundant, but remains indirect for AKI prediction.
Measurement frequency of paced rhythm in 0–12h is mainly a monitoring/EMR documentation intensity proxy. It is not a physiologic renal signal and is unlikely to survive sparse selection.
Counts how often paced rhythm status was recorded in 13–24h, reflecting clinician attention rather than kidney physiology. Redundant as a proxy and weak for predicting creatinine trajectory.

Recording frequency of paced rhythm in 25–36h is a documentation/acuity proxy rather than a direct biomarker. Even with better timing, it is low-value compared with actual rhythm/hemodynamic extremes.

Average heart block burden in the immediate 0–12h post-op period, when transient conduction abnormalities are common. Mean aggregation and early timing make it noisy and redundant with later/max indicators.

Average heart block burden in 13–24h may reflect persistent conduction disease and potential low cardiac output. Still indirect for renal injury and mean is typically less informative than an extreme (any heart block) in sparse selection.

Average heart block burden in 25–36h is the closest window and could correlate with ongoing hemodynamic compromise. However, it is likely dominated by the simpler extreme indicator (max) and by direct perfusion/renal features.

Any heart block occurrence in 0–12h can signal conduction complications that may affect cardiac output. Early timing limits reliability for Hour-60 creatinine, so it is a weak-to-moderate sparse candidate.

Any heart block in 13–24h suggests persistent conduction disturbance with potential impact on renal perfusion. This extreme is more informative than mean/min and may add some unique severity signal, though still indirect.

Any heart block in 25–36h is a near-term marker of ongoing conduction-related instability that can contribute to low cardiac output and renal hypoperfusion. Best-timed and most informative aggregation within the heart-block family, making it the strongest sparse-selection candidate here.
Heart block present throughout 0–12h is uncommon and may be inconsistently documented; early post-op period is noisy. Adds little beyond the max indicator and later windows.

Sustained heart block across 13–24h could indicate significant conduction disease requiring intervention. However, it is often redundant with the simpler 'any heart block' (max) and remains an indirect renal predictor.

Sustained heart block in 25–36h implies persistent conduction compromise close to the prediction horizon. Potentially adds incremental information beyond 'any heart block,' but likely correlated with pacing and other instability markers, limiting sparse-model priority.
Count/frequency of documented heart block in the first 12h post-op. Mostly a monitoring/documentation proxy and early post-op conduction issues are common; weak and indirect for AKI prediction.
Frequency of heart block documentation/events in hours 13–24. Persistent conduction disease may correlate with low output/need for pacing, but remains indirect and largely a care-intensity proxy.

Frequency of heart block documentation/events in hours 25–36. Closer to the creatinine outcome and may reflect ongoing low cardiac output physiology, but still indirect and unlikely to survive sparse selection versus direct hemodynamic/renal features.

Average presence of ischemic ST changes in the first 12h. Myocardial ischemia can contribute to low output and renal hypoperfusion, but early post-op ECG noise and indirectness limit sparse-model value.
Average presence of ischemic ST changes in hours 13–24. Some signal for ongoing ischemia/low output, but the mean smooths short insults and is redundant with a max indicator.

Average presence of ischemic ST changes in hours 25–36. Later sustained ischemia may better track impending renal hypoperfusion, but mean is still less informative than an extreme/any-occurrence metric.
Whether any ischemic ST change occurred (max) in the first 12h. Extremes are preferable to means, but early post-op artifacts/transient changes reduce reliability for Hour-60 creatinine.
Whether any ischemic ST change occurred (max) in hours 13–24. Captures episodic ischemia better than the mean and may add some unique low-output risk signal, though still indirect for AKI.

Whether any ischemic ST change occurred (max) in hours 25–36. Closest window and an extreme/any-occurrence metric best preserves clinically meaningful ischemia that can drive renal hypoperfusion, making it the strongest in this ST-change family.

Minimum of a binary ischemic ST-change indicator in the first 12h (typically uninformative since min often reflects any period without ST change). Dominated by max/mean and adds little unique predictive value.
Minimum of a binary ischemic ST-change indicator in hours 13–24. For binary signals, min is generally non-informative and redundant with max/mean, so low sparse-selection priority.
Minimum of a binary ischemic ST-change indicator in hours 25–36. Even though later timing is better, the min aggregation is still dominated by max and unlikely to contribute.
Number of times ischemic ST change was assessed/documented in the first 12h. Primarily reflects monitoring intensity/clinical concern rather than physiology; weak and noisy for AKI prediction.
Measurement/documentation frequency of ischemic ST change in hours 13–24. Mostly a clinician-behavior proxy and redundant with actual ST-change presence variables.

Measurement/documentation frequency of ischemic ST change in hours 25–36. Later increased monitoring may correlate with instability, but remains an indirect proxy and inferior to the max ST-change indicator.
Average burden of 'other' rhythm category in the first 12h (heterogeneous arrhythmias). Poorly specific, likely noisy and redundant with more defined rhythm/hemodynamic features, so low priority.

Represents proportion of time in a non-standard/other cardiac rhythm during 13–24h. Rhythm category is an indirect hemodynamic/illness marker and the mean is likely redundant with simpler presence/absence indicators, so low sparse-model priority.

Proportion of time in an other rhythm during 25–36h, closer to the Hour-60 creatinine outcome. Still indirect for AKI but the later window may capture persistent instability; keep only as a modest candidate if rhythm adds unique signal beyond BP/pressors (not shown here).

Binary indicator that an other rhythm occurred at any point in 0–12h. Early post-op rhythm disturbances are common/transient and are a weak, nonspecific proxy for renal risk, dominated by later-window rhythm if used.

Binary indicator of any other rhythm in 13–24h. Some association with hemodynamic stress is plausible, but it remains indirect and likely redundant with the 25–36h indicator/mean for sparse selection.

Binary indicator that an other rhythm occurred in 25–36h. Later occurrence may reflect ongoing instability that can contribute to renal hypoperfusion, but it is still a coarse, indirect signal so not top-tier.

Binary/min-style encoding for other rhythm in 0–12h (effectively another presence/absence variant). Redundant with the corresponding max/mean and early timing makes it unlikely to survive sparse screening.
Binary/min-style encoding for other rhythm in 13–24h. Adds little beyond the max/mean versions and is not a direct renal physiology marker, so very low incremental value.
Binary/min-style encoding for other rhythm in 25–36h. Later timing helps, but it is still redundant with the 25–36h max/mean representations and unlikely to be selected in a sparse model.

Count/frequency of rhythm documentation in 0–12h, mainly reflecting monitoring intensity/workflow. This is a clinician-behavior proxy and early post-op monitoring is routine, so minimal unique predictive value.
Measurement frequency of the other-rhythm field in 13–24h. Primarily a proxy for attention/complexity rather than physiology, and unlikely to add value beyond actual rhythm occurrence variables.

Measurement frequency of the other-rhythm field in 25–36h. Could weakly proxy ongoing instability/ICU complexity closer to outcome, but still indirect and generally dominated by physiologic variables.
Average use/intensity of ASV ventilator mode in 0–12h. Vent mode choice is largely practice/patient-management driven and early post-op ventilation is common, so it is a weak proxy for renal outcome.

Average ASV mode use in 13–24h, reflecting ongoing ventilatory support strategy. May weakly correlate with illness severity and hemodynamics, but is indirect and likely redundant with more direct respiratory/hemodynamic measures.

Binary indicator that ASV was used at any time in 0–12h. Mostly reflects ventilator management preference and routine immediate post-op care, offering little unique signal for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
Binary indicator of any ASV use in 13–24h. Slightly more informative than 0–12h as it may indicate delayed weaning/complexity, but remains an indirect clinician-behavior proxy.
Binary/min-style encoding for ASV use in 0–12h (another redundant presence/absence variant). Adds no incremental information beyond the max/mean and is unlikely to be selected in a sparse model.
Indicates whether ASV mode was used at any point in 13–24h. Vent mode choice is an indirect severity/management proxy and is unlikely to add unique renal predictive value in a sparse model.
Count/frequency of ASV documentation in the first 12h post-op. This is primarily a monitoring/documentation intensity proxy and early post-op ventilation patterns are noisy for Hour-60 creatinine.
Frequency of ASV documentation in 13–24h. Still largely clinician-behavior/vent-management proxy with weak, non-specific linkage to AKI compared with hemodynamics/renal markers.

Frequency of ASV documentation in 25–36h, closer to the outcome window. Timing is better, but it remains a proxy for respiratory course/management rather than a direct renal signal, so low sparse-selection priority.
Average use of controlled ventilation in 0–12h. Early post-op ventilation mode is heavily protocol/anesthesia influenced and provides little unique information for later creatinine ratio.

Average use of controlled ventilation in 13–24h, reflecting ongoing need for full ventilatory support. This can weakly proxy overall severity, but is indirect and likely dominated by more specific shock/renal features.
Whether controlled mode occurred at any time in 0–12h. This is common immediately post-op and thus low-discrimination and redundant with other ventilation indicators.

Whether controlled mode occurred at any time in 13–24h, suggesting failure to wean and higher illness severity. Still indirect for AKI and likely redundant with other severity/hemodynamic variables in a sparse model.
Whether controlled mode was continuously present (or lowest value) in 0–12h. Early ventilation mode is expected post-cardiac surgery and offers minimal unique renal prognostic signal.

Minimum/continuous presence of controlled mode in 13–24h, implying sustained full support. Some severity signal, but still a non-specific proxy and unlikely to survive sparse selection over direct perfusion/renal markers.
Frequency of controlled-mode documentation in 0–12h. Primarily reflects charting/monitoring and routine immediate post-op care rather than kidney injury risk.
Frequency of controlled-mode documentation in 13–24h. Measurement frequency is a weak clinician-behavior proxy and adds little unique predictive value beyond any binary/summary ventilation indicator.

Frequency of controlled-mode documentation in 25–36h, closer to Hour 60 and may reflect prolonged critical illness. Still largely a documentation/management proxy and not a direct renal physiology marker.
Average SIMV use in 0–12h. Early ventilator mode selection is protocolized and noisy, with weak and indirect association to later creatinine changes.
Average SIMV use in 13–24h, potentially reflecting partial weaning status. This is an indirect severity/weaning proxy and likely redundant with stronger physiologic predictors of AKI.
Whether SIMV occurred at any time in 0–12h. Common early post-op and mainly reflects ventilator management rather than unique information about evolving renal dysfunction.

Represents whether SIMV ventilation was used at any point in 13–24h, a proxy for ongoing respiratory support/sedation. Indirect for AKI and largely redundant with other ventilation-mode indicators, so low sparse-model priority.

Indicates whether SIMV was continuously present in the immediate 0–12h window. Early post-op ventilator mode is noisy and mainly reflects routine management rather than renal physiology, so very weak unique value.

Captures sustained SIMV use across 13–24h, reflecting delayed weaning/illness severity. Still an indirect clinician-management proxy and likely dominated by hemodynamics/renal markers, so low priority.
Count/frequency of SIMV mode documentation in 0–12h is primarily monitoring/EMR density. This is a clinician-behavior proxy with high redundancy and minimal direct renal signal.
Measurement frequency of SIMV in 13–24h reflects documentation intensity rather than physiology. Unlikely to survive sparse selection given weak, indirect association with creatinine trajectory.
Later (25–36h) SIMV documentation frequency may weakly proxy persistent critical illness closer to the outcome time. Still mostly a monitoring/management artifact, so only slight value.
Represents proportion/average presence of spontaneous mode in 0–12h, largely reflecting early extubation practices. Early window and indirectness make it a poor sparse-model candidate.
Average spontaneous mode presence in 13–24h reflects weaning progress and overall recovery. It is still an indirect severity proxy and redundant with max/min indicators, so low-moderate priority.

Whether spontaneous mode occurred at any time in 0–12h mainly captures immediate post-op extubation attempts. This is noisy and practice-driven, offering little unique prediction of Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
Any spontaneous mode in 13–24h suggests successful weaning and less severe illness. Indirect for renal injury and likely redundant with other ventilation variables, so limited sparse-model value.
Continuous spontaneous mode in 0–12h is uncommon and heavily influenced by immediate post-op protocols. Early timing and management confounding make it weak for predicting later creatinine rise.

Sustained spontaneous mode in 13–24h indicates stable respiratory status and potentially lower systemic stress. Still a downstream recovery marker rather than a direct renal perfusion/AKI signal, so low priority.
Frequency of documenting spontaneous mode in 0–12h is a monitoring/recording proxy. It adds little physiologic information and is dominated by actual hemodynamic/renal variables.
Documentation frequency for spontaneous mode in 13–24h reflects charting intensity and care patterns. Weak, indirect, and redundant—unlikely to be selected in a sparse model.
Later documentation frequency (25–36h) may weakly track ongoing ICU complexity near the outcome horizon. Still primarily a proxy variable, so only modest sparse-selection priority.

Mean arterial pressure in the first 12h reflects systemic perfusion pressure, a key driver of renal hypoperfusion and AKI risk after cardiac surgery. Although early and averaged (less sensitive than minima), it is a direct physiologic signal and likely more informative than ventilator-mode proxies in this batch.
Average MAP over 13–24h reflects overall perfusion but smooths over short hypotensive insults most relevant to AKI. Likely redundant with MAP minima (and later-window summaries) for sparse selection.

Average MAP over 25–36h captures sustained perfusion closer to the Hour-60 creatinine outcome. Still less sensitive than MAP minima for renal hypoperfusion, but may add some unique signal if hypotension is prolonged.

Peak MAP in the immediate 0–12h post-op period is usually driven by transient vasopressor/physiologic swings and is not a direct renal-risk signal. Dominated by hypotension-focused features (MAP minima) and later epochs.
Peak MAP over 13–24h mainly reflects treatment intensity or episodic hypertension rather than kidney hypoperfusion. Provides little incremental value compared with MAP minima/mean in a sparse model.
Peak MAP over 25–36h is not strongly mechanistically linked to AKI compared with low-perfusion metrics. Likely redundant/noisy and unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Minimum MAP in 0–12h captures early hypotensive episodes that can contribute to renal ischemia. However, immediate post-bypass/anesthesia effects make this window noisier and less predictive than later minima.

Minimum MAP in 13–24h captures clinically meaningful hypotension after initial post-op stabilization, a strong precursor of AKI. High-value extreme aggregation with less noise than 0–12h and less redundancy than means/max.

Minimum MAP in 25–36h is the closest hypotension signal to the Hour-60 creatinine ratio and directly reflects renal hypoperfusion risk. This extreme, late-window feature is a top sparse-model keeper among MAP summaries.
Count/frequency of MAP measurements is primarily a monitoring-intensity/acuity proxy rather than physiology. In 0–12h it is near-universal and adds little unique predictive value.
Measurement frequency reflects clinician behavior and device availability more than renal pathophysiology. Likely redundant with other severity indicators and weak for sparse selection.
MAP measurement frequency in 25–36h is still mainly a proxy for ongoing ICU intensity rather than a direct kidney-risk marker. Unlikely to add incremental value beyond actual MAP levels/extremes.
Proportion of time NPO in 0–12h is routine post-cardiac surgery care and not a direct renal signal. Mostly non-informative and dominated by hemodynamics/renal markers.
NPO status in 13–24h largely reflects standard post-op progression and practice patterns. Weak, indirect proxy with minimal expected incremental predictive value for creatinine ratio.

Persistently NPO by 25–36h may indicate delayed recovery/ongoing critical illness, which can correlate with AKI risk. Still an indirect clinician-practice proxy and likely dominated by direct perfusion/renal features.
Any NPO in 0–12h is nearly universal after surgery and provides little discrimination. Redundant with NPO mean and not useful for sparse selection.
Any NPO in 13–24h is common and mostly reflects routine care rather than kidney injury risk. Adds little beyond the more informative late-window NPO persistence (mean) and physiologic variables.

Whether the patient was NPO at any point in the 25–36h window, reflecting feeding status/clinical trajectory. This is an indirect care-process proxy (often correlates with illness severity or ongoing intubation) and is unlikely to add unique AKI signal in a sparse model.

Whether the patient was NPO throughout the 0–12h window (min implies persistent NPO). Early post-op feeding status is near-universal and noisy, offering little unique predictive value for 60h creatinine ratio.

Persistent NPO during 13–24h may indicate delayed recovery or ongoing critical illness. Still largely a clinician-behavior/trajectory proxy and typically dominated by more direct hemodynamic/renal features.

Persistent NPO in the closest window (25–36h) can reflect ongoing instability or inability to take PO. Timing is favorable, but it remains indirect and redundant with other severity markers, so low sparse-selection priority.
Frequency/availability of NPO documentation in 0–12h is mainly a monitoring/documentation intensity proxy. Not a physiologic renal signal and unlikely to survive sparse feature selection.
Measurement count for NPO status in 13–24h reflects charting intensity rather than kidney physiology. Adds minimal unique information for predicting creatinine ratio.
Measurement count for NPO status in 25–36h is a documentation/attention proxy. Even with closer timing, it is not a direct AKI precursor and is weak for sparse modeling.
Average coded diet status in 0–12h reflects early post-op feeding orders. This is largely routine and noisy immediately after surgery, with little unique value for later creatinine rise.
Average diet status in 13–24h may capture progression toward PO intake vs continued restriction. Still indirect and likely redundant with stronger indicators of recovery/organ dysfunction.

Average diet status in 25–36h is closer to the outcome and may reflect recovery trajectory. However it remains a care-process proxy and is unlikely to add unique renal predictive signal versus direct labs/hemodynamics.
Whether any diet (non-NPO) occurred in 0–12h is uncommon and heavily influenced by immediate post-op protocols. Early window and indirect nature make it a poor sparse-model candidate.
Any diet exposure in 13–24h suggests earlier recovery and less severe course. Useful only as a weak trajectory proxy and typically dominated by direct renal/perfusion variables.
Any diet exposure in 25–36h indicates ability to tolerate intake and clinical stability. Closer timing helps, but it is still indirect and redundant with other severity/recovery signals.
Diet present throughout 0–12h (min) is rare and protocol-driven early after cardiac surgery. Low prevalence and early timing limit its unique predictive value for 60h creatinine ratio.
Diet present throughout 13–24h reflects sustained PO tolerance and recovery. Still an indirect proxy and unlikely to be selected over direct kidney function and perfusion markers.
Diet present throughout 25–36h suggests sustained stability and recovery in the closest window. Despite favorable timing, it remains a non-physiologic proxy and offers limited unique AKI prediction.
Count/frequency of diet-type documentation in the first 12h is mainly a monitoring/workflow proxy. It has no direct renal physiology signal and is unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Diet documentation frequency in 13–24h reflects care processes rather than kidney injury. Any association with AKI risk would be indirect and dominated by hemodynamics/fluids/labs not present in this batch.

Later (25–36h) diet documentation frequency may weakly proxy recovery vs ongoing critical illness (e.g., still NPO), which can correlate with AKI trajectory. Still largely clinician-behavior/feeding-practice driven and low priority for a sparse model.

Mean proportion/time classified as an 'other' diet type in 0–12h is a nonspecific nutrition-order category. It is unlikely to add unique predictive value for creatinine ratio beyond general severity proxies.
Average presence of an 'other' diet type in 13–24h is not a direct marker of renal hypoperfusion or evolving AKI. It is also redundant with the corresponding max/min indicators if any signal exists.

In 25–36h, sustained 'other' diet status could weakly reflect delayed GI recovery/ongoing instability, indirectly correlating with AKI risk. However it remains nonspecific and likely redundant with a simpler binary indicator (max) if kept.
Whether 'other' diet occurred at all in the first 12h is mostly driven by immediate post-op NPO/ordering practices. Early timing is noisy and provides little unique renal prediction.

Any occurrence of an 'other' diet type in 13–24h may weakly proxy delayed progression of diet/clinical course. Still indirect and low-yield compared with true renal/hemodynamic features, so low sparse-selection priority.

Any 'other' diet status in 25–36h is the closest window and may capture failure to advance diet (proxy for ongoing critical illness), which can track with AKI trajectory. Among the diet-type variables, this binary extreme is the most plausible keeper, though still indirect.
The minimum of a binary 'other diet' indicator is typically uninformative (often 0 unless always present). It adds no renal-specific signal and is dominated by the max indicator.
Binary min for 'other diet' in 13–24h is largely redundant and usually reflects absence at some point rather than meaningful exposure. It is dominated by max/mean and unlikely to help a sparse model.
Binary min in 25–36h is not a useful extreme for exposure (it mainly indicates the diet was not 'other' at least once). Redundant with max/mean and not a direct AKI predictor.
Measurement/documentation count for 'other diet' in 0–12h is a workflow proxy with high post-op noise. It is unlikely to contribute unique predictive information for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
Documentation frequency of 'other diet' in 13–24h reflects charting intensity rather than physiology. Any correlation with AKI would be indirect and not sparse-model competitive.

In 25–36h, more frequent diet documentation could weakly indicate active management/instability, indirectly related to AKI risk. Still primarily a clinician-behavior proxy and lower priority than the simple occurrence (max) variable.

Average regular cardiac rhythm in 0–12h may reflect arrhythmias (e.g., AF) that can reduce renal perfusion and increase AKI risk. However it is early and indirect, and would be dominated by more direct hemodynamic/vasopressor/urine output signals in a sparse model.

Represents the proportion of time/rhythm assessments showing a regular rhythm in hours 13–24. Cardiac rhythm regularity is an indirect hemodynamic proxy and is largely redundant with the corresponding irregular indicators and closer (25–36h) window.

Represents the proportion of time/rhythm assessments showing a regular rhythm in hours 25–36, closest to the creatinine outcome window. May add modest unique value as a marker of ongoing arrhythmia-related instability, but remains indirect for AKI compared with direct perfusion/renal markers.

Binary indicator that the patient was ever documented as regular rhythm in hours 0–12. Early post-op rhythm documentation is noisy and this 'ever regular' max is not discriminative (most patients will be regular at least once), so low sparse-model value.

Binary indicator that the patient was ever documented as regular rhythm in hours 13–24. This max aggregation is typically near-constant and dominated by more informative measures of irregularity burden or later-window summaries.

Binary indicator that the patient was ever documented as regular rhythm in hours 25–36. Still likely near-constant and provides little incremental information beyond irregular rhythm features or mean burden measures.

Binary indicator that rhythm was regular for the entire 0–12h window (i.e., never irregular). Could weakly reflect early atrial fibrillation/ectopy burden, but early window is confounded by transient post-bypass instability and is indirect for renal injury.

Binary indicator that rhythm remained regular throughout hours 13–24. Captures sustained absence of arrhythmia (more informative than max), but still an indirect severity/hemodynamic proxy and redundant with irregular rhythm summaries.

Binary indicator that rhythm remained regular throughout hours 25–36. Closest window and captures sustained arrhythmia absence/presence (via complement), which may relate to renal hypoperfusion risk, but remains secondary to direct renal/perfusion variables.
Count/frequency of regularity measurements in hours 0–12. Primarily a monitoring-intensity/clinician-behavior proxy rather than physiology, and early post-op measurement density is routine.
Count/frequency of regularity measurements in hours 13–24. Mostly reflects documentation/monitoring patterns and is unlikely to add unique predictive signal for creatinine ratio in a sparse model.

Count/frequency of regularity measurements in hours 25–36. Could weakly proxy ongoing instability prompting closer monitoring, but remains a clinician-behavior surrogate and should be deprioritized versus physiologic extremes.

Represents the proportion of time/rhythm assessments showing irregular rhythm in hours 0–12. Irregular rhythm burden can relate to hemodynamic instability, but early post-op arrhythmias are common/transient and less predictive for Hour-60 creatinine.

Represents irregular rhythm burden in hours 13–24. More informative than 0–12h and more discriminative than max/min binaries, but still an indirect proxy and likely interchangeable with the later (25–36h) rhythm burden.

Represents irregular rhythm burden in hours 25–36, the closest available window to the outcome. Persistent arrhythmia burden can uniquely capture ongoing hemodynamic compromise and renal hypoperfusion risk, making it the strongest rhythm-derived candidate in this batch.

Binary indicator that any irregular rhythm occurred in hours 0–12. 'Ever irregular' in the immediate post-op period is common and noisy, so it is a weak, non-specific predictor and dominated by later-window burden measures.

Binary indicator that any irregular rhythm occurred in hours 13–24. Slightly more informative than 0–12h, but still a coarse 'ever' measure and generally dominated by irregular_mean (burden) and the closer 25–36h window.

Represents whether any irregular cardiac rhythm (e.g., AF) occurred in the 25–36h window. Later arrhythmia can proxy hemodynamic instability/vasopressor need that may affect renal perfusion, but it is indirect and likely redundant with stronger hemodynamic/pressor features.

Indicates absence/presence of irregular rhythm early (0–12h), where transient post-bypass/anesthesia effects are common. Early timing and binary/min aggregation make it a weak, indirect predictor for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Captures whether irregular rhythm was present at any point in 13–24h. Some association with evolving instability exists, but it remains an indirect proxy and is dominated by the closer 25–36h rhythm signal.

Binary/min version of irregular rhythm in the closest window. Less informative than the corresponding max/any-occurrence representation for capturing intermittent arrhythmia burden, so lower priority within this family.
Number/frequency of documenting irregular rhythm in 0–12h is mainly a monitoring/clinician-attention proxy. Early post-op measurement intensity is noisy and not a direct renal physiology signal.

Measurement frequency of irregular rhythm in 13–24h reflects documentation/monitoring rather than physiology. Unlikely to survive sparse selection versus actual rhythm occurrence or hemodynamic variables.

Later measurement frequency may weakly proxy ongoing instability/arrhythmia management closer to the outcome. Still largely clinician-behavior/monitoring intensity and inferior to direct rhythm/hemodynamic features.

Mean proportion/time labeled as 'other' rhythm category in 0–12h is often a documentation artifact and early post-op noise. Provides little unique renal-relevant signal and is likely redundant with clearer rhythm indicators.
Indicates average presence of a non-standard/other rhythm label in 13–24h. This is typically nonspecific and documentation-driven, offering minimal unique predictive value for AKI trajectory.

Later sustained 'other' rhythm labeling could reflect persistent pacing/arrhythmia states that correlate with instability. Still nonspecific and likely dominated by explicit arrhythmia/hemodynamic features, so only modest priority.
Any occurrence of 'other' rhythm in 0–12h is nonspecific and prone to transient post-op labeling. Weak and indirect for predicting Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Any 'other' rhythm occurrence in 13–24h may capture pacing/arrhythmia episodes that can accompany instability. Still a vague category and likely redundant with better-defined rhythm/hemodynamic predictors.

Any 'other' rhythm in 25–36h (closest window) may reflect ongoing pacing/arrhythmia management and associated hemodynamic compromise. Among the 'other' family this is the most informative aggregation/timing, though still indirect.
Min/binary version of 'other' rhythm in 0–12h is both early and poorly specific. Adds little beyond noise and is dominated by later-window indicators.
Min/binary 'other' rhythm in 13–24h is a weak encoding of a nonspecific rhythm category. Low sparse-model value due to poor specificity and redundancy with max/closer-window versions.

Min/binary 'other' rhythm in 25–36h is closer to the outcome but still a low-information encoding of a nonspecific label. Lower priority than the corresponding max in the same window for capturing intermittent episodes.

Count/frequency of documenting an 'other' cardiac rhythm regularity category in the first 12h. This is largely a monitoring/documentation proxy and nonspecific for renal hypoperfusion/AKI, so low sparse-model priority.

Measurement frequency of an 'other' cardiac regularity label during 13–24h. Still primarily clinician-behavior/monitoring intensity and not a direct renal signal, so unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Measurement frequency of an 'other' cardiac regularity label during 25–36h (closest window). Slightly more informative as a proxy for ongoing instability/arrhythmia concern, but remains indirect and weak for predicting creatinine ratio.

Average heart rate from pulse-ox monitor in the immediate 0–12h post-op period. HR is a nonspecific illness/adrenergic marker and early values are noisy post-bypass/anesthesia, so limited unique value for AKI prediction.

Mean pulse-ox-derived heart rate during 13–24h. Sustained tachycardia can reflect low output/sepsis/pain but is indirect and often redundant with stronger hemodynamic/vasopressor features, so modest priority.

Mean pulse-ox-derived heart rate during 25–36h, the closest available window to the 60h creatinine outcome. Persistent tachycardia may capture ongoing stress/low perfusion and can add some signal, but remains nonspecific.
Peak pulse-ox-derived heart rate in 0–12h. Extremes can capture acute instability, but early post-op peaks are often transient and nonspecific, so low-to-moderate sparse-model value.

Maximum pulse-ox-derived heart rate during 13–24h. A later tachycardic extreme may better reflect evolving shock/arrhythmia burden relevant to renal hypoperfusion, and is more informative than the mean within this family.

Maximum pulse-ox-derived heart rate during 25–36h. This near-term extreme best captures ongoing hemodynamic stress/instability among the HR aggregations and is the most likely HR feature to survive sparse selection.

Minimum pulse-ox-derived heart rate in 0–12h. Early bradycardia can be procedural/sedation-related and is a weak, nonspecific predictor of later creatinine rise, dominated by later-window instability signals.

Minimum pulse-ox-derived heart rate during 13–24h. Could reflect conduction issues or oversedation but is generally less tied to renal hypoperfusion than tachycardic extremes, so lower priority than HR max.

Minimum pulse-ox-derived heart rate during 25–36h. Closer timing helps, but bradycardic extremes are still an indirect and less consistent AKI precursor than tachycardia/pressor needs, so modest value.
Frequency of pulse-ox heart rate measurements in 0–12h. This is primarily a monitoring intensity proxy (often routine in ICU) and not a physiologic renal marker, so very low selection priority.
Frequency of pulse-ox heart rate measurements in 13–24h. Measurement count mainly reflects workflow/acuity monitoring rather than kidney physiology, so unlikely to add unique predictive value.

Frequency of pulse-ox heart rate measurements in 25–36h. Even though temporally closer, it remains a clinician-behavior proxy and is typically redundant with other severity indicators, so low priority.
Average apical (auscultated) heart rate in 0–12h. This is an early, less continuous/less reliable HR source and largely redundant with monitor-derived HR, so minimal sparse-model value.

Average apical heart rate in the 13–24h window, reflecting general stress/illness rather than a kidney-specific signal. Likely redundant with other hemodynamic variables and less informative than extremes or later-window measures for sparse selection.

Average apical heart rate in the 25–36h window, closer to the Hour-60 creatinine outcome and may reflect sustained sympathetic drive/low output. Still an indirect AKI predictor and often redundant with BP/vasopressors, so moderate priority.

Indicator of high apical heart rate early (0–12h), when post-bypass/anesthesia effects and transient instability are common. Early tachycardia is a nonspecific severity marker and unlikely to add unique value in a sparse model.

Indicator of peak apical heart rate in 13–24h, capturing episodic tachycardia that may correlate with low output, pain, or arrhythmia. Still indirect for renal injury and typically dominated by more kidney-proximal/hemodynamic features.
