Indicator of peak apical heart rate in 25–36h, which can capture late tachycardic episodes associated with ongoing shock/arrhythmia and renal hypoperfusion risk. Among HR-derived features this is one of the more informative due to timing and extreme, but remains nonspecific.

Indicator of low apical heart rate in the immediate 0–12h period, often influenced by pacing, sedation, hypothermia, or beta-blockade. Weak and noisy relationship to later creatinine ratio and unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Indicator of low apical heart rate in 13–24h, potentially reflecting conduction disease, pacing, or medication effects rather than renal physiology. Indirect and likely redundant with treatment variables, so low priority.

Indicator of low apical heart rate in 25–36h, which may reflect persistent pacing/AV block or heavy beta-blockade and could correlate with low cardiac output. Still a clinician-management proxy more than a direct renal signal, so limited sparse-model value.

Count/frequency of apical HR measurements in 0–12h, mainly reflecting monitoring intensity and workflow. This is a proxy for care complexity rather than physiology and is early/noisy, so very low priority.

Frequency of apical HR measurements in 13–24h, primarily a clinician-behavior/acuity proxy. Generally dominated by actual physiologic values and not kidney-specific, so minimal sparse-selection value.
Frequency of apical HR measurements in 25–36h, which may weakly proxy ongoing instability closer to the outcome. Still largely monitoring-intensity rather than a biomarker, so only slight priority.

Average peripheral HR in 0–12h, a nonspecific early postoperative signal affected by artifacts and transient physiology. Redundant with apical HR summaries and unlikely to add unique predictive value for creatinine ratio.

Peripheral HR mean in 13–24h (binary-typed here), which is both indirect and likely redundant with apical HR and other hemodynamic markers. Low priority for sparse selection given measurement/source redundancy.
Peripheral HR mean in 25–36h (binary-typed), closer to the outcome and may reflect sustained stress/low output. However it is still nonspecific and redundant with apical HR, so only modest value.

Indicator of peak peripheral HR in 0–12h, when tachycardia is common and often transient post-op. Source is redundant with apical HR and the timing is least reliable for Hour-60 creatinine prediction.

Indicator of peak peripheral HR in 13–24h, capturing episodic tachycardia but still a nonspecific severity marker. Likely redundant with apical HR max and other shock/vasopressor variables, so low-to-moderate priority.

Indicates whether the heart rate measurement source was peripheral (e.g., pulse ox/pleth) at any point in 25–36h. This is a monitoring/source artifact and clinician-behavior proxy with little direct renal signal, likely not retained in a sparse model.
Whether peripheral source was used at least once in 0–12h. Early post-op monitoring choices are noisy and not physiologically specific for AKI risk, so it is very low priority.
Whether peripheral source was used at least once in 13–24h. Still primarily reflects monitoring modality rather than hemodynamics or renal perfusion, offering minimal unique predictive value.

Whether peripheral source was used at least once in 25–36h. Despite being the closest window, it remains a source/measurement indicator and is unlikely to add signal beyond true vitals/hemodynamics features.

Count/frequency of peripheral-source HR measurements in 0–12h. Measurement frequency is mainly an intensity-of-monitoring proxy and early post-op patterns are especially non-specific for later creatinine rise.
Count/frequency of peripheral-source HR measurements in 13–24h. This is still a monitoring-intensity proxy with weak, indirect linkage to AKI and is dominated by actual hemodynamic/renal markers.
Count/frequency of peripheral-source HR measurements in 25–36h. Even though temporally closer, it remains a clinician-behavior/monitoring proxy and is unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Proportion/extent of time on mechanical ventilation in 0–12h. Mechanical ventilation reflects illness severity and hemodynamic stress but is indirect for AKI and early post-op ventilation is common/redundant.

Proportion/extent of time on mechanical ventilation in 13–24h. Persistent ventilation beyond the immediate post-op period can mark ongoing shock/complications and may add some unique severity signal for AKI risk.

Proportion/extent of time on mechanical ventilation in 25–36h. Ongoing ventilation this late is a strong marker of persistent critical illness/hemodynamic instability and is the most temporally relevant version for Hour-60 creatinine trajectory.
Whether mechanically ventilated at any point in 0–12h. Nearly universal immediately post-cardiac surgery and thus low-variance/noisy, offering little sparse-model value.
Whether mechanically ventilated at any point in 13–24h. More informative than 0–12h but still a coarse binary dominated by the mean (duration) measure and other physiologic variables.
Whether mechanically ventilated at any point in 25–36h. Timing is favorable, but as a binary it is less informative than the corresponding mean (extent/duration) and is therefore lower priority.

Whether mechanically ventilated for the entire 0–12h window (minimum indicates always mechanical). Early continuous ventilation is common post-op and not specific for evolving AKI, so it is weak for sparse selection.
Whether mechanically ventilated throughout 13–24h. Suggests failure to extubate and higher severity, but is still a coarse proxy and partially redundant with the mean measure.

Whether mechanically ventilated throughout 25–36h. Continuous ventilation this late is a stronger severity marker and temporally closer to the outcome, but remains indirect compared with direct renal/hemodynamic features.

Frequency/amount of documentation of mechanical ventilation in the first 12h is mainly a monitoring/intensity-of-care proxy. It is indirect for AKI risk and likely redundant with later ventilation status and hemodynamic variables, so low sparse-selection priority.

Represents how often mechanical ventilation is recorded in hours 13–24, reflecting ongoing respiratory support/illness severity. Still largely a clinician-behavior proxy and indirect for renal injury, so unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Ongoing mechanical ventilation documentation at 25–36h suggests persistent critical illness closer to the 60h creatinine outcome. More temporally relevant than earlier windows, but remains an indirect proxy and may be redundant with stronger physiologic predictors.

Mean proportion of time on no oxygen device in the immediate post-op 0–12h window. Early extubation/room air status is noisy after surgery and is an indirect renal signal, dominated by later respiratory/hemodynamic features.

Average time on no oxygen device during 13–24h reflects recovery trajectory and lower illness severity. It is still indirect for kidney dysfunction and likely redundant with more direct perfusion/renal markers.

Average time on no oxygen device at 25–36h captures near-term recovery vs ongoing support, which can correlate with AKI risk via overall severity. Useful as a compact trajectory marker, but still indirect and not top-tier versus direct renal/perfusion variables.

Binary indicator that the patient was on no oxygen device at some point in 0–12h. This is a coarse, early post-op status signal with high noise and limited unique value for predicting 60h creatinine ratio.
Indicates any occurrence of being on no oxygen device in 13–24h, a coarse marker of respiratory recovery. Adds limited incremental information beyond mean/other epochs and is indirect for AKI.

Any room-air/no-device period in 25–36h is closer to the outcome and may separate recovering vs persistently ill patients. Still a coarse proxy and partially redundant with the corresponding mean, but could survive if few severity markers are available.

Binary indicator for being on no oxygen device throughout the 0–12h window (or minimum status), reflecting very early stability. Early post-op period is confounded by routine ventilation/anesthesia effects, making this weak for sparse prediction.

Suggests sustained no-device status across 13–24h, indicating better physiologic recovery. It is still an indirect severity marker and likely redundant with more informative later-window respiratory/hemodynamic features.

Sustained no-device status across 25–36h is a stronger near-term recovery signal and may inversely correlate with AKI risk. However it remains indirect and overlaps with other oxygen-device summaries, limiting uniqueness.

Measurement frequency for 'no oxygen device' in 0–12h is primarily documentation/monitoring behavior. It is not a physiologic biomarker and is unlikely to add unique predictive value for creatinine ratio.
How often 'no oxygen device' is recorded in 13–24h mainly reflects charting patterns and care intensity. As a proxy variable it is weak and dominated by actual device status features.

Documentation frequency of no-device status at 25–36h may weakly reflect stability vs frequent reassessment, but remains largely a clinician-behavior proxy. Slightly more temporally relevant, yet still low priority for sparse selection.

Mean time on low-flow oxygen in the first 12h reflects early respiratory support needs after surgery. This is an indirect illness-severity marker and early-window noise limits its unique value for predicting 60h creatinine ratio.

Represents average use of low-flow oxygen in the 13–24h window (a respiratory support/trajectory marker). It is an indirect illness-severity proxy for AKI risk and is likely redundant with the later 25–36h oxygen-support features.

Represents average low-flow oxygen use in the 25–36h window, closer to the Hour-60 creatinine outcome. Still indirect for renal dysfunction, but timing makes it a better sparse-model candidate than earlier windows as a marker of ongoing cardiopulmonary instability.

Indicates whether low-flow oxygen was used at any point in the immediate 0–12h post-op period. This is early, noisy, and largely reflects routine post-extubation care rather than a unique renal hypoperfusion signal.
Indicates any low-flow oxygen use during 13–24h. It is a weak, indirect severity marker and is dominated by the more proximal 25–36h oxygen-support indicators for sparse selection.

Indicates any low-flow oxygen use during 25–36h, capturing persistent need for supplemental oxygen near the prediction horizon. While still a proxy, it may add some unique information about ongoing instability compared with earlier epochs.

Binary minimum for low-flow oxygen in 0–12h effectively reflects whether there was any time without low-flow oxygen (often routine transitions). It is poorly specific for renal risk and redundant with other low-flow indicators.

Binary minimum for low-flow oxygen in 13–24h is hard to interpret clinically and mostly reflects device switching patterns. It is unlikely to survive sparse selection given redundancy and weak linkage to AKI physiology.

Binary minimum for low-flow oxygen in 25–36h may weakly indicate continuous oxygen requirement vs intermittent weaning. Still largely a care-pattern proxy and less informative than a direct marker like high-flow use.

Counts/reflects frequency of documenting low-flow oxygen in 0–12h, mainly a monitoring/workflow intensity proxy. Early post-op documentation frequency is unlikely to add unique predictive value for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Measurement frequency of low-flow oxygen in 13–24h primarily reflects charting intensity and clinician attention. This is indirect and typically dominated by actual support level (e.g., high-flow use) and more proximal physiology.

Measurement frequency of low-flow oxygen in 25–36h may weakly proxy ongoing respiratory management complexity near the outcome window. Still a clinician-behavior/monitoring proxy and generally low priority for sparse selection.
Represents high-flow oxygen use in 0–12h, suggesting early respiratory compromise. However, the immediate post-op period is noisy and this remains an indirect severity marker for AKI risk.

Represents high-flow oxygen use in 13–24h, indicating more significant respiratory support needs beyond the immediate post-op phase. It can proxy persistent cardiopulmonary dysfunction that correlates with AKI risk, though still indirect.

Represents high-flow oxygen use in 25–36h, a strong marker of ongoing respiratory failure/instability close to the Hour-60 creatinine outcome. Within this batch it is one of the most informative severity signals and less redundant than low-flow indicators.

Indicates any high-flow oxygen use in 0–12h, capturing an early extreme of respiratory support. Timing limits reliability for Hour-60 creatinine prediction and it is likely superseded by later high-flow features.

Indicates any high-flow oxygen use in 13–24h, capturing an extreme support requirement beyond immediate post-op noise. It is a reasonable sparse-model candidate but still somewhat redundant with the 25–36h high-flow mean as the more proximal summary.

Represents whether high-flow oxygen was used in the 25–36h window (max over the window). It is a late marker of respiratory support/illness severity that may correlate with AKI risk, but is indirect and likely dominated by hemodynamics/renal-specific features in a sparse model.

Indicates high-flow oxygen use early (0–12h) using the minimum over the window. Early post-op oxygen device status is noisy and indirect for renal outcomes, and the 'min' aggregation is not very informative for a binary exposure.

Indicates high-flow oxygen use in 13–24h using the minimum over the window. Still an indirect severity proxy and the 'min' version is redundant/less informative than later or 'max' exposure summaries for sparse selection.

Indicates high-flow oxygen use in 25–36h using the minimum over the window. Redundant with the 25–36h max indicator and less likely to capture clinically meaningful exposure, so low priority for a sparse model.

Counts/reflects how often high-flow oxygen device status was recorded in 0–12h. This is primarily a monitoring/workflow proxy rather than physiology and is unlikely to add unique predictive value for creatinine ratio.
Measurement frequency of high-flow oxygen device status in 13–24h. Mostly reflects documentation intensity/acuity rather than a direct renal signal, so it is a weak sparse-model candidate.

Measurement frequency of high-flow oxygen device status in 25–36h. Despite being closer to the outcome, it remains a clinician-behavior proxy and is redundant with the actual high-flow use indicators.

Frequency of documentation of first-degree AV block in 0–12h. This is a monitoring/ECG-label proxy with weak and indirect linkage to renal dysfunction, especially in the immediate post-op noise window.
Frequency of documentation of first-degree AV block in 13–24h. Still largely a documentation proxy and unlikely to survive sparse selection versus direct perfusion/renal markers.

Frequency of documentation of first-degree AV block in 25–36h. Even though later, it remains indirect and redundant with more informative hemodynamic/arrhythmia burden variables (if present), so low priority.

Frequency of documentation of higher-grade (2nd/3rd degree) AV block in 0–12h. High-grade block can imply low cardiac output and renal hypoperfusion risk, but 'measured' is a proxy and early timing is noisy, limiting sparse-model value.

Frequency of documentation of higher-grade AV block in 13–24h. Potentially reflects clinically significant conduction issues affecting perfusion, but as a measurement-frequency proxy it is still indirect and likely dominated by direct hemodynamic/vasopressor features.

Frequency of documentation of higher-grade AV block in 25–36h. This later window is closer to Hour 60 and may capture persistent conduction-related low output risk, but it remains a proxy measure and thus only moderately competitive for sparse selection.

Frequency of documentation of bundle branch block in 0–12h. BBB is often chronic or perioperative and not tightly linked to evolving AKI; 'measured' further makes it a documentation proxy with low unique predictive value.
Frequency of documentation of bundle branch block in 13–24h. Mostly reflects ECG labeling/monitoring rather than a direct renal or perfusion insult signal, so low priority in a sparse model.
Frequency of documentation of bundle branch block in 25–36h. Even in the later window it is typically nonspecific and proxy-heavy, and is unlikely to add unique predictive value for creatinine ratio.

Average ionized calcium in the immediate post-op window reflects transfusion/citrate load and replacement practices more than evolving renal dysfunction. Likely noisy early and dominated by later/min-extreme summaries for sparse selection.

Mean ionized calcium during 13–24h may reflect ongoing critical illness and supplementation but is an indirect AKI signal. Somewhat more stable than 0–12h, yet still redundant with min/max and later epoch values.

Mean ionized calcium at 25–36h is closer to the outcome and may capture sustained metabolic derangements associated with organ dysfunction. Still indirect for kidney injury and typically less informative than extremes, so moderate priority.

Peak ionized calcium in 0–12h is often driven by bolus calcium administration during/after bypass rather than renal physiology. Early and treatment-confounded, so low unique value for predicting 60h creatinine ratio.

Maximum ionized calcium in 13–24h can reflect replacement intensity and acid-base shifts, but it is not a direct renal marker. Likely redundant with other calcium summaries and weaker than hypocalcemia (min) as a stress signal.

Maximum ionized calcium at 25–36h is closer in time but still heavily influenced by clinician-driven supplementation. Provides limited unique information about impending AKI compared with hemodynamics/renal labs, so low-moderate priority.

Minimum ionized calcium early post-op may indicate citrate load, hemodilution, or severe illness, which can correlate with complications. However it is early and nonspecific, so unlikely to survive sparse selection if stronger renal/hypoperfusion features exist.

Minimum ionized calcium in 13–24h can capture persistent hypocalcemia associated with ongoing transfusion, inflammation, or organ dysfunction. More informative than mean/max and less confounded by immediate bypass effects, giving moderate sparse-model value.

Minimum ionized calcium at 25–36h is the most temporally proximate extreme and may reflect sustained physiologic stress linked to worse trajectories. Still an indirect AKI predictor and partly treatment-related, so moderate rather than high priority.
Count/frequency of ionized calcium measurements in 0–12h is primarily a monitoring-intensity/complexity proxy. Early post-op protocols drive testing, so it adds little unique predictive signal.
Measurement frequency of ionized calcium in 13–24h reflects clinician concern and care patterns more than physiology. As a proxy variable it is unlikely to be retained in a sparse linear model.
More frequent ionized calcium checks at 25–36h may indicate ongoing instability or transfusion needs, which can correlate with AKI risk. Still a behavior/monitoring proxy, so only slight priority.

Average magnesium in 0–12h is influenced by perioperative replacement and dilutional effects and is not a direct renal function marker. Early timing and treatment confounding limit sparse-model usefulness.
Mean magnesium at 13–24h may reflect renal handling and supplementation but remains a weak, indirect AKI signal. Likely redundant with later magnesium summaries or more kidney-specific variables.

Mean magnesium at 25–36h is closer to the outcome and can reflect reduced renal clearance or ongoing replacement needs. Still nonspecific and treatment-influenced, but may add modest unique value if few renal markers are available.
Peak magnesium in 0–12h is commonly driven by intra/post-op magnesium administration rather than evolving kidney dysfunction. Early and highly confounded, so very low sparse-selection priority.

Peak magnesium reflects supplementation/renal handling but is usually driven by replacement protocols rather than evolving AKI. Adds limited unique value versus stronger renal perfusion/acid–base signals and is not the closest window.

Later peak magnesium may weakly reflect reduced renal clearance or aggressive repletion. Still largely treatment-driven and typically redundant/low-signal for predicting creatinine ratio compared with hemodynamic/acid–base extremes.
Early low magnesium is common post-bypass/hemodilution and is rapidly corrected. High noise and weak mechanistic link to Hour-60 creatinine makes it a poor sparse-model candidate.
Low magnesium can reflect ongoing losses or inadequate repletion, but is not a direct kidney injury marker. Some signal possible, yet likely dominated by more proximal perfusion/acid–base features.

Later low magnesium may indicate persistent instability or diuretic effect, indirectly correlating with illness severity. Still not kidney-specific and often clinician-management dependent, limiting sparse selection priority.
Measurement count mainly reflects monitoring intensity and routine post-op lab schedules. It is a clinician-behavior proxy with little direct physiologic information for AKI prediction.
Frequency of magnesium checks is largely protocol/acuity driven rather than a renal biomarker. Unlikely to survive sparse selection when physiologic variables are available.
Later measurement frequency remains a proxy for care intensity or electrolyte replacement practices. Provides minimal unique predictive value for creatinine ratio.

Average base excess reflects overall metabolic acid–base status and can correlate with shock/hypoperfusion. In the immediate post-op window it is noisy and the mean smooths short insults, reducing sparse-model value.

Mean base excess in 13–24h captures sustained metabolic acidosis/alkalosis, which can track ongoing hypoperfusion and AKI risk. Moderately useful but likely less informative than the corresponding minimum (worst acidosis).

Closest-window mean base excess reflects persistent metabolic derangement near the prediction horizon. Useful as a trajectory marker, though still somewhat diluted compared with extreme (min) values for detecting acute hypoperfusion.

Maximum base excess (most alkalotic) early post-op often reflects ventilation/bicarbonate administration rather than renal hypoperfusion. Low specificity and early timing make it weak for sparse selection.
Higher (alkalotic) base excess is typically treatment/ventilation related and not a strong AKI precursor. Adds little unique predictive value versus acidosis-focused extremes.

Later alkalosis may reflect diuresis/contraction or bicarbonate therapy and is only indirectly related to renal outcomes. Slightly more relevant due to timing, but still dominated by minimum base excess for AKI risk.
Worst early metabolic acidosis can indicate hypoperfusion/lactate burden, a plausible AKI precursor. However, immediate post-op physiology is noisy, so it is less reliable than later-window extremes.

Minimum base excess captures the worst metabolic acidosis in a more stable post-op period, reflecting ongoing hypoperfusion/shock physiology linked to AKI. Strong extreme-based signal with good sparse-model priority within this batch.

Minimum base excess in the 25–36h window captures the worst metabolic acidosis, often reflecting hypoperfusion/shock that can precede AKI. Among base-excess variants here, this extreme and closest-timed summary is the most likely to add unique predictive value for Hour-60 creatinine rise.

Count/frequency of base excess measurements in 0–12h is mainly a monitoring-intensity/clinician-concern proxy rather than physiology. Early post-op measurement frequency is noisy and unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Base excess measurement frequency in 13–24h reflects care intensity and instability rather than a direct renal signal. It is dominated by actual base-excess values (e.g., min) and is a weak sparse-model candidate.

Base excess measurement frequency in 25–36h may weakly proxy ongoing metabolic derangement/ICU complexity closer to the target time. Still largely a clinician-behavior signal and redundant with the base-excess extreme itself.

Direct bilirubin measurement frequency in 0–12h is primarily a proxy for monitoring/liver concern rather than kidney physiology. Early post-op ordering patterns add little unique value for predicting creatinine ratio.

Direct bilirubin measurement frequency in 13–24h reflects clinician suspicion of cholestasis/hemolysis or illness complexity, not a direct AKI precursor. Unlikely to be retained in a sparse linear model.
Direct bilirubin measurement frequency in 25–36h may indicate ongoing multi-organ dysfunction workup closer to Hour 60. Still indirect and behavior-driven, so only a low-priority sparse feature.
Mean WBC in 0–12h reflects early inflammatory/stress response after surgery and is often nonspecific. It is likely noisy and dominated by later windows and/or extremes.

Mean WBC in 13–24h may capture evolving inflammation/infection risk that can contribute to AKI, but it is indirect. As a mean, it is also less sensitive than extremes and may be redundant with later WBC summaries.

Mean WBC in 25–36h is closer to the outcome and may reflect sustained systemic inflammation/sepsis physiology that can worsen renal function. Still indirect for AKI, but the timing makes it a moderate sparse-model candidate among WBC features.

Peak WBC in 0–12h is heavily influenced by immediate post-bypass stress and steroids and is nonspecific. Early extremes are less reliable for Hour-60 creatinine prediction and redundant with later WBC signals.
Peak WBC in 13–24h can flag stronger inflammatory response than the mean, but remains an indirect AKI predictor. It is likely dominated by the closest (25–36h) WBC extreme/trajectory.

Peak WBC in 25–36h captures the strongest inflammatory signal closest to the target and may correlate with infection/sepsis-related AKI risk. It can add value beyond the mean, though still a nonspecific severity marker.
Minimum WBC in 0–12h is usually not a meaningful renal precursor and may reflect dilutional effects or lab variability early post-op. Low unique predictive value and likely redundant.
Minimum WBC in 13–24h is generally less informative than max/mean for inflammatory burden and AKI risk. It is unlikely to survive sparse selection given indirectness and redundancy.

Minimum WBC in 25–36h could weakly reflect immunosuppression/hemodilution or marrow suppression in sicker patients, but it is not a direct AKI signal. Lower priority than WBC max/mean in the same window.

Count of WBC lab draws in the first 12h is mainly a monitoring-intensity/acuity proxy. Weak direct linkage to AKI and dominated by physiologic/hemodynamic and renal markers, so low sparse-selection priority.
Frequency of WBC checks in 13–24h reflects clinician concern rather than kidney physiology. Adds little unique predictive value for Hour-60 creatinine ratio in a sparse model.

Later (25–36h) WBC measurement frequency may better capture evolving complications prompting closer monitoring. Still a proxy variable and likely redundant with actual severity signals, so only modest value.
Mean PT in 0–12h reflects early coagulopathy/hepatic dysfunction or dilutional effects post-bypass. Early window is noisy and mean blunts extremes, so it is a weak, indirect AKI predictor.
Mean PT at 25–36h captures more persistent coagulopathy/critical illness closer to the outcome window. Indirect for renal injury but may add some unique signal about ongoing organ dysfunction.
Peak PT in 0–12h captures worst early coagulopathy, which can correlate with shock/transfusion burden. However it is temporally early and often transient, limiting sparse-model priority.

Peak PT at 25–36h reflects the most severe ongoing coagulopathy near-term, often tracking persistent illness severity, bleeding/transfusion, or hepatic hypoperfusion that can precede AKI. Among PT summaries, this extreme and later epoch is the most informative and least redundant.
Minimum PT in 0–12h is typically near-normal and not a plausible driver of renal dysfunction. Provides little incremental information versus max/mean and is likely noise.
Minimum PT in 25–36h mainly reflects best values rather than insult severity. Dominated by PT_max/mean for capturing pathologic signal, so low priority.
Number of PT measurements in 0–12h is a clinician-behavior/monitoring proxy (bleeding concern, anticoagulation management). Not a direct renal signal and unlikely to survive sparse selection.
PT measurement frequency in 13–24h reflects care intensity rather than physiology. Adds minimal unique predictive value beyond actual PT values and other severity markers.

More frequent PT checks at 25–36h may indicate ongoing bleeding/coagulopathy management and higher complexity closer to outcome. Still a proxy and generally redundant with PT_max itself, so modest score.

Mean NRBCs in 0–12h can indicate severe physiologic stress/hypoxemia or marrow response, sometimes associated with worse outcomes. Early timing and indirectness to kidney injury limit sparse-model value.
NRBC mean in 13–24h reflects ongoing stress/critical illness rather than a renal-specific pathway. Potentially prognostic but likely dominated by more direct perfusion/renal markers.

NRBC mean at 25–36h suggests persistent severe illness/hypoxemia closer to the creatinine outcome window. Indirect but may add unique prognostic signal in a sparse model if other severity markers are absent.

Peak NRBCs in 0–12h captures an extreme stress signal, but it is early and may reflect transient peri-bypass effects. Less likely to add unique predictive value compared with later-epoch severity indicators.

Peak nucleated RBCs reflect physiologic stress/inflammation or marrow response after major surgery. It is an indirect, non-renal marker and likely redundant with stronger severity/hemodynamic signals, so low sparse-selection priority.

Later peak nucleated RBCs may indicate ongoing critical illness or hypoxemic/inflammatory stress closer to the outcome window. Still a weak, indirect proxy for AKI risk and unlikely to add unique value in a sparse linear model.

Minimum nucleated RBCs in the immediate post-op window is largely uninformative and often near zero. Early values are noisy and this aggregation is dominated by max/trajectory or other severity markers.
Minimum nucleated RBCs mainly reflects absence of the signal rather than injury burden. It is indirect for renal dysfunction and redundant with max measures if any NRBC signal matters.

Minimum nucleated RBCs late post-op provides little incremental information beyond whether NRBCs are present at all. As an indirect and low-dynamic feature, it is unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Count of NRBC measurements is primarily a monitoring-intensity/clinician-behavior proxy. In the early post-op period it is especially confounded by routine labs and adds little unique physiologic signal.

Measurement frequency reflects lab ordering patterns and perceived complexity rather than kidney physiology. It is a weak proxy and typically dominated by actual lab values and other acuity indicators.

Later NRBC measurement frequency may correlate with ongoing instability but remains a clinician-behavior proxy. It is unlikely to add unique predictive value for creatinine ratio beyond true physiologic variables.

Average glucose early post-op reflects stress hyperglycemia and insulin management but is influenced by bypass/anesthesia and protocolized care. Mean smooths extremes and is less informative than later or peak glucose for sparse prediction.

Mean glucose in 13–24h captures sustained dysglycemia and stress response, which can correlate with complications including AKI. Still indirect and often redundant with peak glucose and overall illness severity.

Mean glucose closest to the outcome window reflects ongoing metabolic stress and treatment needs, which can track with evolving organ dysfunction. It may add modest unique signal, but remains indirect for renal injury.

Early peak glucose captures acute stress hyperglycemia but is heavily affected by immediate post-op physiology and insulin protocols. As an indirect AKI correlate and early/noisy, it is a weaker sparse-model candidate than later peaks.

Peak glucose in 13–24h better reflects persistent stress response and dysglycemia beyond immediate post-op noise. Extremes can be more informative than means and may contribute modest independent predictive value.

Peak glucose at 25–36h is the most temporally proximate extreme dysglycemia measure and can reflect ongoing critical illness, catecholamine exposure, and inflammatory stress linked to AKI risk. Among glucose features, this is the strongest sparse-selection candidate.

Minimum glucose early post-op is often driven by insulin titration, sampling timing, or transient perioperative factors. It is a noisy, treatment-confounded extreme and typically not a robust predictor of later creatinine rise.

Minimum glucose in 13–24h may indicate hypoglycemia from aggressive insulin or poor intake, serving more as a care-process marker than renal physiology. It could weakly track frailty/instability but is usually not a top sparse-model feature.

Lowest glucose in the 25–36h window reflects hypoglycemia/stress-dysglycemia and overall metabolic instability. Some predictive value for AKI risk via illness severity, but indirect and typically weaker than hemodynamic/renal-specific signals, so mid-tier for sparse selection.

Count/frequency of glucose checks in the first 12h is mainly a monitoring-intensity/insulin-protocol proxy. Early post-op period is noisy and this adds little unique physiologic signal for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Frequency of glucose measurements in 13–24h reflects clinician concern/ICU workflow more than kidney physiology. Likely redundant with actual glucose values and other severity markers, so low priority.

Later (25–36h) glucose measurement frequency may proxy ongoing instability or insulin infusion, closer to the outcome window. Still primarily a behavior/monitoring feature and usually dominated by actual glucose extremes, so only modest value.

Average platelet count in 0–12h reflects post-CPB hemodilution/consumption and bleeding/inflammation. Early timing and mean aggregation are less informative than later nadirs, making it a weak sparse-model candidate.

Mean platelets in 13–24h captures ongoing thrombocytopenia/inflammation. Some association with severity and complications, but mean is redundant with min and less specific for renal hypoperfusion/AKI.

Mean platelet count in 25–36h reflects sustained thrombocytopenia closer to the creatinine outcome window. Moderately useful as a severity/complication marker, but still indirect and often dominated by the platelet nadir.

Maximum platelet count in 0–12h is rarely the clinically meaningful signal post-cardiac surgery and is heavily influenced by dilution/transfusion timing. Provides little unique information for AKI prediction compared with platelet minima.
Platelet maximum in 13–24h is not a typical driver or sensitive marker of evolving organ dysfunction. Likely redundant/noisy relative to platelet minimum and thus low priority.
Platelet maximum in 25–36h generally reflects recovery or transfusion rather than risk physiology. Adds minimal unique predictive value for creatinine ratio compared with nadir-based features.

Platelet nadir in 0–12h captures early thrombocytopenia from CPB consumption, bleeding, or inflammation. It can correlate with severity, but early post-op noise limits reliability for Hour-60 creatinine, so moderate priority.

Platelet nadir in 13–24h reflects evolving thrombocytopenia and systemic inflammatory/consumptive states. More informative than early values, but still an indirect AKI predictor, so moderate sparse-selection value.

Platelet nadir in 25–36h is the most informative platelet aggregation and closest in time, capturing persistent thrombocytopenia linked to complications (bleeding, sepsis/SIRS, low-flow states). Stronger and less redundant than platelet means/max, making it a good sparse-model keeper within this batch.
Frequency of platelet checks in 0–12h is largely routine post-op monitoring and a clinician-behavior proxy. Early timing and lack of direct physiology make it low value for sparse prediction.

Platelet measurement frequency in 13–24h mainly reflects monitoring intensity or bleeding concern rather than kidney injury biology. Typically redundant with actual platelet values and other severity indicators.

More frequent platelet checks in 25–36h may indicate ongoing bleeding/transfusion or instability closer to the outcome. Still a proxy feature and generally weaker than platelet nadir itself, so only modest priority.

Mean corpuscular hemoglobin (MCH) reflects RBC indices/anemia phenotype rather than acute renal physiology. Early post-op mean values are unlikely to add unique predictive value for Hour-60 creatinine ratio and are dominated by hemodynamics/renal markers.

MCH mean in 13–24h is a stable hematologic index with weak mechanistic linkage to evolving AKI. Low sparse-model priority because it is indirect and largely redundant with broader anemia/bleeding/transfusion signals not captured here.

MCH mean at 25–36h remains a chronic RBC characteristic and not a near-direct kidney dysfunction precursor. Despite closer timing, it is unlikely to survive sparse selection versus more renal-perfusion or nephrotoxin features.
Maximum MCH in the immediate 0–12h window mainly reflects lab variability or transfusion-related shifts, not renal injury. Redundant within the MCH family and not a strong AKI signal.
Peak MCH over 13–24h is not a clinically meaningful extreme for kidney risk compared with hypotension/oliguria/lactate-type extremes. Very low unique predictive value for creatinine ratio.
Peak MCH at 25–36h is still an RBC index extreme with minimal pathophysiologic connection to AKI trajectory. Unlikely to be selected in a sparse linear model.
Minimum MCH early post-op is more reflective of baseline microcytosis/iron deficiency or measurement noise than renal hypoperfusion. Weak and redundant signal for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
Low MCH in 13–24h does not capture an acute renal insult and is unlikely to add incremental predictive value. Sparse-model priority remains very low due to indirectness.
Minimum MCH at 25–36h is not a meaningful near-term biomarker of evolving AKI. Even with better temporal proximity, it is dominated by more direct renal/hemodynamic features.
Count of MCH measurements is primarily a monitoring-intensity/ordering behavior proxy. In 0–12h it is especially routine and noisy, offering little unique predictive value.
Measurement frequency reflects clinician behavior and illness complexity more than physiology. Low priority for sparse selection because it is an indirect proxy and likely confounded.

More frequent MCH checks at 25–36h may weakly proxy ongoing instability/bleeding workup, which can correlate with AKI risk. Still indirect and not a primary keeper, but slightly more informative due to later timing.
Mean serum sodium reflects fluid balance and neurohormonal stress but is not a direct AKI marker. Early post-op values are influenced by bypass/fluids, so it is a weak sparse-model candidate.

Sodium mean in 13–24h can reflect evolving free-water balance, diuresis, and severity of illness that may track with renal dysfunction. Moderate priority as an indirect but potentially informative systemic signal.

Later sodium mean is closer to the Hour-60 target and may capture sustained dysnatremia from fluid management/renal concentrating impairment. Still indirect for AKI, but could add incremental value in a sparse model.

Peak sodium in 0–12h may capture hypernatremia from diuresis or hypertonic exposures, but early post-op extremes are often transient and treatment-driven. Lower priority than later-window sodium summaries and more direct renal/hemodynamic predictors.

Peak sodium in the 13–24h window reflects dysnatremia from fluid balance/diuresis rather than a direct renal injury marker. Some prognostic signal for illness/volume status, but largely indirect and dominated by the closer 25–36h sodium features.

Peak sodium closest to the outcome can capture evolving free-water deficit/diuretic effect and concentration from hypoperfusion. Still an indirect AKI signal, but the 25–36h timing makes it the most defensible sodium summary for sparse selection.

Early postoperative nadir sodium is often driven by bypass-related hemodilution and immediate fluid shifts. High noise and weak specificity for later creatinine rise, so low priority in a sparse model.
Nadir sodium at 13–24h may reflect ongoing dilution, SIADH-like physiology, or aggressive fluids. Indirect and typically redundant with other volume/hemodynamic variables, so only a weak candidate.

Later nadir sodium can reflect persistent dilution/over-resuscitation and is closer to the 60h creatinine trajectory. Among sodium minima, this timing is most informative, though still an indirect renal proxy.
Count/indicator of sodium measurements is primarily a monitoring-intensity/acuity proxy rather than physiology. Early window adds additional noise and is unlikely to survive sparse selection.
Measurement frequency reflects clinician concern and care complexity more than kidney function. Provides little unique predictive value compared with actual sodium values and other severity markers.

More frequent sodium checks late may indicate evolving instability (diuresis, hypertonic therapy, dysnatremia) and thus modestly track risk. Still largely a behavior proxy, so only a low-to-moderate sparse-model candidate.
Whether TEG platelet aggregation was measured mainly reflects bleeding/thrombosis evaluation and transfusion management. It is not a direct renal signal and is an early, proxy-heavy feature.

TEG platelet aggregation measurement indicates clinician concern about coagulopathy rather than kidney perfusion/function. Likely redundant with broader severity/procedure variables and low value for sparse selection.
Late TEG measurement can proxy ongoing bleeding/transfusion or ECMO/anticoagulation complexity, which can correlate with AKI risk. Still indirect and behavior-driven, so only modest priority.
Mean MCHC early post-op reflects RBC indices/hemodilution and is weakly related to renal outcomes. High redundancy with hemoglobin/hematocrit and low specificity for AKI.
Average MCHC in 13–24h is a nonspecific anemia/transfusion/hemodilution marker. Unlikely to add unique predictive value for creatinine ratio in a sparse linear model.
Later mean MCHC may weakly reflect ongoing hemodilution/transfusion burden closer to the outcome window. Still a low-specificity RBC index and typically dominated by direct anemia/bleeding variables.

Maximum MCHC early is often a lab artifact or reflects transient concentration changes rather than meaningful physiology. Very indirect for AKI prediction and redundant with other hematology measures.
Peak MCHC at 13–24h remains a nonspecific RBC index with limited mechanistic link to renal dysfunction. Low unique value and unlikely to be retained in sparse feature selection.

Peak MCHC in 25–36h is mainly an RBC index reflecting concentration/hemoconcentration or lab artifact, not a direct renal perfusion/injury signal. Likely redundant with hematocrit/hemoglobin patterns and low unique value for AKI prediction in a sparse model.

Early (0–12h) nadir MCHC is an RBC index influenced by dilution/transfusion and peri-bypass shifts. Weak, indirect link to kidney dysfunction and dominated by more direct hemodynamic/renal markers; low sparse-selection priority.

13–24h minimum MCHC reflects RBC indices/dilution rather than renal injury physiology. Adds little unique predictive information beyond hematocrit and overall fluid status; unlikely to survive sparse screening.

25–36h minimum MCHC may weakly proxy ongoing hemodilution/bleeding/transfusion closer to the outcome window. Still indirect and largely redundant with hematocrit minima/means, so only a weak candidate.
Measurement count of MCHC in 0–12h is primarily a monitoring-intensity/clinical workflow proxy. Early post-op labs are routine and noisy, offering minimal unique signal for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
MCHC measurement frequency in 13–24h reflects clinician behavior and lab ordering rather than kidney physiology. Low specificity for evolving AKI and unlikely to add value in a sparse linear model.

More frequent MCHC checks in 25–36h can indicate ongoing instability/bleeding evaluation, which may correlate with AKI risk. Still a proxy variable and generally dominated by direct physiologic features, so modest priority.

Mean hematocrit in 0–12h reflects immediate post-bypass hemodilution/transfusion and oxygen-carrying capacity. Early values are noisy and less predictive for Hour-60 creatinine than later minima, so low-moderate priority.

13–24h mean hematocrit captures sustained anemia/hemodilution which can relate to renal hypoxia risk. However, mean smooths extremes and is often redundant with hematocrit minima, limiting sparse-model value.

25–36h mean hematocrit is closer to the outcome and reflects ongoing anemia/hemodilution that may contribute to renal hypoxia and AKI trajectory. Still less informative than the nadir for capturing acute insult, but could add some unique signal.

Early maximum hematocrit mainly reflects hemoconcentration/transfusion immediately post-op and is not a strong AKI precursor. Likely redundant and less pathophysiologically relevant than low hematocrit/hypoperfusion signals.
13–24h peak hematocrit is generally not the harmful exposure for kidneys compared with anemia/hypoperfusion. Low unique predictive value and dominated by minima/other severity markers.

25–36h maximum hematocrit may reflect diuresis/hemoconcentration but is nonspecific for renal injury and often confounded by transfusion. Unlikely to be selected over more direct renal/hemodynamic predictors.

0–12h hematocrit nadir captures acute anemia/hemodilution/bleeding which can contribute to renal hypoxia risk. Despite early post-op noise, the extreme is more informative than the mean and may carry some predictive value.

13–24h hematocrit minimum reflects clinically meaningful anemia/hemodilution after initial bypass effects, which can signal ongoing bleeding/transfusion needs and reduced oxygen delivery relevant to AKI. As an extreme, it is a strong sparse-model candidate within this batch.

25–36h hematocrit nadir is the closest window and best captures ongoing or worsening anemia/hemodilution near the time AKI evolves, linking to renal hypoxia and severity. Among these features, this extreme is most likely to add unique predictive value for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Count of hematocrit checks in the first 12h post-op, mainly reflecting monitoring intensity/transfusion concern. Weak, clinician-behavior proxy and early window; unlikely to add unique value for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
Frequency of hematocrit measurement in 13–24h, a proxy for ongoing bleeding/anemia management and acuity. Still largely monitoring behavior and redundant with more direct hemodynamic/renal signals.

Frequency of hematocrit checks in 25–36h, reflecting continued instability/bleeding or transfusion evaluation closer to the outcome. Slightly more informative by timing, but remains an indirect monitoring proxy rather than a renal physiology marker.
Mean absolute basophil count in 0–12h, a nonspecific CBC differential component. No clear mechanistic link to AKI and likely noise; poor sparse-model candidate.
Mean absolute basophil count in 13–24h. Still nonspecific and unlikely to contribute unique predictive signal for creatinine trajectory.
Peak basophil count in 0–12h. Extremes here do not plausibly capture renal hypoperfusion/AKI risk and are likely dominated by other inflammatory/illness markers.
Peak basophil count in 13–24h. Minimal expected association with postoperative creatinine rise and unlikely to survive sparse selection.
Nadir basophil count in 0–12h. Not a meaningful renal insult marker and likely reflects lab variability rather than kidney risk.
Nadir basophil count in 13–24h. Similarly nonspecific and low-yield for predicting Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
Number of basophil measurements (CBC differentials) in 0–12h, essentially lab frequency. Monitoring-intensity proxy with little direct renal information and early-window noise.
Number of basophil measurements in 13–24h, reflecting how often CBCs were ordered. Indirect clinician-behavior signal and unlikely to add unique predictive value.
Number of basophil measurements in 25–36h, a proxy for ongoing concern/infection workup closer to outcome. Timing makes it slightly more informative, but still indirect and weak for sparse selection.

Mean lymphocyte percentage in 0–12h, reflecting stress leukogram/inflammation after surgery. Indirect illness-severity signal and early post-op variability limit unique value for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Mean lymphocyte percentage in 13–24h, capturing sustained stress/inflammatory response beyond immediate bypass effects. Moderately useful as a systemic severity marker, but still indirect for AKI and likely replaceable by stronger physiologic variables.
Peak lymphocyte percentage in 0–12h. Extremes may reflect transient lab shifts rather than renal risk; early timing reduces reliability for Hour-60 prediction.

Peak lymphocyte percentage in 13–24h, potentially reflecting evolving inflammatory/immune trajectory. Some incremental value versus the mean by capturing extremes, but remains an indirect proxy and not top-tier for sparse selection.

Minimum lymphocyte percentage early post-op reflects stress/inflammation and hemodilution more than kidney-specific injury. Weak, indirect signal and early window is noisy for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Minimum lymphocyte percentage in 13–24h may track evolving systemic stress/sepsis risk, which can correlate with AKI. Still indirect and likely low unique value versus hemodynamics/renal markers, so low sparse-selection priority.
Count of lymphocyte % measurements is primarily a monitoring-intensity/ordering proxy rather than physiology. Early post-op labs are routine, so this adds little unique predictive value.
Measurement frequency mainly reflects clinician behavior and illness complexity rather than renal pathophysiology. Unlikely to survive sparse selection given weak, proxy-heavy signal.
Later measurement frequency can indicate ongoing concern for infection/inflammation, which may relate to AKI risk. Still a proxy variable; modest value only because it is closer to Hour 60.
Having a random vancomycin level measured suggests vancomycin exposure and/or concern for renal clearance. Mostly a clinician-behavior proxy and early timing, so limited unique predictive value.

Random vancomycin level measurement in 13–24h may reflect early renal dysfunction prompting drug monitoring. Still indirect (measurement, not level/dose) and likely redundant with other severity signals.

Random vancomycin level measurement closer to Hour 60 can be a stronger proxy for evolving reduced clearance/AKI prompting monitoring. Among measurement-only features, this timing gives it comparatively higher sparse-model value.
Trough measurement indicates vancomycin use and therapeutic drug monitoring, which can be triggered by renal impairment risk. Early post-op and proxy nature limit unique predictive contribution.
Trough monitoring in 13–24h may reflect concern for accumulation due to reduced renal function. Still a measurement proxy and likely redundant with other indicators of acuity/renal trajectory.
Trough monitoring at 25–36h is closer to the outcome and may capture clinician response to declining renal clearance. Proxy-heavy but potentially informative enough to compete within this batch.

Average oxygen saturation early post-op reflects respiratory status and overall instability, which can contribute to renal hypoxia. Mean in 0–12h is often well-maintained and noisy, so weak sparse-selection priority.
Mean SpO2 in 13–24h captures sustained hypoxemia burden, an indirect contributor to AKI risk. Still nonspecific and typically tightly controlled, so limited unique value.

Mean SpO2 in 25–36h is the closest window and can reflect persistent hypoxemia/respiratory failure contributing to renal injury risk. More likely to add incremental value than earlier means, though still indirect.

Maximum SpO2 mainly reflects periods of high supplemental oxygen and is usually near 100%, offering little discrimination. Dominated by other oxygenation/hemodynamic variables and not a renal-specific signal.
Maximum SpO2 in 13–24h is typically ceiling-limited and not informative about hypoxemic insults. Redundant and low-yield for sparse selection compared with mean/min oxygenation metrics.
Represents best oxygenation in the 25–36h window. Max SpO2 is usually near-ceiling and adds little unique signal for AKI risk compared with hypoxemia extremes or hemodynamic/renal markers.

Captures early postoperative hypoxemia episodes. Hypoxemia can contribute to renal injury but 0–12h values are often transient/noisy post-bypass and are a weak, indirect predictor for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
Minimum SpO2 in 13–24h reflects ongoing respiratory failure/low oxygen delivery. Still an indirect illness-severity marker and likely dominated by later (25–36h) hypoxemia if present.

Minimum SpO2 closest to the target window captures clinically meaningful hypoxemia that may impair renal oxygen delivery. Among SpO2 summaries, this extreme and timing make it the most plausible sparse-model keeper, though still indirect.
Number of SpO2 measurements in 0–12h is mainly a monitoring-intensity/acuity proxy. It is not a direct physiologic renal signal and is unlikely to survive sparse selection.
Measurement frequency reflects clinician concern and care patterns more than oxygenation physiology. Adds little unique predictive value beyond actual SpO2 minima/maxima.
Later monitoring frequency may correlate with instability but remains a behavior/complexity proxy. In a sparse linear model it is typically dominated by direct physiologic variables.

Average ALT in the immediate postoperative period reflects hepatic injury/ischemia or baseline liver disease. It is an indirect organ-dysfunction marker and early means are noisy and redundant with more informative extremes.
Peak ALT in 0–12h can indicate ischemic hepatitis/low-flow state, which can co-occur with AKI. Still early and nonspecific, so it is a secondary severity proxy rather than a top sparse-model feature.
Minimum ALT is generally not clinically meaningful for hypoperfusion or AKI risk. Provides little signal and is dominated by max/trajectory measures.
ALT measurement count is largely driven by ordering/monitoring practices. It is a weak proxy and unlikely to add unique predictive value for creatinine ratio.
Frequency of ALT checks reflects clinician behavior and perceived complexity. Not a direct biomarker and typically redundant with actual ALT values and other severity indicators.
Later ALT measurement frequency remains a monitoring proxy rather than physiology. Unlikely to be selected when direct renal markers (e.g., BUN/creatinine/urine output) are available.

Mean BUN is a direct renal-related lab reflecting azotemia, catabolism, and volume status. Early postoperative BUN can be confounded by hemodilution and perioperative factors, so it is useful but not top-tier versus later BUN.
BUN in 13–24h better reflects early renal trajectory and evolving azotemia after initial postoperative noise. As a direct kidney-related marker, it is a strong sparse-model candidate.

BUN closest to Hour 60 captures near-term azotemia/renal dysfunction and volume status, providing direct predictive information for subsequent creatinine rise. Within this batch it is the highest-yield renal signal and likely to survive sparse selection.

Peak BUN in the immediate post-op window reflects early azotemia/hemoconcentration but is often confounded by CPB-related fluid shifts. Some signal for evolving AKI, but earlier and less reliable than later BUN maxima.

Peak BUN at 13–24h captures early trajectory of azotemia after initial post-op noise settles. Stronger sparse-model candidate than 0–12h, though still somewhat redundant with the closer 25–36h maximum.

Peak BUN closest to the prediction horizon is a near-term marker of worsening renal function and/or catabolic/volume status. Among BUN summaries, this max is most likely to add unique predictive value for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
Lowest BUN early post-op is usually driven by dilution from fluids/CPB rather than renal recovery. It is dominated by later and/or peak azotemia measures for predicting subsequent creatinine rise.
Minimum BUN in this window mainly reflects dilution and nutrition/catabolism rather than kidney injury severity. Provides little unique value compared with BUN maxima closer to 60h.

Lower BUN at 25–36h may reflect ongoing dilution or lower catabolic state, which can weakly relate to creatinine trajectory. Still generally less informative than the corresponding BUN max for AKI risk and thus lower sparse priority.

Count/frequency of BUN measurements is primarily a monitoring-intensity/clinician concern proxy in the immediate post-op period. Weak direct physiologic signal and unlikely to survive sparse selection.
BUN measurement frequency reflects care patterns and perceived instability more than renal physiology. Adds little unique predictive value beyond actual BUN values.
Later measurement frequency can proxy evolving concern for AKI closer to the outcome window. Still a behavior/acuity proxy and typically dominated by the BUN level itself.

Whether amylase was checked early post-op is a clinician-behavior marker (e.g., concern for pancreatitis/ischemia) rather than a renal biomarker. Very indirect and unlikely to help a sparse model for creatinine ratio.
Amylase measurement indicates diagnostic workup intensity and nonspecific complications. Minimal unique relevance to predicting Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
Amylase measurement in this later window remains a proxy for clinician concern and non-renal complications. Not a direct renal signal and low priority for sparse selection.

Average pH early post-op reflects acid-base status but is heavily influenced by ventilation, transfusion, and CPB-related shifts. It is an indirect severity marker and less predictive than later acid-base measures.

Mean pH at 13–24h captures more stable metabolic acidosis/alkalosis, which can correlate with hypoperfusion and AKI risk. Useful but indirect and potentially redundant with more extreme/closer physiologic markers.

Mean pH closest to the outcome window reflects sustained acid-base derangement from shock/renal dysfunction and is more temporally aligned with Hour-60 creatinine changes. Stronger sparse candidate among pH means, though still indirect compared with direct renal labs.

Maximum pH early post-op often reflects transient respiratory alkalosis from ventilator management rather than renal-relevant pathology. Low unique value and dominated by later, more stable acid-base indicators.

Represents the highest alkalemia in the 13–24h window. Max pH is usually less informative for AKI risk than acidemia/hypoperfusion signals and is likely redundant with stronger hemodynamic/acid-base extremes.

Represents the highest alkalemia closest to the prediction horizon (25–36h). Despite better timing, alkalemia is a weaker/less specific renal-risk signal than low pH and often reflects ventilation/bicarb management, so low sparse priority.

Represents the worst acidemia immediately post-op (0–12h), which can reflect hypoperfusion and early shock. However, this epoch is noisy after bypass/anesthesia, so it is less reliable than later minima for Hour-60 creatinine.

Represents the worst acidemia in 13–24h, a more stable period where low pH can capture ongoing hypoperfusion/sepsis and AKI risk. Stronger sparse candidate than early pH_min and more pathophysiologically relevant than pH_max/means.

Represents the worst acidemia in the closest available window (25–36h), a near-term marker of ongoing hypoperfusion/respiratory failure and evolving organ dysfunction preceding AKI. Among pH features, this extreme and timing make it the highest-value sparse predictor.

Counts/indicates frequency of pH checks in 0–12h, mainly reflecting monitoring intensity and immediate post-op workflow. This is a clinician-behavior proxy and is dominated by actual pH values for prediction.
Measurement frequency of pH in 13–24h is primarily a proxy for acuity/ventilation management rather than a direct renal signal. Likely redundant with severity markers and actual pH extremes.

Measurement frequency of pH in 25–36h may weakly indicate ongoing instability closer to Hour 60. Still largely a monitoring/behavior proxy and typically dominated by pH_min and other physiologic variables.

Whether cholesterol was measured in 0–12h reflects ordering behavior and baseline risk assessment, not acute renal physiology. Very low likelihood of surviving sparse selection for predicting creatinine ratio.
Cholesterol measurement in 13–24h is an indirect care-pattern signal with minimal mechanistic link to AKI trajectory. Unlikely to add unique predictive value in a sparse model.
Cholesterol measurement in 25–36h remains a weak clinician-behavior proxy and not a near-term kidney dysfunction marker. Very low priority for sparse inclusion.

Mean lymphocyte count in 0–12h reflects stress response/inflammation and hemodilution early post-op. It is indirect and early/noisy, so unlikely to add unique value versus stronger perfusion/renal markers.

Mean lymphocyte count in 13–24h is a nonspecific inflammation/physiologic stress marker. It may correlate with illness severity but is indirect and typically dominated by more proximal renal/perfusion features.
Maximum lymphocyte count in 0–12h is rarely a meaningful AKI precursor and is sensitive to lab variability/hemodilution. Low unique predictive value and dominated by other severity signals.
Maximum lymphocyte count in 13–24h is not a strong mechanistic driver of AKI and adds little beyond general illness context. Low sparse-model priority compared with acid-base/hemodynamic extremes.
Minimum lymphocyte count in 0–12h can reflect stress leukogram/inflammation and may weakly track severity. Still indirect and early post-op, so only a weak candidate for sparse selection.

Minimum lymphocyte count reflects stress/inflammation and possible infection risk rather than a direct renal signal. Likely a weak, nonspecific severity marker and unlikely to survive sparse selection for predicting creatinine_ratio.

Measurement frequency is mainly a monitoring-intensity/clinician-behavior proxy, not a physiologic biomarker. Early (0–12h) adds additional noise and little unique value for Hour-60 creatinine prediction.
Counts how often lymphocytes were checked, reflecting care intensity rather than kidney physiology. Redundant proxy signal with low expected incremental value in a sparse model.
Later measurement frequency may weakly proxy evolving complications prompting more labs. Still primarily clinician-behavior and not a direct renal precursor, so low priority despite closer timing.

Whether glucose was measured is a monitoring proxy (e.g., insulin protocols/diabetes) rather than a renal biomarker. In the immediate post-op window it is especially routine and unlikely to add unique predictive value.
Glucose measurement presence reflects protocolized care/acuity more than kidney injury. Low incremental value for sparse selection compared with direct renal function/hemodynamic features.

Later glucose monitoring may indicate ongoing instability or insulin infusion, indirectly associated with worse outcomes. Still a proxy variable and generally dominated by direct physiologic/lab predictors for AKI.
CK measurement frequency is a clinician-suspicion proxy (rhabdo/ischemia) rather than the CK level itself. Early post-op ordering is often routine/low-yield for predicting Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
Checking CK in this window may reflect concern for muscle injury/low-flow states that can contribute to AKI. Still indirect and weaker than actual renal function markers, so low-to-moderate priority.

Later CK monitoring may signal ongoing tissue injury or complications, which can correlate with AKI risk. However it remains a behavior proxy and is unlikely to beat direct kidney function features in sparse selection.

Mean eGFR summarizes early post-op renal function derived from creatinine, but 0–12h values can be distorted by hemodilution and peri-bypass shifts. Useful but less reliable and more redundant than later eGFR for predicting Hour-60 creatinine_ratio.

Mean eGFR in 13–24h captures early trajectory of renal function after initial post-op noise settles. Strong, fairly direct signal for evolving AKI and likely to contribute in a sparse model, though still somewhat smoothed.

Mean eGFR at 25–36h is the closest available direct renal-function summary to the Hour-60 target and reflects evolving AKI trajectory. High unique predictive value for creatinine_ratio and a top sparse-model keeper in this batch.

Maximum eGFR mainly reflects the best (often dilution-inflated) apparent renal function early after surgery. It is less informative for impending creatinine rise and is dominated by later eGFR summaries.

Higher (best) eGFR in this window is less predictive of subsequent creatinine increase than lower/average renal function measures. Likely redundant with eGFR_mean and not the aggregation that preserves AKI signal.

Best eGFR in the closest window may add limited information about renal reserve/recovery, but it is generally less aligned with AKI risk than sustained/average dysfunction. Some incremental value, yet typically dominated by 25–36h eGFR_mean for sparse selection.

Minimum eGFR in the first 12h reflects early postoperative renal function but is often confounded by immediate bypass/anesthesia fluid shifts. Useful signal, yet likely noisier and less predictive than later-window eGFR for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Minimum eGFR at 13–24h captures early AKI trajectory after initial postoperative noise settles. Strong renal-proximal predictor and less redundant than 0–12h, though still not as temporally close as 25–36h.
Minimum eGFR at 25–36h is a near-direct marker of evolving kidney dysfunction closest to the Hour-60 target. The extreme (min) preserves acute deterioration and should be a top sparse-model keeper.
Count/frequency of eGFR measurements in 0–12h mainly reflects monitoring intensity and clinician concern rather than physiology. Low unique predictive value and dominated by actual eGFR values.
Measurement frequency of eGFR at 13–24h is primarily a care-process proxy (acuity/attention) and is not a direct renal biomarker. Unlikely to survive sparse selection when eGFR minima are available.
Measurement frequency at 25–36h may weakly encode evolving concern for AKI closer to the outcome window. Still largely a clinician-behavior proxy and typically redundant with the eGFR_min signal.

LDH measurement frequency early post-op reflects monitoring patterns (e.g., hemolysis/ischemia workup) more than a direct renal pathway. Very weak candidate compared with direct renal function features.
LDH measurement frequency at 13–24h is a nonspecific process-of-care marker and not a physiologic value. Adds little unique information for predicting creatinine ratio.
LDH measurement frequency at 25–36h remains a proxy for clinician suspicion/complexity rather than kidney injury itself. Unlikely to contribute beyond direct renal markers and hemodynamics.

Average INR in 0–12h reflects early coagulopathy/hepatic dysfunction and transfusion/CPB effects, which are indirect correlates of illness severity. Mean aggregation and early timing make it a weaker, more redundant predictor for AKI than later extremes.
Average INR at 25–36h captures more persistent coagulopathy/organ dysfunction closer to the outcome window. Still indirect for renal injury, but may add some unique severity signal in a sparse model.

Peak INR in 0–12h can capture acute coagulopathy from CPB/transfusion and early shock, but this window is noisy and often transient. Provides limited unique renal prediction compared with later-window INR extremes and direct renal markers.

Peak INR at 25–36h reflects significant ongoing coagulopathy/organ dysfunction and may track systemic hypoperfusion/inflammation associated with AKI. The extreme and later timing make it the most informative INR feature in this batch.

Minimum INR in 0–12h is generally not a meaningful insult marker and is heavily influenced by baseline anticoagulation status and lab variability. Low relevance and dominated by INR_max/mean features.
Minimum INR at 25–36h is rarely the clinically informative direction for postoperative organ dysfunction. Likely redundant/noisy and not a priority for sparse selection.

INR measurement frequency in 0–12h is a monitoring/protocol proxy (bleeding concern, anticoagulation management) rather than a physiologic value. Very low unique predictive value for creatinine ratio.

Counts whether INR was checked in the 13–24h window, reflecting monitoring intensity/bleeding concern rather than renal physiology. As a clinician-behavior proxy it is unlikely to add unique value in a sparse AKI model.

Indicates INR measurement frequency in the 25–36h window, a proxy for ongoing instability/anticoagulation management. Slightly more informative due to closer timing, but still indirect and dominated by true hemodynamic/renal markers.

Average eosinophil percentage early post-op; typically reflects inflammation/stress or steroid effect, not a direct AKI signal. Early timing and weak mechanistic link make it a low-priority sparse feature.

Mean eosinophil percentage in 13–24h; nonspecific and often driven by perioperative steroids or stress leukogram. Unlikely to contribute unique predictive value for creatinine ratio versus stronger renal perfusion/injury features.

Peak eosinophil percentage in 0–12h; extremes here are still usually noise from perioperative immune/steroid effects rather than kidney injury. Redundant with other eosinophil summaries and low relevance to Hour-60 creatinine.

Maximum eosinophil percentage in 13–24h could weakly capture atypical drug reaction/allergic inflammation, which can rarely relate to renal injury. Still nonspecific and likely not selected in a sparse model unless few better predictors exist.
Minimum eosinophil percentage in 0–12h mainly reflects stress response or steroid exposure. Minimal direct renal signal and highly redundant within the eosinophil feature family.

Minimum eosinophil percentage in 13–24h is a nonspecific marker of stress/steroid effect. Low expected incremental value for predicting creatinine ratio compared with hemodynamics, urine output, or renal labs.

Whether eosinophils were measured in 0–12h is essentially a CBC-ordering proxy and near-routine in post-cardiac surgery care. Provides little unique information and is dominated by actual physiologic values.
Measurement indicator for eosinophils in 13–24h reflects lab-ordering frequency rather than kidney status. Low sparse-selection priority as a monitoring proxy.

Whether eosinophils were checked in 25–36h may weakly proxy ongoing complications prompting repeat CBCs. Closer timing gives slight value, but it remains indirect and unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Binary indicator that LDL was measured early post-op, reflecting chronic risk assessment or medication reconciliation rather than acute renal physiology. Very unlikely to add predictive value for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
LDL measurement in 13–24h is a clinician-behavior proxy and not mechanistically tied to evolving AKI. Low priority for sparse modeling.
LDL measurement in 25–36h remains a non-renal, non-acute lab-ordering signal. Even with closer timing, it is unlikely to contribute unique predictive information.

Mean MCV in 0–12h reflects baseline anemia/macrocytosis or transfusion effects, which may correlate with frailty and illness severity. It is indirect for AKI and early/noisy, so only a weak candidate.

Mean MCV in 13–24h captures anemia phenotype/transfusion milieu and may weakly proxy comorbidity burden. Still indirect and likely dominated by more proximal renal perfusion/injury variables in a sparse model.

Mean corpuscular volume (MCV) reflects RBC size and anemia/nutrition/transfusion context rather than renal physiology. The 25–36h mean is the best-timed version but still an indirect, low-uniqueness predictor for creatinine_ratio.
Early post-op peak MCV is largely driven by hemodilution/transfusion and lab variability immediately after bypass. It is weakly related to AKI risk and is dominated by later-window summaries.

Peak MCV in 13–24h mainly captures transfusion/volume status rather than kidney injury. Adds little unique value for sparse selection compared with more direct renal perfusion/biomarker features (not in this batch).
Later peak MCV may reflect ongoing transfusion or macrocytosis and is closer to the outcome window. Still indirect for renal dysfunction and likely redundant with the mean/min MCV in the same epoch.
Early low MCV is unlikely to change acutely and is confounded by immediate post-op fluid shifts and sampling. It has minimal direct linkage to evolving AKI and is low priority in a sparse model.
Minimum MCV in 13–24h is mostly a hematologic/transfusion signal rather than renal hypoperfusion or injury. Unlikely to survive sparse selection given weak mechanistic proximity to creatinine rise.

Minimum MCV at 25–36h could proxy ongoing hemodilution/bleeding/transfusion burden, which may correlate with illness severity. Still indirect and redundant within the MCV family, so only modest priority.
Count of MCV measurements is primarily a monitoring-intensity/clinician-behavior proxy. In the immediate 0–12h period it is especially routine and noisy, offering little unique predictive value.
MCV measurement frequency reflects lab ordering patterns rather than physiology. It is a weak, indirect proxy for complexity and is unlikely to add value in a sparse linear model.

More frequent CBCs at 25–36h may indicate ongoing instability/bleeding/transfusion needs, which can correlate with AKI risk. Still a proxy feature and should be deprioritized versus physiologic extremes.

Mean platelet volume (MPV) can reflect platelet activation/inflammation and postoperative stress but is not a direct renal marker. The early 0–12h mean is prone to peri-bypass noise and is low-to-moderate priority.
MPV mean in 13–24h may weakly track systemic inflammation/severity that correlates with AKI. It remains indirect and likely replaceable by stronger hemodynamic/renal features.

MPV mean at 25–36h is the best-timed summary and may capture sustained inflammatory/consumptive physiology associated with AKI risk. Among MPV variants, the later mean can add modest unique signal, though still indirect.

Early peak MPV may reflect acute platelet activation but is affected by immediate post-op variability and lab artifact. It is an indirect severity marker and is dominated by later-window MPV summaries.
Peak MPV in 13–24h could capture inflammatory extremes, but its relationship to creatinine rise is nonspecific. Likely redundant with the 25–36h MPV measures and not a top sparse-model keeper.

Peak MPV at 25–36h captures later extremes of platelet activation/inflammation, which may correlate with evolving organ dysfunction including AKI. Within this batch it is one of the more plausible indirect predictors, though still not kidney-specific.

Minimum mean platelet volume (MPV) in the immediate post-op window is a nonspecific hematologic/inflammatory signal. It is weakly and indirectly related to AKI risk and unlikely to survive sparse selection versus hemodynamics, lactate, or renal markers.

MPV minimum at 13–24h remains a nonspecific marker of platelet activation/inflammation. It offers little unique predictive value for 60h creatinine ratio and is likely dominated by stronger perfusion/organ dysfunction features.

Later (25–36h) MPV minimum is closer to the outcome and could reflect ongoing systemic inflammation or consumptive processes. Still, it is indirect for kidney injury and typically redundant with more proximal perfusion/illness-severity signals.
Count/frequency of MPV measurements in 0–12h is primarily a monitoring-intensity/clinician-behavior proxy. It is not a physiologic renal signal and is low priority for a sparse model.
MPV measurement frequency at 13–24h mainly reflects lab ordering patterns and perceived complexity. This proxy is unlikely to add stable, unique predictive value for creatinine ratio.
MPV measurement frequency at 25–36h is still a clinician-behavior proxy rather than a biomarker. Even though later timing is closer to Hour 60, it remains low-value for sparse selection.

Mean lactate in 0–12h reflects early global hypoperfusion/low cardiac output after surgery, which can contribute to AKI. However, early post-bypass lactate can be noisy and the mean can dilute short insults, so it is moderate priority.

Mean lactate at 13–24h captures sustained hypoperfusion/ongoing shock physiology after initial post-op noise has settled. This is a strong, relatively unique precursor of AKI risk and likely to survive sparse screening.

Peak lactate in 0–12h captures the worst early hypoperfusion/oxygen debt, which is mechanistically linked to renal ischemic injury. Despite early-window noise, the extreme is more informative than the mean/min and is a strong candidate within this batch.

Peak lactate at 13–24h is a high-value marker of ongoing or recurrent hypoperfusion after the immediate post-op period. As an extreme close to the evolving trajectory, it is one of the best sparse predictors here for later creatinine rise.

Minimum lactate in 0–12h largely reflects periods of adequate perfusion and is less informative than peaks for AKI prediction. It is redundant given lactate max/mean and low priority for sparse selection.

Minimum lactate at 13–24h may indicate recovery, but it is generally less predictive of renal injury than persistent elevation or peaks. It is likely redundant with lactate mean/max and thus lower priority.

Lactate measurement frequency in 0–12h is mostly a proxy for instability and clinician concern rather than a direct physiologic value. It may add a small amount of context but is dominated by actual lactate levels.
Lactate measurement frequency at 13–24h reflects ongoing monitoring for shock/low output. It is an indirect proxy and typically adds limited incremental value beyond lactate max/mean.

Lactate measurement frequency at 25–36h can signal persistent concern for hypoperfusion closer to the outcome window. Still a proxy, but later timing may provide incremental information when actual lactate values are not included for this epoch.
