Mean alkaline phosphatase in 0–12h reflects baseline cholestasis/bone turnover more than acute renal physiology. It is weakly related to AKI and unlikely to be selected in a sparse model versus perfusion markers like lactate.

Peak alkaline phosphatase reflects cholestasis/liver/bone signal rather than renal function. Early post-op and unlikely to add unique value for AKI prediction versus hemodynamics/renal labs, so low sparse-selection priority.
Minimum alkaline phosphatase is not a meaningful physiologic insult marker for kidney injury. Redundant/non-informative for creatinine trajectory and early timing further limits utility.
Count of ALP measurements mainly reflects monitoring/ordering behavior rather than physiology. Weak, proxy-heavy signal and early window makes it unlikely to survive sparse selection.
ALP measurement frequency in 13–24h is still primarily a clinician-behavior proxy. Not a direct renal signal and unlikely to add unique predictive value in a sparse model.

Later ALP measurement frequency may weakly proxy evolving complications prompting broader lab surveillance. Still indirect and non-renal, but closer timing gives slightly more potential signal than earlier epochs.

Mean RDW reflects baseline anisocytosis/chronic illness and perioperative inflammation rather than acute renal hypoperfusion. Early window and redundancy with later RDW summaries make it a weak sparse-model candidate.
RDW mean in 13–24h is a nonspecific severity/frailty marker with limited mechanistic linkage to AKI. Likely dominated by later (25–36h) RDW if any RDW feature is kept.

Later RDW mean may capture sustained inflammation/hemolysis/transfusion effects associated with worse outcomes including AKI. It is still nonspecific, but the 25–36h timing makes it the most plausible RDW mean for sparse selection.
Max RDW in 0–12h may reflect transfusion/hemolysis or baseline variability but is nonspecific for kidney injury. Early timing and redundancy with later maxima limit its priority.
Max RDW in 13–24h is a nonspecific marker of illness/transfusion burden. Some potential association with complications, but likely replaceable by the closer 25–36h extreme.

Peak RDW in 25–36h may better capture ongoing transfusion/hemolysis/inflammatory stress that correlates with subsequent AKI risk. Among RDW variants, this extreme in the closest window is the best sparse-model candidate.
Minimum RDW is not an interpretable insult marker and adds little beyond baseline hematologic status. Early timing and poor mechanistic relevance to AKI make it very low priority.
RDW minimum in 13–24h is unlikely to carry meaningful predictive information for creatinine rise. Dominated by more informative summaries (e.g., max/mean) if RDW is used at all.
RDW minimum in 25–36h remains non-informative and largely redundant with other RDW aggregations. Not a plausible sparse-selection feature for predicting creatinine ratio.
RDW measurement count is essentially CBC frequency and reflects monitoring intensity rather than renal physiology. Early window and proxy nature make it unlikely to add unique predictive value.
CBC/RDW measurement frequency in 13–24h is a clinician-behavior proxy for acuity. It is indirect and typically dominated by physiologic variables, so low sparse-model priority.

Counts whether RDW was checked in 25–36h, reflecting monitoring intensity rather than renal physiology. Likely a clinician-behavior proxy with minimal unique value for a sparse AKI/creatinine-ratio model.

Average chloride in the immediate post-op window, influenced by bypass/fluids and early shifts. Some signal for saline load/acidosis risk but early timing and redundancy with later chloride summaries limit sparse-model priority.

Average chloride during 13–24h reflects ongoing fluid/acid-base management. Still a smoothed metric and generally weaker than later (25–36h) or extreme chloride values for capturing renal-relevant insults.

Average chloride closest to the prediction horizon, capturing sustained hyperchloremia from resuscitation that can correlate with AKI risk. Mean is still less sensitive than extremes but may add unique value if chloride variability is limited.

Peak chloride in 0–12h may reflect large early saline exposure but is confounded by immediate post-op physiology. Early extremes are noisier and likely dominated by later chloride measures for Hour-60 creatinine prediction.

Peak chloride in 13–24h better reflects evolving hyperchloremia after initial stabilization. An extreme can capture a potentially nephrotoxic/acidotic exposure and may survive sparse selection over mean values.

Peak chloride in 25–36h is the closest extreme measure and best captures significant hyperchloremia near-term, which can track with acid-base disturbance and AKI risk. Among chloride features, this is the most likely to add unique predictive value in a sparse model.

Lowest chloride in the immediate post-op period is often driven by dilutional effects and lab/volume shifts. It is less mechanistically tied to AKI than hyperchloremia and is early/noisy, so low sparse-model priority.
Minimum chloride in 13–24h is an indirect marker and typically less informative for renal injury than high chloride extremes. Likely redundant and weak for predicting Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Minimum chloride in 25–36h may reflect dilution or diuretic/volume status but has a weaker and less consistent link to AKI than hyperchloremia. Some proximity benefit, but still lower priority than chloride_max.

Number/indicator of chloride measurements in 0–12h mainly reflects monitoring intensity and routine post-op labs. It is a proxy variable with little direct renal signal and high redundancy with standard care patterns.
Measurement frequency/indicator for chloride in 13–24h is largely clinician behavior and illness-complexity proxy. Unlikely to add stable, unique predictive value beyond actual chloride values.

More frequent chloride checks in 25–36h may indicate ongoing instability or acid-base management, indirectly associated with AKI risk. Still primarily a monitoring proxy, so only modest sparse-model value.

Whether HDL was measured early post-op reflects ordering behavior (often non-ICU routine panels) rather than kidney physiology. Very unlikely to contribute unique predictive signal for creatinine ratio.
HDL measurement in 13–24h is a clinician/workflow proxy with minimal mechanistic connection to acute creatinine rise. Low priority for sparse selection.
HDL measurement in 25–36h remains a non-renal monitoring/order-set artifact rather than a physiologic AKI precursor. Provides negligible unique value for predicting Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Average neutrophil percentage reflects early postoperative inflammatory/stress response. It is an indirect AKI signal and likely redundant with other severity markers; early (0–12h) values are noisy for Hour-60 creatinine.
Mean neutrophil percentage in 13–24h captures ongoing inflammation/infection risk. Still indirect for renal dysfunction and unlikely to survive sparse selection versus hemodynamics/renal markers.
Peak neutrophil percentage may indicate acute stress/inflammation. However it is nonspecific for AKI and early postoperative peaks are common and noisy, limiting unique predictive value.

A higher peak neutrophil percentage in 13–24h may better reflect evolving infection/SIRS that can contribute to AKI. Among neutrophil summaries, the max is the most informative, but it remains an indirect proxy.

Minimum neutrophil percentage is rarely a meaningful renal-risk signal post-cardiac surgery and can reflect lab variability or dilution. It is dominated by more interpretable inflammatory extremes (e.g., max) and other physiologic predictors.
Minimum neutrophil percentage in 13–24h is not a strong mechanistic precursor of AKI and is hard to interpret clinically. It is unlikely to add unique value in a sparse model.

Count/frequency of neutrophil measurements is mainly a monitoring-intensity/clinician-behavior proxy. It is not a direct physiologic renal signal and is especially noisy in the immediate post-op window.
Measurement frequency reflects care intensity or concern rather than kidney physiology. It is unlikely to be selected when actual lab values and hemodynamics are available.
Later (25–36h) measurement frequency may weakly proxy clinical deterioration closer to the outcome time. Still, it is a behavior/monitoring signal and generally dominated by physiologic variables.

Mean albumin reflects oncotic status, capillary leak, and dilution from fluids/transfusions, which relate to AKI risk. Useful but early and somewhat confounded by perioperative fluid shifts, so not top-tier.
Maximum albumin in 0–12h is usually less informative than low values and can reflect timing of draws rather than physiology. It is dominated by albumin minima/means for renal-risk signal.

Minimum albumin in 0–12h captures the worst hypoalbuminemia/dilution/capillary leak state, which correlates with AKI risk and fluid overload. Within the albumin family, the min best preserves the clinically relevant extreme signal for sparse selection.

Albumin measurement count is primarily a proxy for monitoring intensity and ordering behavior. It provides little direct information about renal physiology and is unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Frequency of albumin checks reflects clinician concern or protocolized care rather than kidney injury. It is a weak, indirect proxy and typically redundant with actual albumin values and other severity markers.

Later measurement frequency may weakly indicate ongoing instability or fluid/oncotic management closer to Hour 60. Still a proxy variable and generally inferior to albumin level extremes for sparse modeling.

Whether triglycerides were measured is almost entirely clinician-driven (e.g., nutrition/propofol monitoring) and not a direct AKI precursor. It is unlikely to add unique predictive value for creatinine ratio.

Indicates whether triglycerides were checked in the 13–24h window (monitoring/clinician-behavior proxy). It has little direct mechanistic link to AKI and is unlikely to add unique value in a sparse model.

Indicates whether triglycerides were checked in the 25–36h window (monitoring intensity proxy). Even though closer to Hour 60, it remains indirect and dominated by true renal perfusion/function variables not present in this batch.

Average hemoglobin in the immediate post-op period reflects anemia/hemodilution and bleeding/transfusion context. Early means are noisy after bypass and are generally less predictive than later or extreme anemia measures for Hour 60 creatinine ratio.

Average hemoglobin at 13–24h reflects ongoing anemia/hemodilution. Mean is a smoothed signal and is likely redundant with the corresponding minimum hemoglobin, which better captures renal hypoxia risk.

Average hemoglobin at 25–36h captures more stable anemia exposure closer to the outcome window. Still somewhat redundant with hemoglobin_min, but timing makes it a more plausible sparse-model candidate than earlier means.

Peak hemoglobin in 0–12h often reflects hemoconcentration/transfusion timing rather than renal insult. It is a weak and noisy predictor for later creatinine changes and is dominated by anemia extremes (min) and later windows.

Peak hemoglobin in 13–24h is more reflective of transfusion/volume status than kidney injury risk. It is unlikely to survive sparse selection given redundancy and weaker pathophysiologic linkage than hemoglobin_min.

Peak hemoglobin in 25–36h may reflect hemoconcentration/diuresis or transfusion but is not a direct AKI signal. Some potential association via volume status exists, yet it remains secondary and redundant versus hemoglobin_min/mean.

Nadir hemoglobin in 0–12h captures acute anemia/hemodilution which can contribute to renal hypoxia. However, early post-op nadirs are often transient/noisy, so it’s a moderate rather than top sparse-model feature.

Nadir hemoglobin in 13–24h reflects sustained or evolving anemia after initial bypass effects, which can worsen renal oxygen delivery. As an extreme measure, it is more informative than mean/max and a strong sparse-model candidate within this batch.

Nadir hemoglobin in 25–36h is the closest extreme anemia signal to the Hour 60 creatinine ratio and best captures ongoing low oxygen-carrying capacity. Within this batch it is the most pathophysiologically relevant and least redundant hemoglobin summary for sparse selection.

Count/frequency of hemoglobin measurements in 0–12h is primarily a monitoring intensity/acuity proxy. It adds little unique physiologic information beyond the hemoglobin values themselves and is unlikely to be selected sparsely.

Hemoglobin measurement frequency in 13–24h reflects clinician concern, bleeding, or instability rather than kidney function directly. This proxy is typically dominated by actual hemoglobin extremes and other severity markers.

Hemoglobin measurement frequency in 25–36h may weakly proxy ongoing bleeding/transfusion or instability closer to the outcome. Still largely a behavior/monitoring signal and usually redundant with hemoglobin_min/mean.
Whether TEG fibrinogen activity was measured early post-op is mainly a bleeding/coagulopathy workup proxy. It is indirect for AKI and unlikely to add unique predictive value for creatinine ratio.
Whether TEG fibrinogen activity was measured at 13–24h reflects clinician concern for coagulopathy/bleeding. This is a weak, indirect proxy for renal outcomes and not a priority for sparse selection.

Indicates whether TEG fibrinogen activity was checked (monitoring/bleeding-management intensity) in the 25–36h window. This is primarily clinician-behavior/procedural complexity and is unlikely to add unique signal for Hour-60 creatinine beyond hemodynamics/renal markers.

Average ACT in the immediate post-op period reflects anticoagulation/heparin reversal management rather than renal physiology. Mean in 0–12h is noisy and redundant with extremes, with weak direct linkage to later creatinine rise.

Peak ACT may capture excessive anticoagulation or prolonged bypass/heparin effect early after surgery. Still largely a procedural/management proxy and early-timing noise, so low sparse-model priority.

Minimum ACT mainly reflects adequacy of reversal/return toward baseline coagulation early post-op. It is not a direct renal injury signal and is dominated by more kidney-relevant perfusion/lab features.

Frequency of ACT measurement in 0–12h is a monitoring-intensity proxy (e.g., ongoing bleeding/OR return) rather than physiology. In a sparse model it is unlikely to add stable, unique predictive value for creatinine_ratio.

ACT measurement count in 13–24h reflects continued anticoagulation/bleeding management and clinician concern. Still a proxy feature with weak mechanistic connection to AKI compared with hemodynamics/renal labs.

ACT measurement frequency closer to the target window may indicate ongoing complications (bleeding, ECMO/assist devices) that correlate with AKI risk. However it remains indirect and behavior-driven, so only modest priority.
Mean AST in 0–12h reflects early hepatic/ischemic injury burden and systemic hypoperfusion. It is indirect for kidney injury and early post-op values can be transient, so limited sparse-model value.

Peak AST in 0–12h can capture ischemic hepatitis/low-flow states from bypass or shock, which are associated with subsequent AKI. Among AST aggregations, the max best preserves the insult signal, but timing is still early and indirect.

Minimum AST is not clinically informative for hypoperfusion/organ injury and mostly reflects baseline/measurement noise. It is dominated by max/trajectory-type signals and unlikely to survive sparse selection.
AST measurement frequency in 0–12h is largely routine post-op lab practice and/or monitoring intensity. It is a weak proxy and not a direct biomarker for predicting Hour-60 creatinine_ratio.

AST measurement count in 13–24h mainly reflects clinician monitoring patterns rather than organ dysfunction magnitude. As a proxy variable it is unlikely to add unique predictive value in a sparse linear model.

More frequent AST checks at 25–36h may indicate evolving multi-organ dysfunction or complications, which can correlate with AKI risk. Still indirect and behavior-driven, so only modest inclusion priority.

Mean total protein early post-op reflects hemodilution, capillary leak, and nutritional/inflammatory status, which can relate to fluid balance and renal congestion. It is indirect and early, so low-to-moderate sparse-model utility.

Maximum total protein in 0–12h is not a strong pathophysiologic marker for AKI and is influenced by sampling/hemoconcentration variability. It is unlikely to add unique predictive signal for creatinine_ratio.

Minimum total protein in 0–12h can better capture hemodilution/capillary leak and large-volume resuscitation, which may precede renal congestion and AKI. Within this family, the min is the most informative extreme, though still indirect and early.

Count/frequency of total protein labs in the first 12h, mainly reflecting monitoring intensity rather than renal physiology. Weak and proxy-heavy, and early timing is noisy for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
Measurement frequency of total protein in 13–24h is a clinician-behavior/acuity proxy, not a direct kidney signal. Unlikely to survive sparse selection versus true hemodynamic/renal markers.

Later (25–36h) measurement frequency may weakly reflect evolving complications prompting labs, but still mostly a monitoring proxy. Slightly more informative due to temporal proximity, yet low unique value for a sparse model.

Whether HbA1c was checked early post-op reflects preexisting diabetes assessment/availability rather than acute renal trajectory. Mostly redundant with comorbidity history and not time-varying for AKI prediction.
HbA1c measurement flag in 13–24h is a workflow/availability signal, not a physiologic driver of near-term creatinine rise. Very unlikely to add unique predictive value in a sparse model.
HbA1c measurement flag at 25–36h remains a clinician-behavior proxy and does not capture evolving renal dysfunction. Redundant with baseline diabetes status and low priority for sparse selection.

Mean serum bicarbonate/total CO2 in 0–12h reflects acid-base status influenced by bypass, ventilation, and fluids. Early averages are noisy and likely dominated by later CO2 extremes for predicting AKI-related acidosis.

Mean CO2 at 13–24h can reflect developing metabolic acidosis from hypoperfusion or renal dysfunction. Moderately useful but still smoothed and potentially redundant with CO2 minima/maxima and later window values.
Mean CO2 at 25–36h is closer to the outcome and may capture sustained acidosis associated with evolving AKI. Still less sensitive than the minimum CO2 for acute insults, so moderate priority.
Maximum CO2 in 0–12h often reflects transient alkalosis/ventilation effects rather than renal injury. Early and less pathophysiologically tied to AKI than low CO2, so low sparse-model value.
CO2 maximum in 13–24h is generally less informative for AKI than low CO2 (acidosis) and may reflect ventilator/diuretic effects. Likely redundant and low priority.

Later CO2 maximum could reflect contraction alkalosis/diuresis patterns, indirectly related to volume management, but is not a direct AKI signal. Some proximity benefit, yet typically dominated by CO2 minimum/mean for renal risk.
Minimum CO2 in 0–12h captures early metabolic acidosis from shock/hypoperfusion, which can precede AKI. However, immediate post-op period is noisy, so it’s a moderate rather than top sparse feature.

Minimum CO2 at 13–24h is a strong marker of clinically meaningful metabolic acidosis from hypoperfusion/sepsis or impaired renal acid handling. Extremes preserve insult signal and this timing is more predictive for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Minimum CO2 at 25–36h is a near-term, high-signal indicator of ongoing metabolic acidosis consistent with evolving AKI or persistent hypoperfusion. As the closest extreme aggregation, it has high unique value and should be a top sparse-model candidate.

Frequency of CO2 measurements in 0–12h reflects monitoring intensity/instability rather than the CO2 value itself. Slightly informative as an acuity proxy but generally dominated by actual CO2 minima in later windows.
Count/frequency of CO2 measurements in 13–24h is mainly a monitoring-intensity/acuity proxy. It is indirect for AKI risk and unlikely to add unique value in a sparse model.

CO2 measurement frequency in 25–36h may reflect evolving instability closer to the outcome window. Still largely clinician-behavior/monitoring proxy and typically dominated by actual gas values/hemodynamics.
Whether BNP was checked in 0–12h reflects clinician concern for heart failure/volume status rather than renal physiology. Early post-op ordering behavior is noisy and weak for sparse selection.

BNP ordered in 13–24h can indicate suspected congestion/low-output state that may contribute to AKI. However it remains a testing-proxy and is less informative than BNP values or direct renal/hemodynamic features.

BNP ordering in 25–36h is a closer-in-time proxy for concern about cardiac dysfunction/volume overload that can affect kidneys. Still indirect and likely redundant with objective vitals/pressors/diuresis and BNP level if available.
Mean PaCO2 in 0–12h reflects ventilation/anesthesia effects and early respiratory management. It is not a direct renal signal and early averages are noisy for predicting 60h creatinine ratio.

Mean PaCO2 in 13–24h may capture sustained hypoventilation/acidosis burden that can correlate with severity of illness. Still an indirect marker and usually dominated by more kidney-specific/hemodynamic predictors.

Mean PaCO2 in 25–36h is temporally closer and may reflect ongoing ventilatory failure/acidemia associated with worse outcomes. Nonetheless it is a nonspecific severity signal with limited unique renal predictive value.
Peak PaCO2 in 0–12h often reflects transient perioperative hypoventilation or immediate post-bypass effects. Early extremes are less reliable for later AKI prediction and are not kidney-specific.
Max PaCO2 in 13–24h captures episodes of hypercapnia that may indicate respiratory failure and physiologic stress. It is still indirect for AKI and likely secondary to other stronger predictors.

Max PaCO2 in 25–36h captures late hypercapnic episodes closer to the 60h creatinine outcome and may reflect significant ongoing critical illness. Among PaCO2 summaries, this extreme is more likely to add signal than means/mins, though still nonspecific.
Minimum PaCO2 in 0–12h often reflects transient hyperventilation/ventilator settings immediately post-op. It is weakly related to renal outcomes and early-window noise limits sparse-model value.
Minimum PaCO2 in 13–24h is usually driven by ventilator management rather than renal pathophysiology. It is a weak, indirect severity proxy and redundant with other gas/ventilation features.

Minimum PaCO2 in 25–36h may reflect aggressive ventilation or compensation for metabolic acidosis, which can accompany AKI. Still indirect and generally less informative than hypercapnic extremes or direct acid-base/renal markers.
Frequency of PaCO2 measurements in 0–12h mainly reflects routine post-op monitoring and clinician behavior. It provides little unique physiologic information for predicting 60h creatinine ratio.

PaCO2 measurement count in 13–24h is largely a proxy for monitoring intensity/instability rather than a renal mechanism. Unlikely to survive sparse selection when actual gas values and hemodynamics are available.
Count/frequency of PaCO2 measurements in 25–36h is mainly a monitoring-intensity/ventilation-management proxy. It is indirect for AKI and unlikely to add unique value in a sparse model.
Whether indirect bilirubin was checked in 0–12h reflects clinician concern/monitoring rather than a renal biomarker. Early post-op timing and proxy nature make it low priority for sparse selection.

Indicates indirect bilirubin was measured in 13–24h, mostly capturing monitoring intensity or hepatic/hemolysis workup. It is an indirect severity proxy and unlikely to survive sparse screening for creatinine_ratio.

Measurement of indirect bilirubin in 25–36h may reflect evolving hemolysis/hepatic dysfunction and overall complexity closer to the outcome window. Still largely a clinician-behavior proxy rather than a direct renal signal, so only modest priority.

Average calcium in 0–12h is influenced by transfusion/citrate, albumin shifts, and replacement protocols immediately post-bypass. Mean early values are noisy and redundant with later/extreme calcium summaries for AKI prediction.

Mean calcium in 13–24h may reflect ongoing derangements (e.g., citrate load, critical illness) but is still indirect for renal dysfunction. Likely dominated by more informative extremes and the closer 25–36h window.

Mean calcium in 25–36h captures sustained electrolyte/acid-base management and physiologic stability closer to Hour 60. It is still an indirect AKI marker, but timing makes it a reasonable mid-tier sparse candidate.

Peak calcium in 0–12h is often driven by supplementation and peri-bypass shifts rather than kidney injury. Early extreme is less reliable for Hour 60 creatinine_ratio and redundant with later calcium features.

Peak calcium in 13–24h may reflect aggressive replacement or metabolic disturbance but remains nonspecific for AKI. Provides limited unique predictive value compared with later window and calcium minima.

Peak calcium in 25–36h is closer to the outcome but typically reflects treatment decisions more than renal pathophysiology. Less informative than calcium minima for capturing physiologic stress relevant to AKI.
Lowest calcium in 0–12h can indicate citrate effect, hemodilution, or severe illness, which may correlate with later AKI. However, early timing is noisy and likely redundant with later minima.

Systemic lidocaine early post-op is typically for analgesia/antiarrhythmic use and not a direct renal signal. The 0–12h window is noisy and this is likely redundant with broader severity/arrhythmia markers not in this batch.

Later systemic lidocaine may correlate with ongoing arrhythmia/pain but remains a clinician-behavior proxy. Not a strong or unique predictor of subsequent creatinine change.

ACE inhibitor exposure can reduce glomerular filtration pressure and may worsen creatinine in susceptible patients. However, immediate post-op dosing is uncommon/variable and 0–12h is noisy, limiting sparse-model value.

ACE inhibitor administration in this window may indicate hemodynamic stability and can contribute to creatinine rise via efferent arteriolar dilation. Moderately useful but partly a treatment/stability proxy and redundant with ARB signals.

ACE inhibitor exposure closest to the prediction horizon can have a near-term effect on creatinine and also signals recovery/stability trajectory. Stronger sparse-selection candidate within this medication batch due to timing and plausible causal link.

ARB exposure can increase creatinine in the setting of renal hypoperfusion, but early post-op dosing is inconsistent and often withheld. Likely weaker and noisier than later epochs for predicting Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
ARB dosing may both affect creatinine and reflect a clinician assessment of stability. Moderately informative but overlaps heavily with ACE inhibitor exposure as a shared RAAS-inhibition signal.

ARB exposure in the 25–36h window is temporally close and can plausibly influence creatinine trajectory by Hour 60. High priority within this batch, though still somewhat redundant with lisinopril as a RAAS-inhibition marker.
Magnesium sulfate repletion early post-op is common and mainly reflects electrolyte management/arrhythmia prophylaxis. Weak, nonspecific, and unlikely to add unique predictive value for AKI.
Ongoing magnesium sulfate dosing is typically protocolized replacement and a proxy for monitoring/electrolyte abnormalities. Not a direct renal function marker and low sparse-model priority.

Later magnesium sulfate requirement could weakly reflect impaired renal clearance or ongoing electrolyte derangements, but it is still largely replacement-driven. Slightly higher due to timing, yet remains nonspecific and redundant with other severity signals.
Magnesium (non-sulfate) dosing early post-op is routine electrolyte repletion and not kidney-specific. High redundancy with magnesium sulfate dosing and low value for sparse selection.

Magnesium replacement in 13–24h is common ICU care and mainly reflects electrolyte management rather than evolving AKI. Redundant with other magnesium formulations and unlikely to be selected in a sparse model.

Total magnesium replacement in the 25–36h window, usually reflecting electrolyte repletion practices and arrhythmia prophylaxis. Only weakly and indirectly related to AKI risk (and often driven by routine protocols), so low sparse-model priority.
Early postoperative melatonin use is a sleep/delirium management signal. It is largely unrelated to renal physiology and is mostly a care-pattern proxy, especially noisy in 0–12h.
Melatonin dosing in 13–24h reflects sedation/sleep management rather than kidney injury. Adds little unique predictive value for creatinine trajectory in a sparse linear model.
Melatonin dosing closest to the target window still primarily captures sleep/delirium treatment patterns. Minimal direct renal signal and unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Meropenem exposure early post-op is a marker of suspected severe infection or broad empiric coverage. It is an indirect severity/clinician-behavior proxy and early timing is noisy, so limited unique value for predicting Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Meropenem dosing in 13–24h suggests ongoing concern for infection/sepsis, which can precipitate AKI. Still largely a treatment/acuity proxy (not a renal biomarker), but may add some incremental signal beyond vitals/labs.

Meropenem use in 25–36h is closer to Hour 60 and may reflect persistent sepsis/complications associated with evolving AKI. Remains indirect and potentially redundant with physiologic severity markers, so moderate priority.
Methocarbamol use early post-op reflects muscle spasm/pain management. It has little mechanistic link to renal dysfunction and is unlikely to contribute unique predictive value.
Methocarbamol dosing in 13–24h is primarily an analgesia adjunct signal. It is not a meaningful renal predictor and would be dominated by true hemodynamic/renal markers.
Methocarbamol dosing in 25–36h remains a pain/spasm treatment proxy. Minimal direct or unique association with AKI risk for sparse model inclusion.

Methylene blue use indicates vasoplegic shock/post-bypass refractory hypotension, a risk state for renal hypoperfusion. Early (0–12h) instability can be transient/noisy, but the signal is relatively specific for severe shock physiology.

Methylene blue in 13–24h suggests persistent/refractory vasoplegia requiring rescue therapy, strongly linked to ongoing hypoperfusion risk for AKI. More informative timing and relatively unique severity signal, making it a strong sparse-model candidate.

Methylene blue use in 25–36h (closest window) implies continued severe vasoplegia/shock physiology, which is a potent precursor to worsening creatinine by Hour 60. While partly overlapping with vasopressor/hemodynamic features, it can add a distinct ‘refractory shock’ marker.
Metoclopramide use early post-op reflects nausea/gastroparesis management. It is not a renal physiology marker and is mostly routine care/noise in 0–12h.
Metoclopramide in 13–24h is a GI symptom treatment proxy. It offers negligible unique information for predicting creatinine ratio in a sparse model.
Metoclopramide dosing in 25–36h remains a nonspecific antiemetic/prokinetic signal. It is unlikely to improve prediction beyond core renal/hemodynamic variables.

Early beta-blocker dosing mainly reflects routine rate control/AF prophylaxis and clinician preference. It is an indirect, early-window signal with little unique linkage to evolving AKI compared with hemodynamics/renal markers.

Metoprolol use in this window may reflect ongoing tachyarrhythmia or hypertension management. Still largely a treatment/behavior proxy and weakly connected to renal hypoperfusion, so unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Later metoprolol dosing can indicate persistent AF/tachycardia control and some stability. Slightly more temporally relevant than earlier epochs but remains an indirect proxy with limited unique predictive value for creatinine rise.

Metronidazole exposure is typically for suspected/treated infection or prophylaxis rather than renal physiology. In the immediate post-op window it is mostly clinician-driven and not a strong AKI predictor.

Antibiotic administration here reflects infection concern/coverage patterns more than kidney injury mechanisms. It is a weak, nonspecific proxy and likely dominated by direct illness-severity and renal/hemodynamic features.

Continued metronidazole by 25–36h may signal evolving infection/sepsis risk, which can contribute to AKI. Still indirect and treatment-pattern dependent, so only a low-priority sparse-model candidate.
Sedative dosing early post-op mainly reflects ventilation/sedation practice and immediate instability. It is a clinician-behavior proxy with high noise in 0–12h and little unique renal signal.

Ongoing midazolam suggests prolonged ventilation/ICU complexity, which correlates with worse outcomes. However it is nonspecific and largely redundant with direct respiratory/hemodynamic severity measures.

Persistent sedation at 25–36h indicates delayed extubation or ongoing critical illness closer to the prediction horizon. While still indirect, its timing makes it a moderately useful severity proxy that may add some unique signal in a sparse model.

Midodrine use reflects attempts to support blood pressure/wean IV vasopressors, relating to hemodynamic instability. Early post-op use is noisy and treatment-driven, so only weakly informative for later creatinine ratio.

Midodrine in 13–24h suggests ongoing vasoplegia or difficulty maintaining MAP, a plausible precursor to renal hypoperfusion. Still partly a management proxy, but more informative than many medication features.

Midodrine requirement at 25–36h indicates persistent hypotension/vasoplegia near the target time, which is mechanistically linked to AKI risk. Among medication proxies, this later-window hemodynamic-support signal is relatively strong for sparse selection.

Milrinone use reflects low cardiac output/right ventricular dysfunction and vasodilation, both relevant to renal perfusion. The 0–12h window is confounded by immediate post-bypass management, limiting uniqueness.

Continued milrinone at 13–24h suggests persistent cardiac dysfunction and potential renal hypoperfusion/congestion risk. This is a relatively direct physiologic severity marker and more likely to survive sparse selection than many other meds.

Milrinone requirement at 25–36h is a strong near-term indicator of ongoing low-output state and/or RV failure, tightly linked to renal hypoperfusion and cardiorenal AKI. Its proximity to Hour 60 and mechanistic relevance make it a top sparse-model candidate in this batch.

Minocycline exposure is primarily an antibiotic choice reflecting infection coverage patterns rather than renal physiology. In 0–12h it is highly clinician-driven and unlikely to add unique predictive value for creatinine_ratio.
Total minocycline given in hours 13–24 post-op. Antibiotic choice/dose is usually a weak, indication-driven proxy and has little direct renal-signal for Hour-60 creatinine ratio in a sparse model.

Total minocycline given in hours 25–36 post-op. Even though closer to the target, it mainly reflects infection concern/ordering behavior rather than kidney physiology and is unlikely to add unique predictive value.

Total morphine administered in the first 12 hours post-op. Opioid dose is an indirect severity/sedation proxy and early post-op dosing is noisy; limited unique value for predicting AKI compared with hemodynamics/renal markers.
Total morphine administered in hours 13–24 post-op. Still largely reflects analgesia/sedation strategy and ventilation status rather than renal perfusion, so it is a weak sparse-selection candidate.
Total morphine administered in hours 25–36 post-op. Closer timing helps slightly, but it remains a clinician-behavior/illness-context proxy with modest incremental renal predictive signal.

Total nicardipine (antihypertensive) dose in the first 12 hours post-op. May reflect BP management and afterload control but is early and highly practice-driven, making it a weak and potentially confounded predictor of later creatinine rise.

Total nicardipine dose in hours 13–24 post-op. Ongoing need for antihypertensive therapy can encode hemodynamic phenotype (e.g., hypertension/vasoconstriction) that may relate to renal perfusion, but it is still indirect and treatment-confounded.

Total nicardipine dose in hours 25–36 post-op. Closest window makes it the best nicardipine summary here, potentially capturing sustained BP management needs, but it remains an indirect proxy compared with direct MAP/pressors/urine output.

Total nitroglycerin dose in the first 12 hours post-op. Often used for BP/ischemia management and can lower preload/BP, but early dosing is noisy and strongly indication-driven, limiting sparse-model priority.

Total nitroglycerin dose in hours 13–24 post-op. Continued vasodilator requirement may reflect hemodynamic management that could affect renal perfusion, but it is treatment-confounded and likely redundant with direct hemodynamic features.

Total nitroglycerin dose in hours 25–36 post-op. Best-timed nitroglycerin feature for capturing ongoing vasodilation/BP strategy, yet still an indirect proxy and typically dominated by measured hypotension/vasopressor needs if available.

Total normal saline volume given in the first 12 hours post-op. Fluid administration relates to perfusion and dilutional creatinine effects, but early post-op fluid shifts are noisy and may be redundant with net fluid balance/urine output.

Total normal saline volume given in hours 13–24 post-op. Reflects ongoing resuscitation/maintenance that can influence renal perfusion and creatinine concentration, but is still a treatment variable and less specific than balance/diuresis metrics.

Total normal saline volume given in hours 25–36 post-op. Closest window and may capture continued resuscitation needs or fluid overload risk affecting renal function and creatinine dilution, making it the strongest saline feature for sparse selection in this batch.
Total nystatin dose in the first 12 hours post-op. Typically prophylaxis or local antifungal use with minimal systemic renal relevance; largely unrelated to AKI prediction.
Total nystatin dose in hours 13–24 post-op. Continues to reflect prophylaxis/ordering behavior rather than kidney physiology and is very unlikely to add unique predictive value in a sparse model.

Nystatin use is typically prophylaxis/treatment for oral/GI fungal colonization rather than a renal physiology signal. It is mainly a care-pattern proxy with little unique value for predicting Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
Ondansetron dose reflects nausea prophylaxis/antiemetic use and peri-anesthesia practice. It is not a direct AKI precursor and is likely noisy in the immediate 0–12h window.
Ondansetron use in 13–24h remains a symptom-management marker rather than renal perfusion or injury. Any association would be indirect and unlikely to survive sparse selection.
Ondansetron in 25–36h is still largely a clinician-behavior/symptom proxy with minimal mechanistic link to AKI. Despite being closer to Hour 60, it adds little unique predictive signal.
Oxycodone dose reflects analgesia needs and sedation strategy, not kidney function. Early post-op dosing is heavily protocol-driven and unlikely to add unique information for creatinine trajectory.
Oxycodone in 13–24h may weakly proxy recovery trajectory (extubation/mobility) and thus overall illness severity. However it is indirect and likely dominated by more direct hemodynamic/renal markers.

Later oxycodone use can reflect ongoing pain control and recovery status, which may correlate with complications. Still, it is a nonspecific proxy and unlikely to be retained in a sparse AKI-focused model.
Pantoprazole is stress-ulcer prophylaxis and largely protocolized in the immediate post-op period. It provides minimal unique signal about renal hypoperfusion or evolving AKI.

Pantoprazole use at 13–24h remains a routine prophylaxis marker with weak linkage to kidney injury. Any predictive value would be confounded by care patterns and redundancy with other severity indicators.
Pantoprazole at 25–36h is still primarily a standing-order medication rather than a physiologic signal. It is unlikely to contribute unique predictive value for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Phenylephrine use indicates vasopressor support and potential hypotension/vasoplegia, which can contribute to renal hypoperfusion. The 0–12h window is noisier post-bypass, so this is a moderate rather than top-tier sparse feature.

Phenylephrine requirement in 13–24h suggests persistent hemodynamic support needs, a stronger precursor of ongoing renal hypoperfusion than immediate post-op use. As a binary exposure it is less granular than dose, but still a strong sparse-model candidate in this batch.

Phenylephrine use in 25–36h is the closest window to the Hour-60 creatinine outcome and captures ongoing vasopressor dependence, a key driver/proxy of renal hypoperfusion and evolving AKI. Within this medication-heavy batch, it is the most uniquely informative feature for sparse selection.

Phosphate supplementation can reflect hypophosphatemia from shifts/refeeding or renal losses, but early post-op electrolyte replacement is common and protocol-driven. It is an indirect and noisy marker for later creatinine rise.
Phosphate replacement in 13–24h may weakly indicate metabolic stress or renal handling abnormalities. However it is still indirect and likely redundant with more direct renal/hemodynamic predictors.

Phosphate supplementation in 25–36h may better reflect persistent electrolyte derangements and reduced renal reserve/handling closer to the outcome window. It remains indirect, but could add modest unique signal beyond routine-care meds in this batch.

Early broad-spectrum antibiotic exposure is mainly a proxy for suspected infection/complexity rather than a direct renal signal. The 0–12h window is noisy and dosing is largely protocol-driven, so it’s unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Continued piperacillin-tazobactam suggests ongoing infection concern and higher illness severity, which can correlate with AKI risk indirectly. Still largely clinician-behavior/proxy and not kidney-specific, so low sparse-model priority.

Antibiotic continuation closer to the target time may better reflect evolving sepsis/inflammation that can drive AKI by Hour 60. Among the three epochs this is the most informative, but it remains an indirect proxy and likely dominated by hemodynamics/labs if available.

Early balanced crystalloid volume reflects resuscitation and fluid shifts immediately post-op, which are often transient and protocolized. It’s an indirect renal perfusion/volume proxy and likely redundant with net fluid balance or hemodynamic features.

Ongoing crystalloid needs in 13–24h can indicate persistent vasoplegia/third spacing and may relate to renal hypoperfusion risk. Moderately useful but still a treatment proxy and often redundant with blood pressure/vasopressor and fluid balance summaries.

Later crystalloid administration is closer to Hour 60 and may capture continued resuscitation needs that track with evolving AKI risk. However it remains indirect and confounded by clinician practice, so not a top sparse-model keeper.

Polyethylene glycol is a bowel regimen marker and does not directly reflect renal physiology. In 0–12h it mostly captures routine care/feeding status and adds little unique predictive value for creatinine ratio.
Bowel regimen use is primarily a proxy for extubation/enteral tolerance and nursing practice rather than kidney injury. Unlikely to contribute meaningfully in a sparse linear model.
Later PEG dosing still reflects GI management and recovery trajectory more than renal function. Any association with AKI would be weak and highly indirect, so very low selection priority.

Potassium repletion can reflect hypokalemia from diuresis/insulin shifts and indirectly care intensity; it may also relate to renal handling but early post-op shifts are common. Useful but noisy and often redundant with serum potassium and diuretic exposure if present.

KCl dosing in 13–24h may better reflect ongoing electrolyte losses (e.g., diuretics) or renal dysfunction affecting potassium balance. Still a treatment proxy and typically dominated by actual potassium/acid-base labs, so moderate priority.

KCl requirement closer to Hour 60 can capture sustained diuretic-driven losses or evolving renal tubular handling changes, providing a near-term trajectory signal. Among the KCl epochs this is most likely to add unique predictive value if labs/diuretics are not already capturing it.

Dexmedetomidine dose mainly reflects sedation strategy and ventilation status rather than renal physiology. The immediate post-op period is highly protocolized, making this a weak and indirect predictor.

Ongoing dexmedetomidine may indicate prolonged ventilation/delirium risk and overall severity, which can correlate with AKI indirectly. Still largely a clinician-behavior proxy and likely redundant with respiratory/hemodynamic variables.

Later sedation needs can reflect delayed recovery and persistent critical illness closer to the outcome window. However it remains nonspecific and typically dominated by direct renal/hemodynamic markers in sparse selection.

Prednisone exposure in the first 12h is uncommon and usually reflects chronic steroid use or specific indications, not an acute renal injury signal. Any relationship to creatinine ratio is indirect and likely confounded, so low sparse-model value.

Systemic steroid exposure in the 13–24h window, which may reflect inflammation/airway issues or clinician preference. Weak and indirect for AKI prediction and largely a treatment/indication proxy rather than a renal physiology signal.

Steroid exposure closer to the outcome window (25–36h), potentially marking ongoing inflammatory complications. Still an indirect clinician-behavior/indication proxy with limited unique value for sparse selection.

Early postoperative sedative dose, mainly reflecting ventilation/sedation strategy immediately after surgery. Highly confounded and noisy in 0–12h and not a direct renal insult marker, so low priority.

Propofol dose in 13–24h reflects ongoing sedation/ventilation needs and illness severity. Indirect for kidney injury and likely redundant with hemodynamics/ventilator variables, so weak sparse-model candidate.
Later propofol requirement (25–36h) may proxy persistent critical illness and hypotension risk. Still mostly a management proxy and typically dominated by direct MAP/vasopressor/urine output signals.
Protamine administration early post-op is routine reversal of heparin after bypass. Near-universal/standardized exposure with little interpatient signal for later creatinine ratio, so minimal value.
Protamine use in 13–24h is uncommon and would mainly indicate procedural/bleeding management rather than renal physiology. Likely sparse and noisy with limited generalizable predictive value.
Late protamine suggests re-intervention/bleeding management but is rare and highly context-specific. More a procedural proxy than a direct AKI precursor, so low priority for sparse selection.
Early antipsychotic use reflects agitation/delirium management and sedation practices. Indirect and confounded in the immediate post-op window, offering little unique renal predictive signal.
Quetiapine in 13–24h may indicate delirium or difficulty weaning sedation, a general severity marker. Indirect for AKI and likely redundant with more proximal physiologic predictors.
Later quetiapine use (25–36h) can proxy persistent delirium/ICU course complexity. Still not a direct renal insult marker and unlikely to survive sparse selection over hemodynamics/labs.
Neuromuscular blocker dose early post-op reflects ventilator management and operative/anesthesia carryover. Highly practice-driven and early/noisy, with minimal direct linkage to creatinine rise.
Ongoing paralysis in 13–24h suggests severe respiratory failure or ventilator dyssynchrony. Indirect severity proxy and typically redundant with ventilator settings/gas exchange variables.

Paralysis closer to outcome (25–36h) may mark persistent critical illness and higher risk of complications. Still a management proxy with limited unique renal information compared with direct perfusion/renal markers.

Statin administration early post-op mainly reflects home medication continuation and prescribing habits. Weak mechanistic connection to short-term creatinine ratio and low incremental value for sparse modeling.
Continued statin dosing in 13–24h is largely routine and not a near-term AKI signal. Likely low variance and dominated by comorbidity/history features if those are present.

Rosuvastatin exposure in the 25–36h window mainly reflects chronic lipid therapy/resumption of home meds. It has little direct, unique signal for evolving AKI by Hour 60 and is likely noise in sparse selection.
Early post-op bowel regimen dosing is a care-pattern marker rather than renal physiology. Highly indirect and early (0–12h), so unlikely to add unique predictive value for creatinine ratio.
Bowel regimen use in 13–24h reflects constipation prophylaxis and general recovery/feeding status. It is a weak, clinician-behavior proxy with minimal direct linkage to AKI risk.
Later bowel regimen dosing (25–36h) may correlate with extubation/PO tolerance but not kidney injury mechanisms. Any association is indirect and unlikely to survive sparse-model screening.
Senna dosing in 0–12h is routine post-op constipation prophylaxis and not a renal biomarker. Redundant with other bowel-regimen variables and low priority for a sparse model.
Senna in 13–24h is primarily a care-process indicator (bowel regimen intensity). It provides little unique information about renal hypoperfusion or evolving AKI.
Senna in 25–36h may reflect clinical stability and enteral medication administration rather than kidney function. Indirect and redundant with senna/docusate features, so low sparse-selection value.

Sevelamer use suggests hyperphosphatemia/CKD history or early phosphate control, which can correlate with renal dysfunction risk. However it is largely a treatment/indication proxy and early timing reduces specificity for Hour 60 creatinine ratio.

Sevelamer dosing in 13–24h more likely reflects persistent hyperphosphatemia/renal impairment beyond immediate post-op noise. Still an indirect medication proxy, but may add some unique signal about underlying CKD/early AKI trajectory.

Sevelamer in 25–36h is the closest window and may indicate ongoing phosphate derangements consistent with renal dysfunction. It remains an indication/treatment proxy (not a direct biomarker), but among sevelamer features this timing is most informative for sparse selection.
Simethicone is a benign GI symptom medication and does not reflect renal physiology. Any association would be incidental and dominated by more direct hemodynamic/lab features.
Simethicone use in 13–24h is a nonspecific comfort-care marker. It is unlikely to contribute unique predictive value for creatinine ratio in a sparse linear model.
Simethicone in 25–36h reflects GI gas management/PO meds rather than kidney injury. Even with closer timing, it is a weak proxy and low priority for inclusion.

Sodium bicarbonate administration early post-op often reflects metabolic acidosis/hyperkalemia during immediate instability, which can accompany renal hypoperfusion. However 0–12h is noisy and bicarbonate is a clinician-response proxy, limiting sparse-model priority.

Bicarbonate use in 13–24h suggests persistent metabolic acidosis or renal-related electrolyte issues beyond immediate post-bypass effects. As a near-term marker of physiologic derangement linked to AKI risk, it is a strong sparse-model candidate within this batch.

Bicarbonate dosing in 25–36h is close to the outcome window and indicates ongoing acidosis management potentially driven by renal dysfunction or low perfusion. While still treatment/indication-based, it likely carries the most unique predictive signal among these medications.

TPN use in the immediate post-op window is mostly a nutrition/ICU practice marker rather than a direct renal physiology signal. Likely low prevalence and largely redundant with overall illness complexity, so low sparse-model priority.

TPN in 13–24h reflects feeding strategy and perceived inability to use enteral nutrition, not kidney function per se. Mostly a clinician-behavior proxy with weak unique value for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

TPN by 25–36h may indicate prolonged critical illness or GI intolerance, which can correlate with AKI risk indirectly. Still largely a proxy and unlikely to survive sparse selection versus hemodynamics/renal markers, but timing is the most relevant of the three.

Tamsulosin reflects chronic BPH/urinary retention management and is not a direct driver of intrinsic renal dysfunction post-cardiac surgery. Early post-op dosing is often held/variable and adds little unique predictive signal.
Continued tamsulosin use mainly captures baseline outpatient medication reconciliation rather than evolving AKI physiology. Minimal expected incremental value for predicting creatinine ratio at 60h.

Tamsulosin at 25–36h remains a weak, indirect marker (urinary retention/BPH) and is not tightly linked to serum creatinine rise after cardiac surgery. Unlikely to be selected in a sparse linear model.

Tramadol dose is primarily an analgesia choice and may reflect pain/sedation strategy rather than renal hypoperfusion or nephrotoxic exposure. Early post-op dosing is noisy and not a strong unique predictor of AKI trajectory.
Analgesic dosing in 13–24h is more about comfort and care patterns than kidney injury mechanisms. Any association with creatinine is likely confounded and dominated by stronger physiologic features.
By 25–36h, tramadol use can weakly proxy renal function (dose avoidance in AKI) and overall recovery trajectory. Still indirect and clinician-driven, so only modest sparse-model value.

Tranexamic acid indicates antifibrinolytic exposure and indirectly correlates with bleeding/transfusion risk, which can affect renal perfusion. However it is largely protocol-driven and early, so limited unique predictive value.
TXA beyond the immediate operative period is uncommon and more reflective of practice variation than renal physiology. Likely sparse and not a robust predictor of Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
TXA at 25–36h is rare and would mainly signal unusual bleeding management rather than a consistent AKI precursor. Low expected stability and low selection priority.

Vancomycin exposure is a potential nephrotoxin and also marks suspected infection, both relevant to AKI risk. In 0–12h it may be prophylactic and noisy, but still one of the few direct nephrotoxic signals in this batch.

Vancomycin dosing in 13–24h more often reflects ongoing treatment and sustained exposure, which can contribute to creatinine rise. Still partly confounded by infection severity and dosing adjustments, so moderate priority.

Vancomycin exposure closest to the prediction horizon captures ongoing nephrotoxic risk and/or evolving infection, both linked to subsequent creatinine increase. Among these medication features, this timing is most likely to add unique predictive value in a sparse model.

Vasopressin dose reflects vasoplegia/shock and need for vasopressor support, a strong precursor of renal hypoperfusion and AKI after cardiac surgery. Although early (0–12h) can be transient, vasopressor requirement is a high-signal severity/hemodynamic feature likely to survive sparse selection within this batch.

Total vasopressin requirement reflects vasoplegia/shock and potential renal hypoperfusion in the early trajectory window. Useful severity/hemodynamic support signal but partly redundant with norepinephrine-equivalent dosing and less proximal than 25–36h.

Ongoing vasopressin need at 25–36h suggests persistent vasodilatory shock and risk of renal hypoperfusion closer to the Hour-60 creatinine outcome. Adds prognostic value beyond earlier dosing, though still overlaps with norepinephrine-equivalent dose as a general vasopressor-burden marker.

Warfarin dosing is primarily a clinician-behavior/indication marker (e.g., valve/AF) rather than a direct renal physiology signal. In the immediate post-op window it is unlikely to add unique predictive value for AKI beyond comorbidity/procedure context.

Warfarin dose reflects anticoagulation management and is not a near-direct precursor of creatinine rise. Any association is likely confounded by indication and care pathways, making it a low-priority sparse-model feature.

Later warfarin dosing remains an indirect treatment/indication proxy with minimal mechanistic linkage to evolving AKI by Hour 60. Unlikely to survive sparse selection when direct renal/hemodynamic features are available.

Heparin line flush dose mainly captures line maintenance and documentation practices rather than physiology. It is a weak, noisy proxy and not expected to add unique predictive signal for creatinine ratio.
Line flush dosing is largely routine care and correlates more with device use/ICU workflow than renal injury. Low likelihood of contributing independent predictive value in a sparse linear model.

Even in the closest window, heparin flush totals are not a meaningful renal biomarker and are dominated by direct measures like urine output and vasopressor burden. Best treated as a low-value proxy feature.

Total urine output is a direct functional kidney/perfusion marker, but the 0–12h period is confounded by intraoperative fluids/diuretics and transient post-bypass effects. Still potentially informative, yet less reliable than later urine output for Hour-60 creatinine.

Urine output over 13–24h captures early oliguria trajectory after initial post-op noise settles, providing a direct renal function/perfusion signal. Strong sparse-model candidate, though somewhat redundant with the more proximal 25–36h urine output.

Urine output at 25–36h is the most proximal direct marker of evolving AKI/renal hypoperfusion before the Hour-60 creatinine measurement. High unique predictive value and likely to survive sparse selection over earlier urine-output windows.

Total norepinephrine-equivalent dose reflects early shock/vasoplegia and risk of renal hypoperfusion, but immediate post-op vasopressor use is often transient and protocol-driven. Useful but less specific/proximal than later vasopressor burden and overlaps with vasopressin dosing.

Persisting vasopressor requirement in 13–24h indicates ongoing hemodynamic instability with renal perfusion risk after early transient effects. Strong predictor but partially redundant with 25–36h vasopressor burden and urine output.

Norepinephrine-equivalent dose at 25–36h captures sustained hemodynamic support closest to the outcome and is a strong precursor of renal hypoperfusion and AKI progression. High-priority sparse-model feature, complementary to urine output as a perfusion/illness-severity axis.
